title: SL(C)AMma: Simultaneous Localisation, (Calibration) and Mapping With a Magnetometer Array
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures, 1 table, python implementation available at https://github.com/Tedridge/SL-C-AMma, experimental data available at https://hendeby.gitlab-pages.liu.se/research/magdata/
summary: Indoor localisation techniques suffer from attenuated Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signals and from the accumulation of unbounded drift by integration of proprioceptive sensors. Magnetic field-based Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping (SLAM) reduces drift through loop closures by revisiting previously seen locations, but extended exploration of unseen areas remains challenging. Recently, magnetometer arrays have demonstrated significant benefits over single magnetometers, as they can directly estimate the odometry. However, inconsistencies between magnetometer measurements negatively affect odometry estimates and complicate loop closure detection. We propose two filtering algorithms: The first focuses on magnetic field-based SLAM using a magnetometer array (SLAMma). The second extends this to jointly estimate the magnetometer calibration parameters (SLCAMma). We demonstrate, using Monte Carlo simulations, that the calibration parameters can be accurately estimated when there is sufficient orientation excitation, and that magnetometers achieve inter-sensor measurement consistency regardless of the type of motion. Experimental validation on ten datasets confirms these results, and we demonstrate that in cases where single magnetometer SLAM fails, SLAMma and SLCAMma provide good trajectory estimates with, more than 80% drift reduction compared to integration of proprioceptive sensors.

AI.tldr: Proposes SLAMma and SLCAMma - two filtering algorithms for magnetic field-based SLAM using magnetometer arrays that reduce drift by 80+% compared to proprioceptive sensors alone.
AI.motivation: Indoor localization suffers from weak GNSS signals and drift accumulation from proprioceptive sensors. While magnetic field SLAM reduces drift through loop closures, exploring new areas remains challenging. Magnetometer arrays can estimate odometry directly but suffer from measurement inconsistencies affecting odometry and loop closure detection.
AI.method: Two filtering algorithms: 1) SLAMma for magnetic field-based SLAM using magnetometer arrays, and 2) SLCAMma that extends this to jointly estimate magnetometer calibration parameters. Uses Monte Carlo simulations and experimental validation on ten datasets.
AI.result: Calibration parameters can be accurately estimated with sufficient orientation excitation. Magnetometers achieve inter-sensor measurement consistency regardless of motion type. SLAMma and SLCAMma provide good trajectory estimates where single magnetometer SLAM fails, achieving >80% drift reduction compared to proprioceptive sensor integration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed magnetometer array-based SLAM algorithms effectively address measurement inconsistencies and calibration issues, significantly improving indoor localization accuracy by reducing drift through better odometry estimation and loop closure detection.

title: Radar Odometry Subject to High Tilt Dynamics of Subarctic Environments
summary: Rotating FMCW radar odometry methods often assume flat ground conditions. While this assumption is sufficient in many scenarios, including urban environments or flat mining setups, the highly dynamic terrain of subarctic environments poses a challenge to standard feature extraction and state estimation techniques. This paper benchmarks three existing radar odometry methods under demanding conditions, exhibiting up to 13° in pitch and 4° in roll difference between consecutive scans, with absolute pitch and roll reaching 30° and 8°, respectively. Furthermore, we propose a novel radar-inertial odometry method utilizing tilt-proximity submap search and a hard threshold for vertical displacement between scan points and the estimated axis of rotation. Experimental results demonstrate a state-of-the-art performance of our method on an urban baseline and a 0.3% improvement over the second-best comparative method on a 2-kilometer-long dynamic trajectory. Finally, we analyze the performance of the four evaluated methods on a complex radar sequence characterized by high lateral slip and a steep ditch traversal.

AI.tldr: Paper benchmarks radar odometry methods in challenging terrain, proposes novel radar-inertial odometry with tilt-proximity submap search, achieving SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: Existing rotating FMCW radar odometry methods assume flat ground, which fails in highly dynamic subarctic terrain with extreme pitch/roll angles. Need robust methods for challenging environments.
AI.method: Proposes novel radar-inertial odometry using tilt-proximity submap search and hard threshold for vertical displacement between scan points and estimated rotation axis. Benchmarks three existing methods against proposed approach.
AI.result: Proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on urban baseline and 0.3% improvement over second-best method on 2km dynamic trajectory. Also analyzes performance on complex sequence with high lateral slip and steep ditch traversal.
AI.conclusion: The proposed radar-inertial odometry with tilt-proximity submap search effectively handles challenging terrain conditions, outperforming existing methods in dynamic subarctic environments with extreme pitch/roll variations.

title: Efficient Reinforcement Learning using Linear Koopman Dynamics for Nonlinear Robotic Systems
summary: This paper presents a model-based reinforcement learning (RL) framework for optimal closed-loop control of nonlinear robotic systems. The proposed approach learns linear lifted dynamics through Koopman operator theory and integrates the resulting model into an actor-critic architecture for policy optimization, where the policy represents a parameterized closed-loop controller. To reduce computational cost and mitigate model rollout errors, policy gradients are estimated using one-step predictions of the learned dynamics rather than multi-step propagation. This leads to an online mini-batch policy gradient framework that enables policy improvement from streamed interaction data. The proposed framework is evaluated on several simulated nonlinear control benchmarks and two real-world hardware platforms, including a Kinova Gen3 robotic arm and a Unitree Go1 quadruped. Experimental results demonstrate improved sample efficiency over model-free RL baselines, superior control performance relative to model-based RL baselines, and control performance comparable to classical model-based methods that rely on exact system dynamics.

AI.tldr: Model-based RL framework using Koopman operator theory for learning linear lifted dynamics, integrated with actor-critic architecture for policy optimization with one-step predictions to reduce computational cost.
AI.motivation: To develop a sample-efficient model-based RL approach for optimal closed-loop control of nonlinear robotic systems that can handle complex dynamics while reducing computational costs compared to traditional model-based methods.
AI.method: Learns linear lifted dynamics through Koopman operator theory, integrates the model into actor-critic architecture, uses one-step predictions for policy gradient estimation (instead of multi-step propagation), and implements online mini-batch policy gradient framework for streaming data.
AI.result: Improved sample efficiency over model-free RL baselines, superior control performance relative to model-based RL baselines, and control performance comparable to classical model-based methods that rely on exact system dynamics. Tested on simulated benchmarks and real-world hardware (Kinova Gen3 arm and Unitree Go1 quadruped).
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully combines Koopman operator theory with actor-critic RL to achieve efficient and effective control of nonlinear robotic systems, demonstrating practical applicability on real hardware with competitive performance.

title: Strain in Sound: Soft Corrugated Tube for Local Strain Sensing with Acoustic Resonance
comment: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft). IEEE, 2025
summary: We present a soft corrugated tube sensor designed to estimate strain in each half segment. When air flows through the tube, the internal corrugated cavities induce pressure oscillations that excite the tube's standing wave resonance mode, generating an acoustic tone. Stretching the tube affects both the resonance mode frequency, due to changes in overall length, and the frequency-flow speed relationship, due to variations in cavity width, which is particularly useful for local strain estimation. By sweeping flow rates in a controlled manner, we collected resonance frequency data across flow speeds under various local stretch conditions, enabling a machine learning algorithm (gradient boosting regressor) to estimate segmental strain with high accuracy. The dual-period tube design (3.1 mm and 4.18 mm corrugation periods) achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.8 mm, while the single-period tube (3.1 mm) provided a satisfactory MAE of 1 mm. Testing on a mannequin finger demonstrated the sensor's capability to differentiate multi-joint configurations, showing its potential for estimating non-uniform deformations in soft bodies.

AI.tldr: Soft corrugated tube sensor uses airflow-induced acoustic resonance to estimate local strain via machine learning, achieving 0.8-1mm accuracy and demonstrating finger joint sensing capability.
AI.motivation: Need for soft sensors that can estimate non-uniform deformations and local strain in soft bodies, particularly for applications like robotic fingers and wearable devices that require precise deformation sensing.
AI.method: Corrugated tube design creates standing wave resonance when air flows through; stretching changes both resonance frequency and frequency-flow relationship. Machine learning (gradient boosting regressor) trained on resonance frequency data across flow speeds under various stretch conditions.
AI.result: Dual-period tube (3.1mm & 4.18mm periods) achieved MAE of 0.8mm; single-period tube (3.1mm) achieved MAE of 1mm. Successfully differentiated multi-joint configurations on mannequin finger.
AI.conclusion: The soft corrugated tube sensor enables accurate local strain estimation in soft bodies using acoustic resonance and machine learning, with potential applications in soft robotics and wearable sensing.

title: Rabies diagnosis in low-data settings: A comparative study on the impact of data augmentation and transfer learning
comment: This work has been accepted for publication in ICMI IEEE Conference (04/2026)
summary: Rabies remains a major public health concern across many African and Asian countries, where accurate diagnosis is critical for effective epidemiological surveillance. The gold standard diagnostic methods rely heavily on fluorescence microscopy, necessitating skilled laboratory personnel for the accurate interpretation of results. Such expertise is often scarce, particularly in regions with low annual sample volumes. This paper presents an automated, AI-driven diagnostic system designed to address these challenges. We developed a robust pipeline utilizing fluorescent image analysis through transfer learning with four deep learning architectures: EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB2, VGG16, and Vision Transformer (ViTB16). Three distinct data augmentation strategies were evaluated to enhance model generalization on a dataset of 155 microscopic images (123 positive and 32 negative). Our results demonstrate that TrivialAugmentWide was the most effective augmentation technique, as it preserved critical fluorescent patterns while improving model robustness. The EfficientNetB0 model, utilizing Geometric & Color augmentation and selected through stratified 3fold cross-validation, achieved optimal classification performance on cropped images. Despite constraints posed by class imbalance and a limited dataset size, this work confirms the viability of deep learning for automating rabies diagnosis. The proposed method enables fast and reliable detection with significant potential for further optimization. An online tool was deployed to facilitate practical access, establishing a framework for future medical imaging applications. This research underscores the potential of optimized deep learning models to transform rabies diagnostics and improve public health outcomes.

AI.tldr: AI-driven automated rabies diagnosis system using deep learning on fluorescent microscopy images achieves reliable detection with potential for public health impact.
AI.motivation: Rabies diagnosis in resource-limited regions faces challenges due to reliance on skilled personnel for fluorescence microscopy interpretation, which is scarce in areas with low sample volumes.
AI.method: Developed automated pipeline using transfer learning with four deep learning architectures (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB2, VGG16, Vision Transformer) on 155 microscopic images. Evaluated three data augmentation strategies, with TrivialAugmentWide proving most effective. Used stratified 3-fold cross-validation for model selection.
AI.result: EfficientNetB0 with Geometric & Color augmentation achieved optimal classification performance on cropped images. Despite class imbalance and limited dataset, the system demonstrated viability for automated rabies diagnosis. An online tool was deployed for practical access.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning shows strong potential for transforming rabies diagnostics in resource-limited settings, enabling fast and reliable detection with significant public health impact. The framework establishes foundation for future medical imaging applications.

title: WorkflowGen:an adaptive workflow generation mechanism driven by trajectory experience
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents often suffer from high reasoning overhead, excessive token consumption, unstable execution, and inability to reuse past experiences in complex tasks like business queries, tool use, and workflow orchestration. Traditional methods generate workflows from scratch for every query, leading to high cost, slow response, and poor robustness. We propose WorkflowGen, an adaptive, trajectory experience-driven framework for automatic workflow generation that reduces token usage and improves efficiency and success rate. Early in execution, WorkflowGen captures full trajectories and extracts reusable knowledge at both node and workflow levels, including error fingerprints, optimal tool mappings, parameter schemas, execution paths, and exception-avoidance strategies. It then employs a closed-loop mechanism that performs lightweight generation only on variable nodes via trajectory rewriting, experience updating, and template induction. A three-tier adaptive routing strategy dynamically selects among direct reuse, rewriting-based generation, and full initialization based on semantic similarity to historical queries. Without large annotated datasets, we qualitatively compare WorkflowGen against real-time planning, static single trajectory, and basic in-context learning baselines. Our method reduces token consumption by over 40 percent compared to real-time planning, improves success rate by 20 percent on medium-similarity queries through proactive error avoidance and adaptive fallback, and enhances deployability via modular, traceable experiences and cross-scenario adaptability. WorkflowGen achieves a practical balance of efficiency, robustness, and interpretability, addressing key limitations of existing approaches.

AI.tldr: WorkflowGen is an adaptive framework that reuses past execution experiences to generate workflows efficiently, reducing token usage by 40% and improving success rates by 20% compared to traditional real-time planning approaches.
AI.motivation: LLM agents face high reasoning overhead, excessive token consumption, unstable execution, and inability to reuse past experiences in complex tasks like business queries and workflow orchestration. Traditional methods generate workflows from scratch for every query, leading to high costs, slow responses, and poor robustness.
AI.method: WorkflowGen captures full execution trajectories and extracts reusable knowledge at node and workflow levels (error fingerprints, tool mappings, parameter schemas, execution paths, exception strategies). It uses a closed-loop mechanism with lightweight generation via trajectory rewriting, experience updating, and template induction. A three-tier adaptive routing strategy dynamically selects among direct reuse, rewriting-based generation, and full initialization based on semantic similarity to historical queries.
AI.result: Reduces token consumption by over 40% compared to real-time planning, improves success rate by 20% on medium-similarity queries through proactive error avoidance and adaptive fallback, and enhances deployability via modular, traceable experiences and cross-scenario adaptability.
AI.conclusion: WorkflowGen achieves a practical balance of efficiency, robustness, and interpretability, addressing key limitations of existing approaches by enabling experience reuse, reducing computational overhead, and improving workflow generation performance without requiring large annotated datasets.

title: The Tool-Overuse Illusion: Why Does LLM Prefer External Tools over Internal Knowledge?
summary: Equipping LLMs with external tools effectively addresses internal reasoning limitations. However, it introduces a critical yet under-explored phenomenon: tool overuse, the unnecessary tool-use during reasoning. In this paper, we first reveal this phenomenon is pervasive across diverse LLMs. We then experimentally elucidate its underlying mechanisms through two key lenses: (1) First, by analyzing tool-use behavior across different internal knowledge availability regions, we identify a \textit{knowledge epistemic illusion}: models misjudge internal knowledge boundaries and fail to accurately perceive their actual knowledge availability. To mitigate this, we propose a knowledge-aware epistemic boundary alignment strategy based on direct preference optimization, which reduces tool usage in by 82.8\% while yielding an accuracy improvement. (2) Second, we establish a causal link between reward structures and tool-use behavior by visualizing the tool-augmented training process. It reveals that \textit{outcome-only rewards} inadvertently encourage tool overuse by rewarding only final correctness, regardless of tool efficiency. To verify this, we balance reward signals during training rather than relying on outcome-only rewards, cutting unnecessary tool calls by 66.7\% (7B) and 60.7\% (32B) without sacrificing accuracy. Finally, we provide theoretical justification in this two lenses to understand tool overuse.

AI.tldr: LLMs often overuse external tools unnecessarily during reasoning due to knowledge misjudgment and flawed reward structures. The paper proposes knowledge-aware alignment and balanced reward training to reduce tool overuse by 60-80% while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: While equipping LLMs with external tools addresses reasoning limitations, it introduces tool overuse - unnecessary tool usage during reasoning. This phenomenon is pervasive but under-explored, and understanding its mechanisms is crucial for efficient tool-augmented LLMs.
AI.method: Two-pronged approach: (1) Knowledge-aware epistemic boundary alignment using direct preference optimization to address models' misjudgment of internal knowledge boundaries. (2) Visualizing tool-augmented training to establish causal link between reward structures and tool-use behavior, then balancing reward signals rather than using outcome-only rewards.
AI.result: Knowledge-aware alignment reduces tool usage by 82.8% with accuracy improvement. Balanced reward training cuts unnecessary tool calls by 66.7% (7B model) and 60.7% (32B model) without sacrificing accuracy. Both approaches effectively mitigate tool overuse.
AI.conclusion: Tool overuse stems from two key mechanisms: knowledge epistemic illusion and outcome-only reward structures. Addressing these through knowledge-aware alignment and balanced reward training significantly reduces unnecessary tool usage while maintaining or improving accuracy, providing theoretical justification for understanding tool overuse.

title: Maximum Likelihood Reconstruction for Multi-Look Digital Holography with Markov-Modeled Speckle Correlation
summary: Multi-look acquisition is a widely used strategy for reducing speckle noise in coherent imaging systems such as digital holography. By acquiring multiple measurements, speckle can be suppressed through averaging or joint reconstruction, typically under the assumption that speckle realizations across looks are statistically independent. In practice, however, hardware constraints limit measurement diversity, leading to inter-look correlation that degrades the performance of conventional methods. In this work, we study the reconstruction of speckle-free reflectivity from complex-valued multi-look measurements in the presence of correlated speckle. We model the inter-look dependence using a first-order Markov process and derive the corresponding likelihood under a first-order Markov approximation, resulting in a constrained maximum likelihood estimation problem. To solve this problem, we develop an efficient projected gradient descent framework that combines gradient-based updates with implicit regularization via deep image priors, and leverages Monte Carlo approximation and matrix-free operators for scalable computation. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach remains robust under strong inter-look correlation, achieving performance close to the ideal independent-look scenario and consistently outperforming methods that ignore such dependencies. These results highlight the importance of explicitly modeling inter-look correlation and provide a practical framework for multi-look holographic reconstruction under realistic acquisition conditions. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Computational-Imaging-RU/MLE-Holography-Markov.

AI.tldr: A method for multi-look holographic reconstruction that models correlated speckle noise using a Markov process and solves it with projected gradient descent and deep image priors.
AI.motivation: Conventional multi-look methods assume statistically independent speckle across looks, but hardware constraints create inter-look correlation that degrades reconstruction performance.
AI.method: Models inter-look dependence as first-order Markov process, formulates constrained maximum likelihood estimation, and solves with projected gradient descent combining gradient updates, deep image priors, Monte Carlo approximation, and matrix-free operators.
AI.result: The approach remains robust under strong inter-look correlation, achieving performance close to ideal independent-look scenario and consistently outperforming methods ignoring such dependencies.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling inter-look correlation is crucial for multi-look holographic reconstruction under realistic acquisition conditions, and the proposed framework provides a practical solution.

title: New Insights into Channel vs Subspace Codes for Large-Scale Beamspace MIMO Channel Sensing
comment: Submitted to IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Information Theory special issue "Theoretical Foundations for 6G-and-Beyond Wireless Networks'' on Oct 1 2025; received recommendation of major revision and subsequently retracted due to short review cycle of the journal
summary: This paper provides novel insights into channel and subspace codes in nonadaptive channel sensing with a single RF chain. Observing that this problem naturally maps to a noncoherent decoding problem, we show that the sensing performance of the maximum likelihood (ML) angle estimator, which does not require knowledge of the typically unknown channel coefficient, is governed by two key terms: the minimum subspace distance and beam gain of the used beamformers. We derive an exact expression for the subspace distance of binary linear channel codes mapped to BPSK, which illuminates the relationship between subspace and Hamming distance, used to design subspace and channel codes, respectively. Our result also reveals why good Hamming distance alone is insufficient for sensing, and shows that well-known families of channel codes such as Reed-Muller codes, yield zero subspace distance and thereby poor sensing performance when used naively without proper codebook pruning. Finally, we introduce so-called beamspace subspace codes based on sparse antenna selection patterns (Golomb rulers), which we show provide near-optimal subspace distance. We demonstrate that this property of judiciously designed sparse arrays can be leveraged together with beamforming gain via convolutional beamspaces, enabling hardware- and sample-efficient channel sensing with theoretical guarantees in large-scale multiantenna communications.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes channel/subspace codes for nonadaptive sensing with single RF chain, showing ML angle estimator performance depends on minimum subspace distance and beam gain, reveals why good Hamming distance alone is insufficient, and introduces beamspace subspace codes using Golomb rulers for near-optimal performance.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of efficient channel sensing in large-scale multiantenna communications with hardware constraints (single RF chain), where traditional channel codes with good Hamming distance may perform poorly for sensing applications.
AI.method: 1) Maps nonadaptive channel sensing to noncoherent decoding problem; 2) Analyzes ML angle estimator showing dependence on subspace distance and beam gain; 3) Derives exact expression for subspace distance of binary linear codes mapped to BPSK; 4) Introduces beamspace subspace codes based on Golomb rulers (sparse antenna selection patterns); 5) Combines with convolutional beamspaces for beamforming gain.
AI.result: 1) Shows that good Hamming distance alone is insufficient for sensing; 2) Reveals that Reed-Muller codes yield zero subspace distance when used naively; 3) Demonstrates Golomb ruler-based sparse arrays provide near-optimal subspace distance; 4) Enables hardware- and sample-efficient channel sensing with theoretical guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Properly designed sparse antenna selection patterns (Golomb rulers) combined with convolutional beamspaces enable efficient channel sensing with single RF chain, overcoming limitations of traditional channel codes and providing theoretical performance guarantees for large-scale MIMO systems.

title: Anchor-Aided Multi-User Semantic Communication with Adaptive Decoders
summary: Semantic communication (SemCom) is accelerating its momentum to catch up with the massive increase in users' demands in both quantity and quality, with the assistance of advanced deep learning (DL) techniques. Specifically, SemCom can actively embed the semantic meaning of the data into the transmission process, while eliminating statistical redundancy to preserve bandwidth resources for other users. Therefore, the transmitter encodes the message in the most concise way, while the receiver tries to interpret the message with the DL model and its knowledge of the transmitter's intended meaning. Most existing works only consider one transmitter and one receiver, which limits their ability to address the diversity in users' models and capabilities. Therefore, in this paper, we propose a multi-user semantic communication system where each user is equipped with a distinct DL-based joint source-channel decoder architecture, reflecting the diversity in computing capacity. The challenging issue with the proposed system is the catastrophic forgetting property of neural networks, where the DL-based encoder fails to encode the data for the previous user when being trained with a new user. To address this, we propose an anchor decoder with an architecture that is symmetric to the encoder. The symmetric decoder has the same computational capacity as the encoder, providing feedback that aligns with the encoder's extraction capabilities and enhances optimization efficiency. The parameters of the optimized encoder are then frozen and used to train decoders for various users, aligning them with the encoder outputs. Finally, we conduct a series of simulation experiments to validate the proposed framework against other benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multi-user semantic communication system with distinct DL-based decoders for each user, addressing catastrophic forgetting via symmetric anchor decoder architecture.
AI.motivation: Existing semantic communication systems typically consider only one transmitter and one receiver, which fails to address the diversity in users' models and computing capabilities. There's a need for multi-user systems that can handle different users with varying DL-based decoder architectures.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-user semantic communication system where each user has a distinct DL-based joint source-channel decoder. To address catastrophic forgetting in the encoder when training with new users, introduces an anchor decoder with symmetric architecture to the encoder. The symmetric decoder provides feedback aligned with encoder's capabilities, then freezes optimized encoder parameters to train various user decoders.
AI.result: Conducted simulation experiments to validate the proposed framework against other benchmarks, demonstrating effectiveness of the symmetric anchor decoder approach in preventing catastrophic forgetting in multi-user semantic communication systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-user semantic communication system with symmetric anchor decoder architecture successfully addresses catastrophic forgetting issues, enabling efficient training of diverse user decoders while maintaining encoder performance across different users.

title: JoyAI-RA 0.1: A Foundation Model for Robotic Autonomy
summary: Robotic autonomy in open-world environments is fundamentally limited by insufficient data diversity and poor cross-embodiment generalization. Existing robotic datasets are often limited in scale and task coverage, while relatively large differences across robot embodiments impede effective behavior knowledge transfer. To address these challenges, we propose JoyAI-RA, a vision-language-action (VLA) embodied foundation model tailored for generalizable robotic manipulation. JoyAI-RA presents a multi-source multi-level pretraining framework that integrates web data, large-scale egocentric human manipulation videos, simulation-generated trajectories, and real-robot data. Through training on heterogeneous multi-source data with explicit action-space unification, JoyAI-RA effectively bridges embodiment gaps, particularly between human manipulation and robotic control, thereby enhancing cross-embodiment behavior learning. JoyAI-RA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both simulation and real-world benchmarks, especially on diverse tasks with generalization demands.

AI.tldr: JoyAI-RA is a vision-language-action foundation model for robotic manipulation that addresses data diversity and cross-embodiment generalization challenges through multi-source pretraining.
AI.motivation: Robotic autonomy is limited by insufficient data diversity and poor cross-embodiment generalization. Existing datasets are limited in scale and task coverage, and differences across robot embodiments hinder behavior knowledge transfer.
AI.method: A multi-source multi-level pretraining framework integrating web data, large-scale egocentric human manipulation videos, simulation-generated trajectories, and real-robot data with explicit action-space unification to bridge embodiment gaps.
AI.result: JoyAI-RA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both simulation and real-world benchmarks, especially on diverse tasks with generalization demands.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses cross-embodiment generalization challenges and enhances robotic manipulation capabilities through heterogeneous multi-source data integration.

title: TactileEval: A Step Towards Automated Fine-Grained Evaluation and Editing of Tactile Graphics
comment: Code, data, and models are available at https://TactileEval.github.io/
summary: Tactile graphics require careful expert validation before reaching blind and visually impaired (BVI) learners, yet existing datasets provide only coarse holistic quality ratings that offer no actionable repair signal. We present TactileEval, a three-stage pipeline that takes a first step toward automating this process. Drawing on expert free-text comments from the TactileNet dataset, we establish a five-category quality taxonomy; encompassing view angle, part completeness, background clutter, texture separation, and line quality aligned with BANA standards. We subsequently gathered 14,095 structured annotations via Amazon Mechanical Turk, spanning 66 object classes organized into six distinct families. A reproducible ViT-L/14 feature probe trained on this data achieves 85.70% overall test accuracy across 30 different tasks, with consistent difficulty ordering suggesting the taxonomy suggesting the taxonomy captures meaningful perceptual structure. Building on these evaluations, we present a ViT-guided automated editing pipeline that routes classifier scores through family-specific prompt templates to produce targeted corrections via gpt-image-1 image editing. Code, data, and models are available at https://TactileEval.github.io/

AI.tldr: TactileEval: A pipeline for automated quality assessment and editing of tactile graphics using a 5-category taxonomy and ViT-based classification with GPT-guided editing.
AI.motivation: Current tactile graphics validation requires expert review and existing datasets only provide coarse quality ratings without actionable repair guidance, creating a bottleneck for blind and visually impaired learners.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: 1) Establish 5-category quality taxonomy from expert comments, 2) Collect 14,095 structured annotations across 66 object classes, 3) Train ViT-L/14 feature probe for classification and develop automated editing pipeline using classifier scores with family-specific prompt templates for GPT-guided image editing.
AI.result: ViT-L/14 model achieves 85.70% overall test accuracy across 30 tasks, with consistent difficulty ordering validating taxonomy structure. Automated editing pipeline successfully produces targeted corrections.
AI.conclusion: TactileEval represents a significant step toward automating tactile graphics validation and repair, providing actionable quality assessment and automated editing capabilities to improve accessibility for BVI learners.

title: Transparent Screening for LLM Inference and Training Impacts
summary: This paper presents a transparent screening framework for estimating inference and training impacts of current large language models under limited observability. The framework converts natural-language application descriptions into bounded environmental estimates and supports a comparative online observatory of current market models. Rather than claiming direct measurement for opaque proprietary services, it provides an auditable, source-linked proxy methodology designed to improve comparability, transparency, and reproducibility.

AI.tldr: A transparent framework for estimating environmental impacts of LLMs using natural-language descriptions as proxies when direct measurement isn't possible.
AI.motivation: Current large language models are often opaque proprietary services with limited observability, making it difficult to assess their environmental impacts during inference and training. There's a need for transparent, comparable, and reproducible methods to estimate these impacts.
AI.method: A screening framework that converts natural-language application descriptions into bounded environmental estimates. It provides an auditable, source-linked proxy methodology instead of direct measurement, supporting comparative analysis of current market models through an online observatory.
AI.result: The framework enables estimation of inference and training impacts under limited observability conditions, improving comparability, transparency, and reproducibility of environmental assessments for LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed transparent screening framework offers a practical solution for environmental impact assessment of opaque LLM services, providing auditable proxy estimates that enhance accountability and enable better decision-making about model deployment.

title: AI to Learn 2.0: A Deliverable-Oriented Governance Framework and Maturity Rubric for Opaque AI in Learning-Intensive Domains
summary: Generative AI is entering research, education, and professional work faster than current governance frameworks can specify how AI-assisted outputs should be judged in learning-intensive settings. The central problem is proxy failure: a polished artifact can be useful while no longer serving as credible evidence of the human understanding, judgment, or transfer ability that the work is supposed to cultivate or certify. This paper proposes AI to Learn 2.0, a deliverable-oriented governance framework for AI-assisted work. Rather than claiming element-wise novelty, it reorganizes adjacent ideas around the final deliverable package, distinguishes artifact residual from capability residual, and operationalizes the result through a five-part package, a seven-dimension maturity rubric, gate thresholds on critical dimensions, and a companion capability-evidence ladder. AI to Learn 2.0 allows opaque AI during exploration, drafting, hypothesis generation, and workflow design, but requires that the released deliverable be usable, auditable, transferable, and justifiable without the original large language model or cloud API. In learning-intensive contexts, it additionally requires context-appropriate human-attributable evidence of explanation or transfer. Worked scoring across contrastive cases, including coursework substitution, a symbolic-regression governance contrast, teacher-audited national-exam practice forms, and a self-hosted lecture-to-quiz pipeline with deterministic quality control, shows how the framework separates polished substitution workflows from bounded, auditable, and handoff-ready AI-assisted workflows. AI to Learn 2.0 is proposed as a governance instrument for structured third-party review where capability preservation, accountability, and validity boundaries matter.

AI.tldr: AI to Learn 2.0 is a governance framework that addresses proxy failure in AI-assisted work by requiring deliverables to be usable, auditable, transferable, and justifiable without the original AI system, with additional human-attributable evidence requirements in learning contexts.
AI.motivation: Generative AI is being adopted faster than governance frameworks can specify how to judge AI-assisted outputs in learning-intensive settings. The central problem is "proxy failure" - polished artifacts may be useful but no longer serve as credible evidence of human understanding, judgment, or transfer ability that the work is supposed to cultivate or certify.
AI.method: Proposes AI to Learn 2.0 framework that reorganizes adjacent ideas around final deliverables, distinguishes artifact residual from capability residual, and operationalizes through: 1) five-part package, 2) seven-dimension maturity rubric, 3) gate thresholds on critical dimensions, and 4) companion capability-evidence ladder. Allows opaque AI during exploration/drafting but requires deliverables to be usable, auditable, transferable, and justifiable without original LLM/API.
AI.result: Worked scoring across contrastive cases shows how the framework separates polished substitution workflows from bounded, auditable, handoff-ready AI-assisted workflows. Cases include coursework substitution, symbolic-regression governance contrast, teacher-audited national-exam practice forms, and self-hosted lecture-to-quiz pipeline with deterministic quality control.
AI.conclusion: AI to Learn 2.0 is proposed as a governance instrument for structured third-party review where capability preservation, accountability, and validity boundaries matter. It provides a framework to ensure AI-assisted work maintains credible evidence of human learning and capability development.

title: CKM Beyond Channel Gain: Spatial Correlation Map Construction with Deep Learning
summary: Channel knowledge map (CKM) is a promising technique to achieve environment-aware wireless communication and sensing. Constructing the complete CKM based on channel knowledge observations at sparse locations is a fundamental problem for CKM-enabled wireless networks. However, most existing works on CKM construction only consider the special type of CKM, i.e., the channel gain map (CGM), which only records the channel gain value for each location. In this paper, we consider the channel spatial correlation map (SCM) construction, which signifies the location-specific spatial correlation matrix for multi-antenna systems. Unlike CGM construction, constructing SCM poses significant challenges due to its extremely high-dimensional structure. To address this issue, we first decompose the high-dimensional SCM into lower-dimensional path gain map (PGM) and path angle map (PAM). Then we propose a deep learning model termed E-SRResNet for constructing high-quality SCM from sparse samples, which incorporates multi-head attention (MHA) mechanisms and multi-scale feature fusion (MSFF) to accurately model both local and global spatial relationships of channel parameters and complex nonlinear mappings. Furthermore, we preprocess the dataset to provide priors including line-of-sight (LoS) map, binary building map and base station (BS) map for the model to reconstruct SCM more accurately. Simulations conducted on the CKMImageNet dataset demonstrate that the proposed E-SRResNet achieves significant performance improvements over baseline methods. Moreover, the cosine similarity between the constructed SCM and the ground truth exceeds 0.8 in most regions, validating the effectiveness of the proposed construction method.

AI.tldr: Proposes E-SRResNet, a deep learning model for constructing high-dimensional channel spatial correlation maps from sparse samples using multi-head attention and multi-scale feature fusion.
AI.motivation: Existing CKM construction methods focus only on simple channel gain maps, but spatial correlation maps are crucial for multi-antenna systems and pose significant challenges due to their high-dimensional structure.
AI.method: Decomposes SCM into lower-dimensional path gain and angle maps, then uses E-SRResNet with MHA and MSFF to model spatial relationships. Incorporates prior knowledge (LoS maps, building maps, BS maps) for better reconstruction.
AI.result: E-SRResNet achieves significant performance improvements over baselines on CKMImageNet dataset, with cosine similarity exceeding 0.8 in most regions between constructed SCM and ground truth.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively constructs high-quality spatial correlation maps from sparse samples, enabling better environment-aware wireless communication and sensing for multi-antenna systems.

title: A Hybrid Gauss Markov LSTM Mobility Model for Indoor OWC
summary: Optical wireless communication (OWC) has emerged as a promising candidate for future high-capacity indoor wireless networks, driven by its large unregulated spectrum, high spatial reuse, and ability to support multi-gigabit data rates. However, OWC systems are highly sensitive to user mobility, as link performance depends strongly on the spatial alignment between transmitter and receiver. Accurate modelling of user position and device orientation is therefore essential for reliable channel estimation and system evaluation. To that effect, this paper proposes a hybrid Gauss--Markov and long short-term memory (GM--LSTM) mobility model for indoor OWC environments. The Gauss--Markov component captures the temporal correlation of user motion, while the LSTM learns residual behaviour to model non-linear movement patterns and orientation dynamics. The proposed model jointly predicts user position and device orientation, enabling improved representation of mobility in OWC channels. Performance is evaluated using prediction accuracy and per-user data rate evolution. Results show that the proposed hybrid GM--LSTM model outperforms conventional Random Waypoint and Gauss--Markov models, providing more accurate mobility prediction and more stable communication performance in dynamic indoor environments.

AI.tldr: A hybrid Gauss-Markov and LSTM mobility model for indoor optical wireless communication that jointly predicts user position and device orientation to improve channel estimation and system performance.
AI.motivation: Optical wireless communication (OWC) systems are highly sensitive to user mobility since link performance depends on spatial alignment between transmitter and receiver. Accurate modeling of user position and device orientation is essential for reliable channel estimation and system evaluation in indoor OWC environments.
AI.method: Proposes a hybrid Gauss-Markov and long short-term memory (GM-LSTM) mobility model. The Gauss-Markov component captures temporal correlation of user motion, while the LSTM learns residual behavior to model non-linear movement patterns and orientation dynamics. The model jointly predicts user position and device orientation.
AI.result: The proposed hybrid GM-LSTM model outperforms conventional Random Waypoint and Gauss-Markov models. It provides more accurate mobility prediction and more stable communication performance in dynamic indoor environments, as evaluated using prediction accuracy and per-user data rate evolution metrics.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid GM-LSTM mobility model enables improved representation of mobility in OWC channels, addressing the sensitivity of OWC systems to user mobility and spatial alignment issues, leading to better system performance in dynamic indoor environments.

title: Finite-Length Empirical Comparison of Polar, PAC, and Invertible-Extractor Secrecy Codes over the Wiretap BSC
summary: We compare three secrecy-coding schemes for the degraded wiretap binary symmetric channel (BSC) in the finite-blocklength regime: (i) polar wiretap coset codes, (ii) PAC codes used as wiretap coset codes, and (iii) the invertible-extractor (IE) framework of Bellare-Tessaro. Our comparison is empirical and uses a common semantic-secrecy metric (distinguishing advantage). For polar coset codes, we compute Eve's polarized bit-channel capacities (via the Tal-Vardy construction) to obtain explicit finite-length upper bounds on mutual-information leakage, yielding strong secrecy bounds. For PAC coset codes, we prove that Eve's synthesized bit-channels are equivalent to those of polar codes (up to a permutation), so the same leakage bounds apply; we then convert these strong-secrecy bounds into semantic-secrecy guarantees for symmetric wiretap channels. For the IE scheme, we use the closed-form semantic-secrecy bounds given in the reference work. Finally, we report finite-length results that jointly characterize (a) semantic-secrecy guarantees against Eve and (b) frame-error-rate performance at Bob, illustrating that PAC codes can significantly improve reliability without changing the secrecy bounds inherited from polar coding. Moreover, under the finite-length bounds considered in this work, polar/PAC secrecy codes provide tighter security guarantees than the invertible-extractor framework.

AI.tldr: Comparison of three wiretap secrecy-coding schemes (polar coset codes, PAC coset codes, invertible-extractor) for degraded BSC in finite-blocklength regime, showing PAC codes improve reliability without compromising security and outperform IE framework.
AI.motivation: To empirically compare different secrecy-coding schemes for wiretap channels in practical finite-blocklength scenarios, moving beyond asymptotic analysis to understand real-world performance trade-offs between security and reliability.
AI.method: 1) For polar coset codes: compute Eve's polarized bit-channel capacities via Tal-Vardy construction to get finite-length leakage bounds. 2) For PAC coset codes: prove equivalence to polar codes' bit-channels (up to permutation), then convert strong-secrecy to semantic-secrecy. 3) For IE scheme: use existing closed-form bounds. Compare all using common semantic-secrecy metric (distinguishing advantage).
AI.result: PAC codes significantly improve reliability (frame-error-rate at Bob) without changing the secrecy bounds inherited from polar coding. Polar/PAC codes provide tighter security guarantees than the invertible-extractor framework under the considered finite-length bounds.
AI.conclusion: PAC coset codes offer a practical advantage by enhancing reliability while maintaining strong security properties, making them superior to both polar coset codes and the invertible-extractor framework for finite-blocklength wiretap channel applications.

title: Toward Safe Autonomous Robotic Endovascular Interventions using World Models
comment: This manuscript is a preprint and has been submitted to the IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) 2026
summary: Autonomous mechanical thrombectomy (MT) presents substantial challenges due to highly variable vascular geometries and the requirements for accurate, real-time control. While reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for the automation of endovascular navigation, existing approaches often show limited robustness when faced with diverse patient anatomies or extended navigation horizons. In this work, we investigate a world-model-based framework for autonomous endovascular navigation built on TD-MPC2, a model-based RL method that integrates planning and learned dynamics. We evaluate a TD-MPC2 agent trained on multiple navigation tasks across hold out patient-specific vasculatures and benchmark its performance against the state-of-the-art Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm agent. Both approaches are further validated in vitro using patient-specific vascular phantoms under fluoroscopic guidance. In simulation, TD-MPC2 demonstrates a significantly higher mean success rate than SAC (58% vs. 36%, p < 0.001), and mean tip contact forces of 0.15 N, well below the proposed 1.5 N vessel rupture threshold. Mean success rates for TD-MPC2 (68%) were comparable to SAC (60%) in vitro, but TD-MPC2 achieved superior path ratios (p = 0.017) at the cost of longer procedure times (p < 0.001). Together, these results provide the first demonstration of autonomous MT navigation validated across both hold out in silico data and fluoroscopy-guided in vitro experiments, highlighting the promise of world models for safe and generalizable AI-assisted endovascular interventions.

AI.tldr: TD-MPC2 model-based RL outperforms SAC for autonomous mechanical thrombectomy navigation, showing higher success rates in simulation and better path efficiency in vitro.
AI.motivation: Autonomous mechanical thrombectomy faces challenges due to variable vascular geometries and real-time control requirements. Existing RL approaches lack robustness across diverse patient anatomies and long navigation horizons.
AI.method: World-model-based framework using TD-MPC2 (model-based RL with planning and learned dynamics), trained on multiple navigation tasks and evaluated across hold-out patient-specific vasculatures. Compared against state-of-the-art SAC algorithm, with both validated in vitro using patient-specific vascular phantoms under fluoroscopic guidance.
AI.result: In simulation: TD-MPC2 achieved 58% success rate vs SAC's 36% (p<0.001), with mean tip contact forces of 0.15N (below 1.5N rupture threshold). In vitro: TD-MPC2 had 68% success rate (comparable to SAC's 60%) but superior path ratios (p=0.017) though with longer procedure times (p<0.001).
AI.conclusion: First demonstration of autonomous MT navigation validated across both in silico and in vitro experiments, highlighting the promise of world models for safe and generalizable AI-assisted endovascular interventions.

title: KD-Judge: A Knowledge-Driven Automated Judge Framework for Functional Fitness Movements on Edge Devices
summary: Functional fitness movements are widely used in training, competition, and health-oriented exercise programs, yet consistently enforcing repetition (rep) standards remains challenging due to subjective human judgment, time constraints, and evolving rules. Existing AI-based approaches mainly rely on learned scoring or reference-based comparisons and lack explicit rule-based, limiting transparency and deterministic rep-level validation. To address these limitations, we propose KD-Judge, a novel knowledge-driven automated judging framework for functional fitness movements. It converts unstructured rulebook standards into executable, machine-readable representations using an LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation and chain-of-thought rule-structuring pipeline. The structured rules are then incorporated by a deterministic rule-based judging system with pose-guided kinematic reasoning to assess rep validity and temporal boundaries. To improve efficiency on edge devices, including a high-performance desktop and the resource-constrained Jetson AGX Xavier, we introduce a dual strategy caching mechanism that can be selectively applied to reduce redundant and unnecessary computation. Experiments demonstrate reliable rule-structuring performance and accurate rep-level assessment, with judgment evaluation conducted on the CFRep dataset, achieving faster-than-real-time execution (real-time factor (RTF) < 1). When the proposed caching strategy is enabled, the system achieves up to 3.36x and 15.91x speedups on resource-constrained edge device compared to the non-caching baseline for pre-recorded and live-streaming scenarios, respectively. These results show that KD-Judge enables transparent, efficient, and scalable rule-grounded rep-level analysis that can complement human judging in practice.

AI.tldr: KD-Judge: A knowledge-driven automated judging framework for functional fitness movements that converts rulebook standards into executable rules for transparent, deterministic rep-level validation with edge-optimized performance.
AI.motivation: Current functional fitness movement assessment relies on subjective human judgment with time constraints and evolving rules. Existing AI approaches lack transparency and deterministic rep-level validation due to reliance on learned scoring rather than explicit rule-based systems.
AI.method: Uses LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation and chain-of-thought rule-structuring to convert unstructured rulebooks into machine-readable representations. Implements deterministic rule-based judging with pose-guided kinematic reasoning for rep validity assessment. Introduces dual strategy caching for edge device optimization.
AI.result: Achieves reliable rule-structuring and accurate rep-level assessment on CFRep dataset with faster-than-real-time execution (RTF < 1). Caching provides 3.36x speedup for pre-recorded and 15.91x for live-streaming scenarios on resource-constrained edge devices compared to non-caching baseline.
AI.conclusion: KD-Judge enables transparent, efficient, and scalable rule-grounded rep-level analysis that can complement human judging in practice, addressing limitations of current subjective and opaque AI approaches.

title: Accelerating PayPal's Commerce Agent with Speculative Decoding: An Empirical Study on EAGLE3 with Fine-Tuned Nemotron Models
summary: We evaluate speculative decoding with EAGLE3 as an inference-time optimization for PayPal's Commerce Agent, powered by a fine-tuned llama3.1-nemotron-nano-8B-v1 model. Building on prior work (NEMO-4-PAYPAL) that reduced latency and cost through domain-specific fine-tuning, we benchmark EAGLE3 via vLLM against NVIDIA NIM on identical 2xH100 hardware across 40 configurations spanning speculative token counts (gamma=3, gamma=5), concurrency levels (1-32), and sampling temperatures (0, 0.5). Key findings: (1) gamma=3 achieves 22-49% throughput improvement and 18-33% latency reduction at zero additional hardware cost; (2) acceptance rates remain stable at approximately 35.5% for gamma=3 across all conditions; (3) gamma=5 yields diminishing returns (approximately 25% acceptance rate); (4) LLM-as-Judge evaluation confirms fully preserved output quality; and (5) speculative decoding on a single H100 matches or exceeds NIM on two H100s, enabling 50% GPU cost reduction.

AI.tldr: EAGLE3 speculative decoding boosts PayPal's Commerce Agent performance: 22-49% throughput gain, 18-33% latency reduction with gamma=3, zero hardware cost, preserved quality, and enables 50% GPU cost savings.
AI.motivation: To further optimize inference performance and reduce costs for PayPal's Commerce Agent beyond previous fine-tuning improvements, leveraging speculative decoding as an inference-time optimization.
AI.method: Benchmarked EAGLE3 speculative decoding via vLLM against NVIDIA NIM on identical 2xH100 hardware across 40 configurations varying speculative token counts (gamma=3,5), concurrency levels (1-32), and sampling temperatures (0,0.5).
AI.result: Gamma=3 achieved 22-49% throughput improvement and 18-33% latency reduction with stable ~35.5% acceptance rate; gamma=5 had diminishing returns (~25% acceptance); LLM-as-Judge confirmed preserved output quality; speculative decoding on single H100 matched/exceeded NIM on two H100s.
AI.conclusion: EAGLE3 speculative decoding provides significant performance gains at zero hardware cost, enables 50% GPU cost reduction while maintaining output quality, making it highly effective for production deployment of PayPal's Commerce Agent.

title: Algorithm Selection with Zero Domain Knowledge via Text Embeddings
summary: We propose a feature-free approach to algorithm selection that replaces hand-crafted instance features with pretrained text embeddings. Our method, ZeroFolio, proceeds in three steps: it reads the raw instance file as plain text, embeds it with a pretrained embedding model, and selects an algorithm via weighted k-nearest neighbors. The key to our approach is the observation that pretrained embeddings produce representations that distinguish problem instances without any domain knowledge or task-specific training. This allows us to apply the same three-step pipeline (serialize, embed, select) across diverse problem domains with text-based instance formats. We evaluate our approach on 11 ASlib scenarios spanning 7 domains (SAT, MaxSAT, QBF, ASP, CSP, MIP, and graph problems). Our experiments show that this approach outperforms a random forest trained on hand-crafted features in 10 of 11 scenarios with a single fixed configuration, and in all 11 with two-seed voting; the margin is often substantial. Our ablation study shows that inverse-distance weighting, line shuffling, and Manhattan distance are the key design choices. On scenarios where both selectors are competitive, combining embeddings with hand-crafted features via soft voting yields further improvements.

AI.tldr: ZeroFolio: A feature-free algorithm selection method using pretrained text embeddings on raw instance files, outperforming traditional feature-based approaches across multiple domains.
AI.motivation: Traditional algorithm selection relies on hand-crafted instance features which require domain expertise and may not capture all relevant information. The paper aims to eliminate this dependency by using pretrained text embeddings that can automatically distinguish problem instances without domain knowledge.
AI.method: Three-step pipeline: 1) Read raw instance file as plain text, 2) Embed text with pretrained embedding model, 3) Select algorithm via weighted k-nearest neighbors using Manhattan distance with inverse-distance weighting. Includes techniques like line shuffling for robustness.
AI.result: Outperforms random forest trained on hand-crafted features in 10/11 scenarios with single configuration, and all 11 with two-seed voting. Margin is often substantial. Key design choices: inverse-distance weighting, line shuffling, Manhattan distance. Combining embeddings with hand-crafted features via soft voting yields further improvements in competitive scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained text embeddings provide an effective, domain-agnostic alternative to hand-crafted features for algorithm selection, enabling a simple three-step pipeline that works across diverse problem domains with text-based instance formats.

title: Secure Rate-Distortion-Perception: A Randomized Distributed Function Computation Approach for Realism
summary: Fundamental rate-distortion-perception (RDP) trade-offs arise in applications requiring maintained perceptual quality of reconstructed data, such as neural image compression. When compressed data is transmitted over public communication channels, security risks emerge. We therefore study secure RDP under negligible information leakage over both noiseless channels and broadcast channels, BCs, with correlated noise components. For noiseless channels, the exact secure RDP region is characterized. For BCs, an inner bound is derived and shown to be tight for a class of more-capable BCs. Separate source-channel coding is further shown to be optimal for this exact secure RDP region with unlimited common randomness available. Moreover, when both encoder and decoder have access to side information correlated with the source and the channel is noiseless, the exact RDP region is established. If only the decoder has correlated side information in the noiseless setting, an inner bound is derived along with a special case where the region is exact. Binary and Gaussian examples demonstrate that common randomness can significantly reduce the communication rate in secure RDP settings, unlike in standard rate-distortion settings. Thus, our results illustrate that random binning-based coding achieves strong secrecy, low distortion, and high perceptual quality simultaneously.

AI.tldr: Secure rate-distortion-perception trade-offs with strong secrecy constraints for both noiseless and broadcast channels, showing optimality of separate source-channel coding and benefits of common randomness.
AI.motivation: When compressed data is transmitted over public channels, security risks emerge. Applications like neural image compression need to maintain perceptual quality while ensuring security against information leakage.
AI.method: Study secure RDP with negligible information leakage over noiseless channels and broadcast channels with correlated noise. Characterize exact secure RDP regions, derive inner bounds, and analyze separate source-channel coding with common randomness.
AI.result: Exact secure RDP region characterized for noiseless channels. Inner bound derived for BCs, tight for more-capable BCs. Separate source-channel coding shown optimal. Common randomness significantly reduces communication rate in secure RDP settings.
AI.conclusion: Random binning-based coding achieves strong secrecy, low distortion, and high perceptual quality simultaneously. Common randomness plays crucial role in secure RDP unlike standard rate-distortion settings.

title: Algebraic Diversity: Principles of a Group-Theoretic Approach to Signal Processing
summary: We present principles of algebraic diversity (AD), a group-theoretic approach to signal processing exploiting signal symmetry to extract more information per observation, complementing classical methods that use temporal and spatial diversity. The transformations under which a signal's statistics are invariant form a matched group; this group determines the natural transform for analysis, and averaging an estimator over the group action reduces variance without requiring additional snapshots. The viewpoint is broadened in five directions beyond the single-observation measurement of a companion paper. Rank promotion admits AD on scalar data streams and identifies the law of large numbers as the trivial-group case of a $(G, L)$ continuum combining sample-count with group-orbit averaging. An eigentensor hierarchy handles signals with nested symmetry. A blind group-matching methodology identifies the matched group from data via a polynomial-time generalized eigenvalue problem on the unitary Lie algebra, placing the DFT, DCT, and Karhunen--Loève transforms as distinguished points on a transform manifold. A cost-symmetry matching principle then extends AD from measurement to blind and adaptive signal processing generally; blind equalization is the lead detailed example, with the Constant Modulus Algorithm's residual phase ambiguity predicted analytically and matched within $1.6^\circ$ on 3GPP TDL multipath channels, and other blind problems in signal processing are mapped into the framework. Four theorems formalize a structural capacity $κ$, the Rényi-2 analog of Shannon and von Neumann's Rényi-1 entropies, quantifying how a signal's information is organized rather than how much information it contains. AD complements prior algebraic approaches including invariant estimation, minimax robust estimation, algebraic signal processing, and compressed sensing.

AI.tldr: Algebraic Diversity (AD) is a group-theoretic signal processing framework that exploits signal symmetry to extract more information per observation, complementing classical temporal/spatial diversity methods.
AI.motivation: To develop a more general signal processing framework that goes beyond classical diversity methods by exploiting inherent signal symmetries, allowing for improved information extraction from single observations without requiring additional snapshots.
AI.method: Uses group theory to identify matched groups under which signal statistics are invariant; employs rank promotion for scalar data, eigentensor hierarchy for nested symmetries, blind group-matching via polynomial-time generalized eigenvalue problems, and cost-symmetry matching for blind/adaptive processing.
AI.result: Establishes AD as a comprehensive framework that unifies DFT, DCT, and Karhunen-Loève transforms as points on a transform manifold; demonstrates practical applications including blind equalization with Constant Modulus Algorithm achieving 1.6° phase ambiguity on 3GPP channels; introduces structural capacity κ as Rényi-2 analog of Shannon entropy.
AI.conclusion: AD provides a broad, mathematically rigorous framework that complements classical signal processing by exploiting symmetry through group theory, enabling more efficient information extraction and unifying various transforms and algorithms under a single theoretical umbrella.

title: Robust Uniform Recovery of Structured Signals from Nonlinear Observations
summary: While it is well known that the restricted isometry property (RIP) guarantees uniform sparse recovery from noisy linear measurements, uniform recovery of structured signals from nonlinear observations remains much less understood. This paper shows that the restricted approximate invertibility condition (RAIC) provides a unified approach to this end. Particularly, uniform recovery is achieved by projected gradient descent (PGD) with gradients obeying RAIC for all signals. As an application, under a large class of piecewise Lipschitz link functions (possibly discontinuous), we develop a uniform recovery theory for Gaussian single-index model by establishing the uniform RAIC for the gradient of the (scaled) $\ell_2$ loss via a covering argument. The theory generalizes the nonuniform recovery guarantees due to Plan and Vershynin (2016); Oymak and Soltanolkotabi (2017) and exhibits additional error terms that can be interpreted as the cost of uniform recovery. Intriguingly, in the three canonical settings of (a) sparse recovery via PGD with $\ell_0$ projection (i.e., iterative hard thresholding (IHT)), (b) sparse recovery via PGD with $\ell_1$ projection, and (c) recovering approximately sparse signals via PGD with $\ell_1$ projection, the additional error terms are negligible and in turn our uniform recovery error rates are at the same order of existing nonuniform ones, up to log factors. Our results hence improve on Genzel and Stollenwerk (2023). Under the specific nonlinearity of 1-bit quantization, we use a VC dimension argument to show that the uniform recovery error of IHT is at the same order of the nonuniform recovery error, with no loss of log factor. In addition, we show that the robustness of PGD to noise and corruption can be incorporated elegantly by bounding a single additional random process that captures the gradient mismatch.

AI.tldr: RAIC enables uniform sparse recovery from nonlinear measurements via projected gradient descent, generalizing previous nonuniform results with minimal additional error.
AI.motivation: While RIP guarantees uniform sparse recovery from linear measurements, uniform recovery from nonlinear observations remains poorly understood. The paper aims to develop a unified theory for uniform recovery of structured signals from nonlinear measurements.
AI.method: Introduces Restricted Approximate Invertibility Condition (RAIC) as a unified framework. Uses projected gradient descent (PGD) with gradients obeying RAIC for all signals. Applies this to Gaussian single-index models with piecewise Lipschitz link functions via covering arguments. Also uses VC dimension arguments for 1-bit quantization case.
AI.result: Establishes uniform recovery theory for nonlinear measurements. Shows that in three canonical settings (sparse recovery via PGD with ℓ₀/ℓ₁ projection, and approximately sparse signals with ℓ₁ projection), additional error terms for uniform recovery are negligible compared to existing nonuniform results. For 1-bit quantization, uniform recovery error matches nonuniform error without log factor loss.
AI.conclusion: RAIC provides a powerful unified framework for uniform sparse recovery from nonlinear measurements, achieving error rates comparable to existing nonuniform results while improving upon prior work. The approach elegantly handles noise and corruption through gradient mismatch analysis.

title: LLM-Guided Safety Agent for Edge Robotics with an ISO-Compliant Perception-Compute-Control Architecture
summary: Ensuring functional safety in human-robot interaction is challenging because AI perception is inherently probabilistic, whereas industrial standards require deterministic behavior. We present an LLM-guided safety agent for edge robotics, built on an ISO-compliant low-latency perception-compute-control architecture. Our method translates natural-language safety regulations into executable predicates and deploys them through a redundant heterogeneous edge runtime. For fault-tolerant closed-loop execution under edge constraints, we adopt a symmetric dual-modular redundancy design with parallel independent execution for low-latency perception, computation, and control. We prototype the system on a dual-RK3588 platform and evaluate it in representative human-robot interaction scenarios. The results demonstrate a practical edge implementation path toward ISO 13849 Category 3 and PL d using cost-effective hardware, supporting practical deployment of safety-critical embodied AI.

AI.tldr: LLM-guided safety agent for edge robotics that translates natural-language safety regulations into executable predicates using ISO-compliant low-latency architecture with dual-modular redundancy for fault tolerance.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of ensuring functional safety in human-robot interaction where AI perception is probabilistic but industrial standards require deterministic behavior. Bridge the gap between probabilistic AI and deterministic safety requirements.
AI.method: Uses LLM to translate natural-language safety regulations into executable predicates. Implements redundant heterogeneous edge runtime with symmetric dual-modular redundancy design for parallel independent execution of perception, computation, and control. Built on ISO-compliant low-latency perception-compute-control architecture.
AI.result: Prototyped on dual-RK3588 platform and evaluated in human-robot interaction scenarios. Demonstrates practical edge implementation path toward ISO 13849 Category 3 and PL d using cost-effective hardware.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical deployment path for safety-critical embodied AI by combining LLM-guided safety regulation translation with fault-tolerant edge architecture, enabling deterministic safety behavior from probabilistic AI systems.

title: Environmental Understanding Vision-Language Model for Embodied Agent
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown strong perception and reasoning abilities for instruction-following embodied agents. However, despite these abilities and their generalization performance, they still face limitations in environmental understanding, often failing on interactions or relying on environment metadata during execution. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework named Environmental Understanding Embodied Agent (EUEA), which fine-tunes four core skills: 1) object perception for identifying relevant objects, 2) task planning for generating interaction subgoals, 3) action understanding for judging success likelihood, and 4) goal recognition for determining goal completion. By fine-tuning VLMs with EUEA skills, our framework enables more reliable task execution for instruction-following. We further introduce a recovery step that leverages these core skills and a group relative policy optimization (GRPO) stage that refines inconsistent skill predictions. The recovery step samples alternative actions to correct failure cases, and the GRPO stage refines inconsistent skill predictions. Across ALFRED tasks, our VLM significantly outperforms a behavior-cloning baseline, achieving an 8.86% improvement in average success rate. The recovery and GRPO stages provide an additional 3.03% gain, further enhancing overall performance. Finally, our skill-level analyses reveal key limitations in the environmental understanding of closed- and open-source VLMs and identify the capabilities necessary for effective agent-environment interaction.

AI.tldr: EUEA framework fine-tunes VLMs with four core skills for better embodied agent performance, achieving 8.86% improvement over baselines on ALFRED tasks.
AI.motivation: VLMs have strong perception and reasoning for embodied agents but still face limitations in environmental understanding, often failing on interactions or relying on environment metadata during execution.
AI.method: Proposes EUEA framework that fine-tunes VLMs with four core skills: object perception, task planning, action understanding, and goal recognition. Includes recovery step for alternative actions and GRPO stage to refine inconsistent skill predictions.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms behavior-cloning baseline with 8.86% improvement in average success rate on ALFRED tasks. Recovery and GRPO stages provide additional 3.03% gain. Skill-level analyses reveal limitations in VLMs' environmental understanding.
AI.conclusion: EUEA framework enables more reliable task execution for instruction-following agents by addressing environmental understanding limitations through skill fine-tuning and refinement mechanisms.

title: On-Meter Graph Machine Learning: A Case Study of PV Power Forecasting for Grid Edge Intelligence
comment: This paper has been accepted for presentation at the 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE 2026) in Nanjing, China, April 17-19, 2026
summary: This paper presents a detailed study of how graph neural networks can be used on edge intelligent meters in a microgrid to forecast photovoltaic power generation. The problem background and the adopted technologies are introduced, including ONNX and ONNX Runtime. The hardware and software specifications of the smart meter are also briefly described. Then, the paper focuses on the training and deployment of two graph machine learning models, GCN and GraphSAGE, with particular emphasis on developing and deploying a customized ONNX operator for GCN. Finally, a case study is conducted using real datasets from a village microgrid. The performance of the two models is compared on both the PC and the smart meter, exhibiting successful deployments and executions on the smart meter.

AI.tldr: Graph neural networks (GCN and GraphSAGE) deployed on edge smart meters for PV power forecasting in microgrids using ONNX Runtime.
AI.motivation: Need for efficient photovoltaic power generation forecasting at the edge in microgrids using graph neural networks on resource-constrained smart meters.
AI.method: Developed and deployed GCN and GraphSAGE models using ONNX Runtime on edge smart meters, including creating a customized ONNX operator for GCN.
AI.result: Successful deployment and execution of both models on smart meters using real village microgrid datasets, with performance comparisons between PC and edge device.
AI.conclusion: Graph neural networks can be effectively deployed on edge intelligent meters for PV forecasting, demonstrating practical edge AI applications in microgrids.

title: Exploring Data Augmentation and Resampling Strategies for Transformer-Based Models to Address Class Imbalance in AI Scoring of Scientific Explanations in NGSS Classroom
comment: Published as a conference paper at NARST 2026
summary:
Automated scoring of students' scientific explanations offers the potential for immediate, accurate feedback, yet class imbalance in rubric categories particularly those capturing advanced reasoning remains a challenge. This study investigates augmentation strategies to improve transformer-based text classification of student responses to a physical science assessment based on an NGSS-aligned learning progression. The dataset consists of 1,466 high school responses scored on 11 binary-coded analytic categories. This rubric identifies six important components including scientific ideas needed for a complete explanation along with five common incomplete or inaccurate ideas. Using SciBERT as a baseline, we applied fine-tuning and test these augmentation strategies: (1) GPT-4--generated synthetic responses, (2) EASE, a word-level extraction and filtering approach, and (3) ALP (Augmentation using Lexicalized Probabilistic context-free grammar) phrase-level extraction.
  While fine-tuning SciBERT improved recall over baseline, augmentation substantially enhanced performance, with GPT data boosting both precision and recall, and ALP achieving perfect precision, recall, and F1 scores across most severe imbalanced categories (5,6,7 and 9). Across all rubric categories EASE augmentation substantially increased alignment with human scoring for both scientific ideas (Categories 1--6) and inaccurate ideas (Categories 7--11). We compared different augmentation strategies to a traditional oversampling method (SMOTE) in an effort to avoid overfitting and retain novice-level data critical for learning progression alignment. Findings demonstrate that targeted augmentation can address severe imbalance while preserving conceptual coverage, offering a scalable solution for automated learning progression-aligned scoring in science education.
