AI.tldr: Transformer-based text classification for scoring student science explanations using augmentation strategies to address class imbalance in rubric categories.
AI.motivation: Automated scoring of scientific explanations offers immediate feedback, but class imbalance in rubric categories (especially advanced reasoning) remains challenging.
AI.method: Used SciBERT as baseline, fine-tuned it, and tested three augmentation strategies: GPT-4-generated synthetic responses, EASE (word-level extraction/filtering), and ALP (phrase-level extraction). Compared with traditional SMOTE oversampling.
AI.result: Fine-tuning improved recall; augmentation substantially enhanced performance. GPT-4 boosted precision and recall; ALP achieved perfect scores in most severe imbalanced categories. EASE increased alignment with human scoring across all categories.
AI.conclusion: Targeted augmentation can address severe class imbalance while preserving conceptual coverage, offering scalable solution for automated learning progression-aligned scoring in science education.

title: Adaptive Multi-UAV Relay Deployment Framework in Satellite Aerial Ground Integrated Systems
summary: The sixth generation (6G) communication networks are expected to provide high data rates, ultra-reliable communication, and massive connectivity, especially in challenging environments such as dense urban areas and disaster-affected regions. However, traditional terrestrial-only networks face significant challenges in these scenarios, including signal blockages from high-rise buildings, traffic congestion, and dynamic user distributions. To address these limitations, we propose the adaptive multi-UAV deployment (AMUD) framework within satellite air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs). The AMUD framework dynamically deploys amplify-and-forward multiple unmanned aerial vehicle relay (UAVr) in with low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites to improve coverage, alleviate congestion, and ensure reliable communication in non-line-of-sight and high-demand conditions. We formulate an optimization problem that aims to jointly maximize the energy efficiency of the total network and the total capacity while ensuring the fairness of the total capacity and satisfying the users' requirements. The simulation results demonstrate that AMUD improves the total capacity of the network, improves the total energy efficiency, and increases the fairness of the capacity compared to traditional LEO satellite and ground base station (LEO-GBS) only systems.

AI.tldr: AMUD framework uses adaptive multi-UAV deployment in satellite-air-ground networks to improve coverage, capacity, and energy efficiency in challenging 6G environments.
AI.motivation: Traditional terrestrial networks struggle with signal blockages, congestion, and dynamic user distributions in dense urban and disaster areas for 6G networks.
AI.method: Proposes AMUD framework that dynamically deploys amplify-and-forward UAV relays with LEO satellites in SAGINs, formulated as an optimization problem maximizing energy efficiency and capacity while ensuring fairness.
AI.result: AMUD improves total network capacity, energy efficiency, and capacity fairness compared to traditional LEO satellite and ground base station only systems.
AI.conclusion: AMUD framework effectively addresses 6G network challenges in difficult environments by integrating adaptive UAV deployment with satellite-ground systems.

title: Computationally Efficient Sparse Signal Recovery via Linear Sketching and Deep Unfolding
summary: This paper provides a sparse signal recovery algorithm, DU-PSISTA (Deep Unfolded-Periodic Sketched Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm), which aims to balance computational efficiency and accuracy for recovering high-dimensional sparse signals, and a convergence analysis under sufficient conditions. DU-PSISTA introduces a random matrix projection known as sketching to reduce the dimensionality of gradient computations and periodically alternates between the standard ISTA and the sketched variant. This hybrid structure enables flexible control over the trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity through a pre-configurable period parameter. The algorithm includes many parameters to be tuned such as step sizes and thresholding factors so that we incorporate deep unfolding that optimizes the parameters through data-driven training, enabling the algorithm to adaptively improve convergence speed and performance. We show that the proposed method achieves a linear-type contraction to a neighborhood of the true sparse signal with properly selected parameters. The analysis provides an interpretation for the effectiveness of the hybrid structure to improve recovery accuracy. Numerical experiments confirm that our method achieves comparable recovery performance to conventional deep unfolded ISTA while reducing computational complexity, especially when the period parameter and sketch size are properly selected. The results are also consistent with the theoretical insights.

AI.tldr: DU-PSISTA is a hybrid sparse signal recovery algorithm combining standard ISTA with sketched variants using deep unfolding for parameter optimization, achieving computational efficiency with comparable accuracy.
AI.motivation: To balance computational efficiency and accuracy for recovering high-dimensional sparse signals, addressing the trade-off between computational complexity and recovery performance.
AI.method: Hybrid algorithm that periodically alternates between standard ISTA and sketched ISTA (using random matrix projection/sketching to reduce gradient computation dimensionality), with deep unfolding to optimize parameters like step sizes and thresholding factors through data-driven training.
AI.result: The method achieves linear-type contraction to a neighborhood of the true sparse signal with properly selected parameters, achieves comparable recovery performance to conventional deep unfolded ISTA while reducing computational complexity, especially when period parameter and sketch size are properly selected.
AI.conclusion: DU-PSISTA effectively balances accuracy and computational efficiency through its hybrid structure and deep unfolding optimization, with theoretical analysis supporting its effectiveness and numerical experiments confirming practical performance gains.

title: Stochastic Barrier Certificates in the Presence of Dynamic Obstacles
summary: Safety of stochastic dynamic systems in environments with dynamic obstacles is studied in this paper through the lens of stochastic barrier functions. We introduce both time-invariant and time-varying barrier certificates for discrete-time, continuous-space systems subject to uncertainty, which provide certified lower bounds on the probability of remaining within a safe set over a finite horizon. These certificates explicitly account for time-varying unsafe regions induced by obstacle dynamics. By leveraging Bellman's optimality perspective, the time-varying formulation directly captures temporal structure and yields less conservative bounds than state-of-the-art approaches. By restricting certificates to polynomial functions, we show that time-varying barrier synthesis can be formulated as a convex sum-of-squares program, enabling tractable optimization. Empirical evaluations on nonlinear systems with dynamic obstacles show that time-varying certificates consistently achieve tight guarantees, demonstrating improved accuracy and scalability over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces stochastic barrier functions for safety verification of discrete-time systems with dynamic obstacles, providing probabilistic safety guarantees through convex optimization.
AI.motivation: To address safety verification for stochastic dynamic systems operating in environments with moving obstacles, where traditional methods may be conservative or intractable.
AI.method: Develops both time-invariant and time-varying stochastic barrier certificates for discrete-time continuous-space systems with uncertainty. Uses Bellman's optimality principle to capture temporal structure and formulates time-varying barrier synthesis as convex sum-of-squares programs for polynomial functions.
AI.result: Time-varying certificates provide less conservative safety bounds than state-of-the-art approaches and achieve tight probabilistic guarantees for nonlinear systems with dynamic obstacles, demonstrating improved accuracy and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed stochastic barrier function framework offers a tractable, convex optimization approach for safety verification of stochastic systems in dynamic environments, with time-varying certificates providing superior performance over existing methods.

title: If you're waiting for a sign... that might not be it! Mitigating Trust Boundary Confusion from Visual Injections on Vision-Language Agentic Systems
summary: Recent advances in embodied Vision-Language Agentic Systems (VLAS), powered by large vision-language models (LVLMs), enable AI systems to perceive and reason over real-world scenes. Within this context, environmental signals such as traffic lights are essential in-band signals that can and should influence agent behavior. However, similar signals could also be crafted to operate as misleading visual injections, overriding user intent and posing security risks. This duality creates a fundamental challenge: agents must respond to legitimate environmental cues while remaining robust to misleading ones. We refer to this tension as trust boundary confusion. To study this behavior, we design a dual-intent dataset and evaluation framework, through which we show that current LVLM-based agents fail to reliably balance this trade-off, either ignoring useful signals or following harmful ones. We systematically evaluate 7 LVLM agents across multiple embodied settings under both structure-based and noise-based visual injections. To address these vulnerabilities, we propose a multi-agent defense framework that separates perception from decision-making to dynamically assess the reliability of visual inputs. Our approach significantly reduces misleading behaviors while preserving correct responses and provides robustness guarantees under adversarial perturbations. The code of the evaluation framework and artifacts are made available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Visual-Prompt-Inject.

AI.tldr: Current vision-language agents struggle to distinguish legitimate environmental signals from malicious visual injections, creating security vulnerabilities in embodied AI systems.
AI.motivation: Embodied vision-language agents must respond to legitimate environmental cues (like traffic lights) but remain robust against malicious visual injections that could override user intent and pose security risks. This creates a fundamental trust boundary confusion problem.
AI.method: Created a dual-intent dataset and evaluation framework to test agents' ability to balance this trade-off. Evaluated 7 LVLM agents across multiple embodied settings under both structure-based and noise-based visual injections. Proposed a multi-agent defense framework that separates perception from decision-making to dynamically assess visual input reliability.
AI.result: Current LVLM-based agents fail to reliably balance the trade-off - they either ignore useful signals or follow harmful ones. The proposed multi-agent defense significantly reduces misleading behaviors while preserving correct responses and provides robustness guarantees under adversarial perturbations.
AI.conclusion: There's a critical need for robust trust boundary management in embodied AI systems. The proposed separation of perception and decision-making with dynamic reliability assessment offers an effective defense against visual injection attacks while maintaining responsiveness to legitimate environmental signals.

title: Expert Upcycling: Shifting the Compute-Efficient Frontier of Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has become the dominant architecture for scaling large language models: frontier models routinely decouple total parameters from per-token computation through sparse expert routing. Scaling laws show that under fixed active computation, model quality scales predictably with total parameters, and MoEs realize this by increasing expert count. However, training large MoEs is expensive, as memory requirements and inter-device communication both scale with total parameter count. We propose expert upcycling, a method for progressively expanding MoE capacity by increasing the number of experts during continued pre-training (CPT). Given a trained E-expert model, the upcycling operator constructs an mE-expert model through expert duplication and router extension while holding top-K routing fixed, preserving per-token inference cost. Duplication provides a warm initialization: the expanded model inherits the source checkpoint's learned representations, starting from a substantially lower loss than random initialization. Subsequent CPT then breaks the symmetry among duplicated experts to drive specialization. We formalize the upcycling operator and develop a theoretical framework decomposing the quality gap into a capacity term and an initialization term. We further introduce utility-based expert selection, which uses gradient-based importance scores to guide non-uniform duplication, more than tripling gap closure when CPT is limited. In our 7B-13B total parameter experiments, the upcycled model matches the fixed-size baseline on validation loss while saving 32% of GPU hours. Comprehensive ablations across model scales, activation ratios, MoE architectures, and training budgets yield a practical recipe for deploying expert upcycling, establishing it as a principled, compute-efficient alternative to training large MoE models from scratch.

AI.tldr: Expert upcycling: A compute-efficient method to expand MoE models by duplicating experts during continued pre-training, preserving inference cost while reducing training costs by 32%.
AI.motivation: Training large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models is expensive due to memory requirements and inter-device communication scaling with total parameters. There's a need for efficient ways to expand model capacity without training from scratch.
AI.method: Expert upcycling expands MoE capacity by duplicating experts during continued pre-training. Given an E-expert model, it constructs an mE-expert model through expert duplication and router extension while preserving top-K routing. Duplication provides warm initialization, and subsequent CPT breaks symmetry among duplicated experts for specialization. Includes utility-based expert selection using gradient importance scores.
AI.result: In 7B-13B parameter experiments, upcycled models match fixed-size baselines on validation loss while saving 32% of GPU hours. Utility-based expert selection triples gap closure when CPT is limited. Comprehensive ablations across scales, activation ratios, and architectures yield practical deployment recipes.
AI.conclusion: Expert upcycling is a principled, compute-efficient alternative to training large MoE models from scratch, enabling progressive capacity expansion while maintaining inference efficiency and reducing training costs.

title: Explainable AML Triage with LLMs: Evidence Retrieval and Counterfactual Checks
summary: Anti-money laundering (AML) transaction monitoring generates large volumes of alerts that must be rapidly triaged by investigators under strict audit and governance constraints. While large language models (LLMs) can summarize heterogeneous evidence and draft rationales, unconstrained generation is risky in regulated workflows due to hallucinations, weak provenance, and explanations that are not faithful to the underlying decision. We propose an explainable AML triage framework that treats triage as an evidence-constrained decision process. Our method combines (i) retrieval-augmented evidence bundling from policy/typology guidance, customer context, alert triggers, and transaction subgraphs, (ii) a structured LLM output contract that requires explicit citations and separates supporting from contradicting or missing evidence, and (iii) counterfactual checks that validate whether minimal, plausible perturbations lead to coherent changes in both the triage recommendation and its rationale. We evaluate on public synthetic AML benchmarks and simulators and compare against rules, tabular and graph machine-learning baselines, and LLM-only/RAG-only variants. Results show that evidence grounding substantially improves auditability and reduces numerical and policy hallucination errors, while counterfactual validation further increases decision-linked explainability and robustness, yielding the best overall triage performance (PR-AUC 0.75; Escalate F1 0.62) and strong provenance and faithfulness metrics (citation validity 0.98; evidence support 0.88; counterfactual faithfulness 0.76). These findings indicate that governed, verifiable LLM systems can provide practical decision support for AML triage without sacrificing compliance requirements for traceability and defensibility.

AI.tldr: An explainable AML triage framework using evidence-constrained LLMs with retrieval augmentation, structured output contracts, and counterfactual validation to improve auditability and reduce hallucinations.
AI.motivation: AML transaction monitoring generates many alerts that need rapid triage under strict audit constraints. LLMs can help but unconstrained generation risks hallucinations, weak provenance, and unfaithful explanations in regulated workflows.
AI.method: Proposes an explainable AML triage framework with: (1) retrieval-augmented evidence bundling from policy guidance, customer context, alerts, and transaction subgraphs; (2) structured LLM output contracts requiring explicit citations and separating supporting/contradicting/missing evidence; (3) counterfactual checks validating if minimal perturbations lead to coherent changes in recommendations and rationales.
AI.result: Evidence grounding substantially improves auditability and reduces numerical/policy hallucination errors. Counterfactual validation increases decision-linked explainability and robustness. Best overall triage performance (PR-AUC 0.75; Escalate F1 0.62) with strong provenance/faithfulness metrics (citation validity 0.98; evidence support 0.88; counterfactual faithfulness 0.76).
AI.conclusion: Governed, verifiable LLM systems can provide practical decision support for AML triage without sacrificing compliance requirements for traceability and defensibility.

title: Descriptor: A Hybrid Indoor and Indoor-Outdoor Positioning Multi-Technology Dataset (HYMN)
summary: This article introduces the HYMN (HYbrid Multi-technology Navigation) dataset: a multi-system, and time synchronized dataset for localization research based on opportunistic signals collected in an indoor-outdoor scenario. HYMN comprises measurement data collected in an industrial hall setting for five different positioning systems including Ultra-Wideband (UWB), Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), WiFi, 5G, and Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS). Unlike existing datasets that focus on single technologies or purely indoor/outdoor scenarios, HYMN combines five positioning technologies with explicit coverage of indoor-outdoor transitions, enabling multi-sensor fusion research for seamless localization. Each instance of data is identified through a unique measurement id and it represents time-stamped observations relevant for each system respectively along with the ground truth information. HYMN is designed to support a wide range of localization tasks including multi-sensor fingerprinting, cross-technology fusion, and seamless indoor-outdoor positioning. The synchronized measurements from GNSS and other terrestrial systems enable researchers to investigate how heterogeneous signals complement each other to overcome individual technology limitations such as GNSS degradation in covered areas or terrestrial system variability in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: HYMN dataset provides synchronized multi-technology (UWB, BLE, WiFi, 5G, GNSS) measurements for indoor-outdoor localization research with ground truth data.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets focus on single technologies or purely indoor/outdoor scenarios, lacking comprehensive multi-system data for seamless localization research across indoor-outdoor transitions.
AI.method: Created a time-synchronized dataset collected in an industrial hall setting with five positioning systems: UWB, BLE, WiFi, 5G, and GNSS. Each data instance has unique measurement ID with time-stamped observations and ground truth information.
AI.result: HYMN dataset enables multi-sensor fusion research for seamless localization, supporting tasks like multi-sensor fingerprinting, cross-technology fusion, and indoor-outdoor positioning.
AI.conclusion: The synchronized multi-technology dataset allows researchers to investigate how heterogeneous signals complement each other to overcome individual technology limitations (e.g., GNSS degradation indoors, terrestrial system variability).

title: Trajectory Design for Fairness Enhancement in Movable Antennas-Aided Communications
summary: Through adaptive antenna repositioning, the movable antenna (MA) technology enables on-demand reconfiguration of wireless channels, thereby creating an additional spatial degree of freedom in improving communication performance. This paper investigates a multiuser uplink communication system aided by MAs, where a base station (BS) equipped with multiple MAs serves multiple single-antenna users. Specifically, given that an optimized array geometry cannot guarantee rate fairness, we focus on designing antenna trajectory at the BS to maximize the minimum achievable rate among all users over a finite time period. The resulting optimization problem is fundamentally challenging to solve due to the continuous-time nature. To address it, we first examine an ideal case with infinitely fast MA movement and demonstrate that the relaxed problem can be optimally solved via the Lagrangian dual method. The obtained trajectory solution reveals that the BS should employ a finite set of MA deployment patterns, each allocated an optimal time duration. Building on this, we then study the general case with limited MA movement speed and propose a heuristic trajectory design inspired by the optimal patterns identified in the ideal scenario. Several insights are also gained by examining the simplified special case. Finally, numerical results are provided to validate the effectiveness of the proposed designs compared to competitive benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes movable antenna (MA) trajectory designs for multiuser uplink systems to maximize minimum user rates, addressing both ideal (infinitely fast movement) and practical (limited speed) scenarios.
AI.motivation: Movable antenna technology enables on-demand channel reconfiguration, creating spatial degrees of freedom to improve communication performance. The paper aims to address rate fairness in multiuser uplink systems by optimizing MA trajectories at the base station.
AI.method: The authors first solve an ideal case with infinitely fast MA movement using Lagrangian dual method, revealing optimal deployment patterns. Then they propose heuristic trajectory designs for practical limited-speed scenarios based on insights from the ideal case. They also examine simplified special cases for additional insights.
AI.result: The optimal solution for the ideal case shows that the BS should employ a finite set of MA deployment patterns with optimal time allocations. For practical scenarios, the proposed heuristic designs effectively leverage these patterns while accommodating speed constraints.
AI.conclusion: Numerical results validate that the proposed MA trajectory designs outperform competitive benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of using movable antennas to improve rate fairness in multiuser uplink communications.

title: Toward Cooperative Driving in Mixed Traffic: An Adaptive Potential Game-Based Approach with Field Test Verification
summary: Connected autonomous vehicles (CAVs), which represent a significant advancement in autonomous driving technology, have the potential to greatly increase traffic safety and efficiency through cooperative decision-making. However, existing methods often overlook the individual needs and heterogeneity of cooperative participants, making it difficult to transfer them to environments where they coexist with human-driven vehicles (HDVs).To address this challenge, this paper proposes an adaptive potential game (APG) cooperative driving framework. First, the system utility function is established on the basis of a general form of individual utility and its monotonic relationship, allowing for the simultaneous optimization of both individual and system objectives. Second, the Shapley value is introduced to compute each vehicle's marginal utility within the system, allowing its varying impact to be quantified. Finally, the HDV preference estimation is dynamically refined by continuously comparing the observed HDV behavior with the APG's estimated actions, leading to improvements in overall system safety and efficiency. Ablation studies demonstrate that adaptively updating Shapley values and HDV preference estimation significantly improve cooperation success rates in mixed traffic. Comparative experiments further highlight the APG's advantages in terms of safety and efficiency over other cooperative methods. Moreover, the applicability of the approach to real-world scenarios was validated through field tests.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an Adaptive Potential Game (APG) framework for cooperative driving in mixed traffic environments with both connected autonomous vehicles (CAVs) and human-driven vehicles (HDVs), addressing heterogeneity and individual needs.
AI.motivation: Existing cooperative driving methods often overlook individual needs and heterogeneity of participants, making them difficult to transfer to mixed traffic environments where CAVs coexist with HDVs. There's a need for a framework that can handle diverse vehicle types and preferences while optimizing both individual and system objectives.
AI.method: Proposes an Adaptive Potential Game (APG) framework with three key components: 1) Establishing system utility function based on individual utility and their monotonic relationship for simultaneous optimization, 2) Using Shapley value to compute each vehicle's marginal utility and quantify varying impacts, 3) Dynamically refining HDV preference estimation by comparing observed behavior with APG's estimated actions.
AI.result: Ablation studies show adaptive updating of Shapley values and HDV preference estimation significantly improves cooperation success rates in mixed traffic. Comparative experiments demonstrate APG's advantages in safety and efficiency over other cooperative methods. Field tests validate the approach's applicability to real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The APG framework effectively addresses heterogeneity in mixed traffic environments, enabling better cooperation between CAVs and HDVs by considering individual needs, quantifying vehicle impacts through Shapley values, and dynamically adapting to human driving preferences, resulting in improved safety and efficiency.

title: Wan-Image: Pushing the Boundaries of Generative Visual Intelligence
summary: We present Wan-Image, a unified visual generation system explicitly engineered to paradigm-shift image generation models from casual synthesizers into professional-grade productivity tools. While contemporary diffusion models excel at aesthetic generation, they frequently encounter critical bottlenecks in rigorous design workflows that demand absolute controllability, complex typography rendering, and strict identity preservation. To address these challenges, Wan-Image features a natively unified multi-modal architecture by synergizing the cognitive capabilities of large language models with the high-fidelity pixel synthesis of diffusion transformers, which seamlessly translates highly nuanced user intents into precise visual outputs. It is fundamentally powered by large-scale multi-modal data scaling, a systematic fine-grained annotation engine, and curated reinforcement learning data to surpass basic instruction following and unlock expert-level professional capabilities. These include ultra-long complex text rendering, hyper-diverse portrait generation, palette-guided generation, multi-subject identity preservation, coherent sequential visual generation, precise multi-modal interactive editing, native alpha-channel generation, and high-efficiency 4K synthesis. Across diverse human evaluations, Wan-Image exceeds Seedream 5.0 Lite and GPT Image 1.5 in overall performance, reaching parity with Nano Banana Pro in challenging tasks. Ultimately, Wan-Image revolutionizes visual content creation across e-commerce, entertainment, education, and personal productivity, redefining the boundaries of professional visual synthesis.

AI.tldr: Wan-Image is a unified visual generation system that transforms image generation models from casual tools into professional-grade productivity tools with superior controllability, typography rendering, and identity preservation.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion models excel at aesthetic generation but fail in professional design workflows requiring absolute controllability, complex typography rendering, and strict identity preservation. There's a need for models that can handle rigorous design demands beyond casual synthesis.
AI.method: Unified multi-modal architecture combining large language models' cognitive capabilities with diffusion transformers' high-fidelity pixel synthesis. Powered by large-scale multi-modal data scaling, systematic fine-grained annotation engine, and curated reinforcement learning data to enable expert-level capabilities.
AI.result: Wan-Image achieves professional capabilities including ultra-long complex text rendering, hyper-diverse portrait generation, palette-guided generation, multi-subject identity preservation, coherent sequential visual generation, precise multi-modal interactive editing, native alpha-channel generation, and high-efficiency 4K synthesis. It exceeds Seedream 5.0 Lite and GPT Image 1.5 in overall performance and reaches parity with Nano Banana Pro in challenging tasks.
AI.conclusion: Wan-Image revolutionizes visual content creation across e-commerce, entertainment, education, and personal productivity by redefining the boundaries of professional visual synthesis, transforming image generation models into true productivity tools.

title: Graph-Theoretic Models for the Prediction of Molecular Measurements
summary: Graph-theoretic approaches offer simplicity, interpretability, and low computational cost for molecular property prediction. Among these, the model proposed by Mukwembi and Nyabadza, based on the external activity $D(G)$ and internal activity $ζ(G)$ indices, achieved strong results on a small flavonoid dataset. However, its ability to generalize to larger and chemically diverse datasets has not been tested. This study evaluates the baseline $D(G)$-$ζ(G)$ polynomial model on five benchmark datasets from MoleculeNet, covering biological activity (BACE, 1,513 molecules), lipophilicity (LogP synthetic, 14,610 molecules; LogP experimental, 753 molecules), aqueous solubility (ESOL, 1,128 molecules), and hydration free energy (SAMPL, 642 molecules). The baseline model achieves an average $R^2 = 0.24$, confirming limited transferability. To address this, a systematic enhancement framework is proposed, progressively incorporating Ridge regularization, additional graph descriptors, physicochemical properties, ensemble learning with Gradient Boosting, Lasso feature selection, and a hybrid approach combining topological indices with Morgan fingerprints. The enhanced models raise the average best $R^2$ to 0.79, with individual improvements ranging from 165\% to 274\%. All improvements are statistically significant ($p < 0.001$). A direct comparison with a Graph Convolutional Network under identical experimental conditions shows that the enhanced classical models match or outperform deep learning on all five datasets. Comparison with the recent GNN+PGM hybrid of Djagba et al.\ further confirms competitiveness, with the enhanced models achieving the best results on two datasets and tying on one. The entire framework requires no GPU, trains in under five minutes, and uses only open-source tools, making it accessible for researchers in resource-limited settings.

AI.tldr: A graph-theoretic molecular property prediction model (D(G)-ζ(G) polynomial) shows limited generalization to diverse datasets (avg R²=0.24). Enhanced with regularization, descriptors, ensemble learning, and feature selection, performance improves dramatically (avg R²=0.79), matching or outperforming GNNs without GPU requirements.
AI.motivation: To test the generalization ability of the D(G)-ζ(G) polynomial model on larger, chemically diverse molecular datasets, and to develop an accessible enhancement framework that can compete with deep learning methods without requiring GPU resources.
AI.method: Systematic enhancement framework: Ridge regularization → additional graph descriptors → physicochemical properties → ensemble learning with Gradient Boosting → Lasso feature selection → hybrid approach combining topological indices with Morgan fingerprints. Evaluated on five MoleculeNet datasets (BACE, LogP synthetic/experimental, ESOL, SAMPL).
AI.result: Baseline model: average R²=0.24. Enhanced models: average best R²=0.79 (165-274% improvements). Enhanced classical models match or outperform Graph Convolutional Networks on all five datasets, and compete with recent GNN+PGM hybrid. All improvements statistically significant (p<0.001).
AI.conclusion: The enhanced classical framework achieves competitive performance with deep learning methods while requiring no GPU, training in under 5 minutes with open-source tools, making it accessible for resource-limited researchers. Graph-theoretic approaches can be effectively enhanced to handle diverse molecular property prediction tasks.

title: ThermoQA: A Three-Tier Benchmark for Evaluating Thermodynamic Reasoning in Large Language Models
comment: 17 pages, 8 figures, open-source dataset and code
summary: We present ThermoQA, a benchmark of 293 open-ended engineering thermodynamics problems in three tiers: property lookups (110 Q), component analysis (101 Q), and full cycle analysis (82 Q). Ground truth is computed programmatically from CoolProp 7.2.0, covering water, R-134a, and variable-cp air. Six frontier LLMs are evaluated across three independent runs each. The composite leaderboard is led by Claude Opus 4.6 (94.1%), GPT-5.4 (93.1%), and Gemini 3.1 Pro (92.5%). Cross-tier degradation ranges from 2.8 pp (Opus) to 32.5 pp (MiniMax), confirming that property memorization does not imply thermodynamic reasoning. Supercritical water, R-134a refrigerant, and combined-cycle gas turbine analysis serve as natural discriminators with 40-60 pp performance spreads. Multi-run sigma ranges from +/-0.1% to +/-2.5%, quantifying reasoning consistency as a distinct evaluation axis. Dataset and code are open-source at https://huggingface.co/datasets/olivenet/thermoqa

AI.tldr: ThermoQA is a benchmark of 293 thermodynamics problems with three difficulty tiers, used to evaluate LLMs' thermodynamic reasoning beyond property memorization.
AI.motivation: To assess whether LLMs can perform genuine thermodynamic reasoning rather than just memorizing property values, and to provide a standardized benchmark for evaluating engineering thermodynamics capabilities in language models.
AI.method: Created 293 open-ended thermodynamics problems across three tiers (property lookups, component analysis, cycle analysis) with programmatically computed ground truth using CoolProp. Evaluated six frontier LLMs across three independent runs each.
AI.result: Claude Opus 4.6 leads (94.1%), followed by GPT-5.4 (93.1%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (92.5%). Performance degrades across tiers (2.8-32.5 pp drop), showing property memorization ≠ thermodynamic reasoning. Supercritical water, R-134a, and combined-cycle problems serve as discriminators with 40-60 pp spreads.
AI.conclusion: ThermoQA successfully distinguishes between property memorization and genuine thermodynamic reasoning in LLMs, with significant performance variations across difficulty tiers and specific problem types revealing reasoning limitations.

title: High-Fidelity and Location-Robust Respiratory Waveform Monitoring with Single-Antenna WiFi
comment: 16 pages, 15 figures, accepted by IEEE Internet of Things Journal 2026
summary: In recent years, WiFi sensing has been recognized as a promising technology to bring respiratory monitoring into everyday homes, thanks to its contactless nature and ubiquitous availability. However, existing WiFi-based respiratory monitoring systems still fall short of deployment-oriented performance: they suffer from restrained hardware scalability, limited accuracy, and are highly sensitive to user location. To overcome these limitations and push WiFi sensing towards clinically meaningful precision, we propose RespirFi, a novel system that robustly delivers high-fidelity respiratory waveforms with WiFi Channel State Information (CSI), thereby enabling accurate estimation of key physiological biomarkers. At the core of RespirFi is a theoretical human reflection model, through which we perform an in-depth characterization of how CSI variations are shaped by both subcarrier frequency and spatial user location. Guided by these insights, we develop a location-robust waveform construction method that adaptively selects high quality subcarriers and aligns their waveform trends, ensuring accurate waveform recovery. Furthermore, we propose a breathing phase identification method that leverages inter-subcarrier CSI differences to reliably distinguish inhalation from exhalation. We implement RespirFi over commodity WiFi devices, and extensive experiments demonstrate that it outperforms state-of-the-art approaches across a wide range of clinically relevant respiratory metrics.

AI.tldr: RespirFi is a WiFi-based respiratory monitoring system that uses CSI to deliver high-fidelity respiratory waveforms and accurately estimate physiological biomarkers, overcoming limitations of existing systems in hardware scalability, accuracy, and location sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Existing WiFi-based respiratory monitoring systems have deployment limitations: restrained hardware scalability, limited accuracy, and high sensitivity to user location. The authors aim to push WiFi sensing toward clinically meaningful precision for respiratory monitoring in everyday homes.
AI.method: 1) Develops a theoretical human reflection model to characterize how CSI variations are shaped by subcarrier frequency and spatial user location. 2) Creates a location-robust waveform construction method that adaptively selects high-quality subcarriers and aligns their waveform trends. 3) Proposes a breathing phase identification method using inter-subcarrier CSI differences to distinguish inhalation from exhalation.
AI.result: RespirFi outperforms state-of-the-art approaches across a wide range of clinically relevant respiratory metrics when implemented over commodity WiFi devices in extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: RespirFi represents a significant advancement in WiFi sensing for respiratory monitoring, delivering robust, high-fidelity respiratory waveforms with clinical-grade precision while overcoming key limitations of existing systems.

title: Rate-Cost Tradeoffs in Nonlinear Control
summary: We study the rate-cost tradeoff in rate-limited control of general stochastic control systems, including nonlinear systems, over a finite horizon. At each time step, an encoder observes the state and transmits a description to a controller, which then selects the control action. For an average control-cost threshold $D$, we characterize the minimum achievable communication rate $R_n(D)$ via a nonasymptotic bound: $R_n(D)$ lies within an additive logarithmic gap of the optimal value of a directed-information minimization $F_n(D)$, namely, we show that $F_n(D) \le R_n(D) \le F_n(D)+\log \bigl(F_n(D)+3.4\bigr)+2+\frac{1}{n}$, in bits. This establishes directed information as the operationally relevant quantity governing rate-limited control, thereby broadening its utility beyond its previously established roles in causal source coding and linear quadratic Gaussian (LQG) control to general nonlinear control systems. We prove the upper bound constructively by building an encoding-and-control policy using the strong functional representation lemma at each time step. As special cases of our setting, our framework yields nonasymptotic bounds for sequential (causal) rate-distortion and LQG control.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes nonasymptotic bounds for rate-limited control of general stochastic systems, showing that the minimum communication rate lies within an additive logarithmic gap of a directed-information minimization problem.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental tradeoff between communication rate and control performance in stochastic control systems, particularly extending beyond linear quadratic Gaussian (LQG) control to general nonlinear systems.
AI.method: Characterizes the minimum achievable communication rate R_n(D) via a nonasymptotic bound using directed-information minimization F_n(D). Proves upper bound constructively by building encoding-and-control policies using the strong functional representation lemma at each time step.
AI.result: Shows that F_n(D) ≤ R_n(D) ≤ F_n(D) + log(F_n(D)+3.4) + 2 + 1/n bits, establishing directed information as the operationally relevant quantity for rate-limited control in general nonlinear systems.
AI.conclusion: Directed information governs rate-limited control broadly, extending its utility beyond causal source coding and LQG control to general nonlinear control systems, with the framework providing nonasymptotic bounds for sequential rate-distortion and LQG control as special cases.

title: Cortex 2.0: Grounding World Models in Real-World Industrial Deployment
summary: Industrial robotic manipulation demands reliable long-horizon execution across embodiments, tasks, and changing object distributions. While Vision-Language-Action models have demonstrated strong generalization, they remain fundamentally reactive. By optimizing the next action given the current observation without evaluating potential futures, they are brittle to the compounding failure modes of long-horizon tasks. Cortex 2.0 shifts from reactive control to plan-and-act by generating candidate future trajectories in visual latent space, scoring them for expected success and efficiency, then committing only to the highest-scoring candidate. We evaluate Cortex 2.0 on a single-arm and dual-arm manipulation platform across four tasks of increasing complexity: pick and place, item and trash sorting, screw sorting, and shoebox unpacking. Cortex 2.0 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action baselines, achieving the best results across all tasks. The system remains reliable in unstructured environments characterized by heavy clutter, frequent occlusions, and contact-rich manipulation, where reactive policies fail. These results demonstrate that world-model-based planning can operate reliably in complex industrial environments.

AI.tldr: Cortex 2.0 introduces a plan-and-act approach using visual latent space trajectory planning to overcome reactive limitations of Vision-Language-Action models, achieving superior performance in complex industrial manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Industrial robotic manipulation requires reliable long-horizon execution across various embodiments, tasks, and changing object distributions. Current Vision-Language-Action models are fundamentally reactive, optimizing only the next action without evaluating potential futures, making them brittle to compounding failure modes in long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: Cortex 2.0 shifts from reactive control to plan-and-act by generating candidate future trajectories in visual latent space, scoring them for expected success and efficiency, then committing only to the highest-scoring candidate.
AI.result: Cortex 2.0 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action baselines across four complex tasks: pick and place, item and trash sorting, screw sorting, and shoebox unpacking. It remains reliable in unstructured environments with heavy clutter, frequent occlusions, and contact-rich manipulation where reactive policies fail.
AI.conclusion: World-model-based planning can operate reliably in complex industrial environments, demonstrating that plan-and-act approaches overcome the limitations of reactive Vision-Language-Action models for long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks.

title: SGAP-Gaze: Scene Grid Attention Based Point-of-Gaze Estimation Network for Driver Gaze
summary: Driver gaze estimation is essential for understanding the driver's situational awareness of surrounding traffic. Existing gaze estimation models use driver facial information to predict the Point-of-Gaze (PoG) or the 3D gaze direction vector. We propose a benchmark dataset, Urban Driving-Face Scene Gaze (UD-FSG), comprising synchronized driver-face and traffic-scene images. The scene images provide cues about surrounding traffic, which can help improve the gaze estimation model, along with the face images. We propose SGAP-Gaze, Scene-Grid Attention based Point-of-Gaze estimation network, trained and tested on our UD-FSG dataset, which explicitly incorporates the scene images into the gaze estimation modelling. The gaze estimation network integrates driver face, eye, iris, and scene contextual information. First, the extracted features from facial modalities are fused to form a gaze intent vector. Then, attention scores are computed over the spatial scene grid using a Transformer-based attention mechanism fusing face and scene image features to obtain the PoG. The proposed SGAP-Gaze model achieves a mean pixel error of 104.73 on the UD-FSG dataset and 63.48 on LBW dataset, achieving a 23.5% reduction in mean pixel error compared to state-of-the-art driver gaze estimation models. The spatial pixel distribution analysis shows that SGAP-Gaze consistently achieves lower mean pixel error than existing methods across all spatial ranges, including the outer regions of the scene, which are rare but critical for understanding driver attention. These results highlight the effectiveness of integrating multi-modal gaze cues with scene-aware attention for a robust driver PoG estimation model in real-world driving environments.

AI.tldr: SGAP-Gaze is a driver gaze estimation model that integrates facial features with scene context using Transformer-based attention, achieving 23.5% error reduction over state-of-the-art methods on urban driving datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing driver gaze estimation models rely only on facial information, missing important contextual cues from surrounding traffic scenes. This limits their accuracy in real-world driving environments where scene context provides valuable information about driver attention.
AI.method: Proposes SGAP-Gaze network with two-stage approach: 1) Fuses facial features (face, eye, iris) into gaze intent vector, 2) Uses Transformer-based attention to compute spatial attention scores over scene grid, integrating face and scene features for Point-of-Gaze estimation. Introduces UD-FSG benchmark dataset with synchronized driver-face and traffic-scene images.
AI.result: Achieves mean pixel error of 104.73 on UD-FSG dataset and 63.48 on LBW dataset, representing 23.5% reduction in error compared to state-of-the-art methods. Shows consistent improvement across all spatial ranges, including critical outer regions of the scene.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multi-modal gaze cues with scene-aware attention significantly improves driver gaze estimation robustness in real-world driving environments, demonstrating the value of scene context for understanding driver situational awareness.

title: Rethinking Reinforcement Fine-Tuning in LVLM: Convergence, Reward Decomposition, and Generalization
summary: Reinforcement fine-tuning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for equipping large vision-language models (LVLMs) with agentic capabilities such as tool use and multi-step reasoning. Despite striking empirical successes, most notably Visual Agentic Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (Visual-ARFT), the theoretical underpinnings of this paradigm remain poorly understood. In particular, two critical questions lack rigorous answers: (i)~how does the composite structure of verifiable rewards (format compliance, answer accuracy, tool executability) affect the convergence of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), and (ii)~why does training on a small set of tool-augmented tasks transfer to out-of-distribution domains? We address these gaps by introducing the \emph{Tool-Augmented Markov Decision Process} (TA-MDP), a formal framework that models multimodal agentic decision-making with bounded-depth tool calls. Within this framework, we establish three main results. First, we prove that GRPO under composite verifiable rewards converges to a first-order stationary point at rate $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ with explicit dependence on the number of reward components and group size (\textbf{Theorem~1}). Second, we derive a \emph{Reward Decomposition Theorem} that bounds the sub-optimality gap between decomposed per-component optimization and joint optimization, providing a precise characterization of when reward decomposition is beneficial (\textbf{Theorem~2}). Third, we establish a PAC-Bayes generalization bound for tool-augmented policies that explains the strong out-of-distribution transfer observed in Visual-ARFT (\textbf{Theorem~3}).

AI.tldr: The paper provides theoretical foundations for RLVR (Reinforcement fine-tuning with verifiable rewards) in vision-language models, analyzing convergence, reward decomposition, and generalization in tool-augmented agentic systems.
AI.motivation: Despite empirical success of RLVR approaches like Visual-ARFT, there's a lack of theoretical understanding about how composite verifiable rewards affect convergence and why training on limited tool-augmented tasks transfers to out-of-distribution domains.
AI.method: Introduces Tool-Augmented Markov Decision Process (TA-MDP) framework to model multimodal agentic decision-making with bounded-depth tool calls. Uses this framework to prove convergence rates for GRPO, derive reward decomposition theorems, and establish PAC-Bayes generalization bounds.
AI.result: Three main theoretical results: 1) GRPO converges to first-order stationary point at O(1/√T) rate with explicit dependence on reward components and group size; 2) Reward Decomposition Theorem bounds sub-optimality gap between decomposed and joint optimization; 3) PAC-Bayes generalization bound explains out-of-distribution transfer in Visual-ARFT.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides rigorous theoretical foundations for RLVR, explaining previously observed empirical phenomena and offering formal guarantees for convergence, reward decomposition benefits, and generalization in tool-augmented vision-language agents.

title: Automated Detection of Dosing Errors in Clinical Trial Narratives: A Multi-Modal Feature Engineering Approach with LightGBM
summary: Clinical trials require strict adherence to medication protocols, yet dosing errors remain a persistent challenge affecting patient safety and trial integrity. We present an automated system for detecting dosing errors in unstructured clinical trial narratives using gradient boosting with comprehensive multi-modal feature engineering. Our approach combines 3,451 features spanning traditional NLP (TF-IDF, character n-grams), dense semantic embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6v2), domain-specific medical patterns, and transformer-based scores (BiomedBERT, DeBERTa-v3), used to train a LightGBM model. Features are extracted from nine complementary text fields (median 5,400 characters per sample) ensuring complete coverage across all 42,112 clinical trial narratives. On the CT-DEB benchmark dataset with severe class imbalance (4.9% positive rate), we achieve 0.8725 test ROC-AUC through 5-fold ensemble averaging (cross-validation: 0.8833 + 0.0091 AUC). Systematic ablation studies reveal that removing sentence embeddings causes the largest performance degradation (2.39%), demonstrating their critical role despite contributing only 37.07% of total feature importance. Feature efficiency analysis demonstrates that selecting the top 500-1000 features yields optimal performance (0.886-0.887 AUC), outperforming the full 3,451-feature set (0.879 AUC) through effective noise reduction. Our findings highlight the importance of feature selection as a regularization technique and demonstrate that sparse lexical features remain complementary to dense representations for specialized clinical text classification under severe class imbalance.

AI.tldr: Automated dosing error detection system using LightGBM with 3,451 multi-modal features achieves 0.8725 ROC-AUC on imbalanced clinical trial narratives, showing feature selection improves performance over full feature set.
AI.motivation: Dosing errors in clinical trials compromise patient safety and trial integrity, but detecting them in unstructured narratives is challenging due to severe class imbalance and complex medical language.
AI.method: Uses gradient boosting (LightGBM) with comprehensive feature engineering: 3,451 features from traditional NLP (TF-IDF, n-grams), dense embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6v2), domain-specific medical patterns, and transformer scores (BiomedBERT, DeBERTa-v3) extracted from 9 text fields across 42,112 narratives.
AI.result: Achieves 0.8725 test ROC-AUC on CT-DEB benchmark (4.9% positive rate) via 5-fold ensemble averaging. Feature selection (top 500-1000 features) yields optimal performance (0.886-0.887 AUC), outperforming full feature set (0.879 AUC). Sentence embeddings cause largest performance drop when removed (2.39%).
AI.conclusion: Feature selection acts as effective regularization, and sparse lexical features complement dense representations for clinical text classification under severe imbalance. Multi-modal feature engineering with proper selection enables robust dosing error detection.

title: Rank-Aware Link Adaptation for XR Tethering Groups with Realistic Tethering Link: A Multi-Offset OLLA Framework
summary: We investigate higher-rank transmissions for multi-connected Extended Reality (XR) devices enabled through tethering group (TGr), in which a nearby tethering User Equipment (UE) cooperates with an XR UE via a short-range tethering link (TL). In contrast to prior studies that are limited to rank-1 transmission and ideal tethering assumptions, we analyze TGr performance under higher-rank point-to-multipoint (PTM) transmission and realistic TL delays. Conventional single Outer Loop Link Adaptation (OLLA) offset results in inaccurate throughput prediction across ranks, leading to suboptimal rank selection. To address this limitation, we propose a multi-offset Outer Loop Link Adaptation (MO-OLLA) framework that introduces rank-dependent signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) correction to improve Link Adaptation (LA) accuracy. Furthermore, a Wireless Fidelity (WiFi) based delay model is incorporated to characterize the impact of practical TL constraints including limited bandwidth and achievable throughput on XR capacity and cellular resource utilization, providing the first such analysis for higher-rank multi-connected XR device. System-level simulations demonstrate that MO-OLLA provides up to 20% performance improvement over conventional OLLA for multi-connected XR UEs. Moreover, TGrs effectively exploit higher-rank transmission, achieving XR capacity gains of 180-200% over single-link XR UEs under ideal TL conditions. Critically, the gains of the TGr remain at 165-180% under realistic high-throughput TLs relative to single-link XR UEs, confirming the practical viability of TGr based cooperation for XR capacity enhancements within existing cellular resources.

AI.tldr: Proposes multi-offset OLLA framework for higher-rank multi-connected XR devices with tethering groups, achieving 20% performance improvement over conventional OLLA and 165-180% capacity gains over single-link XR.
AI.motivation: Prior studies on tethering groups for XR devices are limited to rank-1 transmission and ideal tethering assumptions, lacking analysis of higher-rank PTM transmission and realistic tethering link delays.
AI.method: Proposes MO-OLLA framework with rank-dependent SINR correction for improved link adaptation accuracy. Incorporates WiFi-based delay model to characterize practical tethering link constraints including limited bandwidth and achievable throughput.
AI.result: MO-OLLA provides up to 20% performance improvement over conventional OLLA. Tethering groups achieve 180-200% XR capacity gains over single-link XR under ideal conditions, maintaining 165-180% gains under realistic high-throughput tethering links.
AI.conclusion: Tethering group-based cooperation is practically viable for XR capacity enhancements within existing cellular resources, with MO-OLLA effectively addressing rank-dependent link adaptation challenges.

title: CSI Feedback Under Basis Mismatch: Rate-Splitting Transform Coding for FDD Massive MIMO
summary: In frequency division duplex massive multiple-input multiple-output systems, downlink channel state information must be fed back within a limited uplink budget. While transform coding with Karhunen-Loeve transform and reverse water-filling is rate-distortion optimal for Gaussian channels, its performance is limited by basis mismatch between the user and base station. We analyze this mismatch and propose a practical architecture separating long-term basis feedback from short-term coefficient quantization. Using a random vector quantization, we derive a closed-form end-to-end mean square error expression. This allows us to characterize the optimal rate split and identify a phase transition threshold for basis updates. Simulations on correlated Gaussian and COST2100 channels demonstrate near-optimal performance, robustness to update overhead, and significant complexity reduction compared to deep-learning-based autoencoders.

AI.tldr: Proposes a practical architecture for FDD massive MIMO channel feedback that separates long-term basis feedback from short-term coefficient quantization, achieving near-optimal performance with reduced complexity.
AI.motivation: In FDD massive MIMO systems, downlink CSI must be fed back within limited uplink budget. While transform coding with KLT is rate-distortion optimal for Gaussian channels, its performance is limited by basis mismatch between user and base station.
AI.method: Proposes architecture separating long-term basis feedback from short-term coefficient quantization. Uses random vector quantization to derive closed-form end-to-end MSE expression. Characterizes optimal rate split and identifies phase transition threshold for basis updates.
AI.result: Simulations on correlated Gaussian and COST2100 channels demonstrate near-optimal performance, robustness to update overhead, and significant complexity reduction compared to deep-learning-based autoencoders.
AI.conclusion: The proposed practical architecture effectively addresses basis mismatch in FDD massive MIMO channel feedback, achieving near-optimal performance with reduced complexity compared to deep learning approaches.

title: Onboard Wind Estimation for Small UAVs Equipped with Low-Cost Sensors: An Aerodynamic Model-Integrated Filtering Approach
summary: To enable autonomous wind estimation for energy-efficient flight in small unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), this study proposes a method that estimates flight states and wind using only the low-cost essential onboard sensors required for autonomous flight, without relying on additional wind measurement devices. The core of the method includes an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) integrated with the aerodynamic model and an Adaptive Moving Average Estimation (AMAE) technique, which improves the accuracy and smoothness of the wind estimation. Simulation results show that the approach efficiently estimates both steady and time-varying 3D wind vectors without requiring flow angle measurements. The impact of aerodynamic model accuracy on wind estimation errors is also analyzed to assess practical applicability. Flight tests validate the effectiveness of the method and its feasibility for real-time onboard computation. Additionally, uncertainties and error sources encountered during testing are systematically examined, providing a foundation for further refinement.

AI.tldr: Proposes wind estimation method for small UAVs using only essential onboard sensors, combining EKF with aerodynamic model and adaptive moving average for improved accuracy without additional wind measurement devices.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous wind estimation for energy-efficient flight in small UAVs without relying on additional wind measurement devices, using only low-cost essential onboard sensors required for autonomous flight.
AI.method: Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) integrated with aerodynamic model and Adaptive Moving Average Estimation (AMAE) technique to improve accuracy and smoothness of wind estimation, working without flow angle measurements.
AI.result: Simulation shows efficient estimation of both steady and time-varying 3D wind vectors; flight tests validate effectiveness and feasibility for real-time onboard computation; analyzes impact of aerodynamic model accuracy on errors.
AI.conclusion: Method provides practical wind estimation for small UAVs using only essential sensors, with systematic examination of uncertainties and error sources providing foundation for further refinement.

title: MMCORE: MultiModal COnnection with Representation Aligned Latent Embeddings
summary:
We present MMCORE, a unified framework designed for multimodal image generation and editing. MMCORE leverages a pre-trained Vision-Language Model (VLM) to predict semantic visual embeddings via learnable query tokens, which subsequently serve as conditioning signals for a diffusion model. This streamlined design effectively transfers the rich understanding and reasoning capabilities of VLMs into the visual generation process. By obviating the need for deep fusion between autoregressive and diffusion models or training from scratch, MMCORE significantly reduces computational overhead while maintaining high-fidelity synthesis.
  MMCORE seamlessly integrates text-to-image synthesis with interleaved image generation, demonstrating robust multimodal comprehension in complex scenarios such as spatial reasoning and visual grounding. Comprehensive evaluations indicate that MMCORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across a broad spectrum of text-to-image and single/multi-image editing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MMCORE is a unified framework for multimodal image generation and editing that uses a pre-trained VLM to predict semantic visual embeddings, which then condition a diffusion model, enabling efficient transfer of VLM reasoning capabilities to visual synthesis.
AI.motivation: To create an efficient multimodal image generation and editing framework that leverages the rich understanding and reasoning capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) without the computational overhead of deep fusion between autoregressive and diffusion models or training from scratch.
AI.method: MMCORE uses a pre-trained VLM to predict semantic visual embeddings via learnable query tokens, which serve as conditioning signals for a diffusion model. This streamlined design transfers VLM capabilities into visual generation while avoiding complex model fusion.
AI.result: MMCORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across a broad spectrum of text-to-image and single/multi-image editing benchmarks, demonstrating robust multimodal comprehension in complex scenarios like spatial reasoning and visual grounding.
AI.conclusion: MMCORE provides an efficient unified framework for multimodal image generation and editing that successfully transfers VLM reasoning capabilities to visual synthesis while significantly reducing computational overhead compared to existing approaches.

title: DR-Venus: Towards Frontier Edge-Scale Deep Research Agents with Only 10K Open Data
summary: Edge-scale deep research agents based on small language models are attractive for real-world deployment due to their advantages in cost, latency, and privacy. In this work, we study how to train a strong small deep research agent under limited open-data by improving both data quality and data utilization. We present DR-Venus, a frontier 4B deep research agent for edge-scale deployment, built entirely on open data. Our training recipe consists of two stages. In the first stage, we use agentic supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to establish basic agentic capability, combining strict data cleaning with resampling of long-horizon trajectories to improve data quality and utilization. In the second stage, we apply agentic reinforcement learning (RL) to further improve execution reliability on long-horizon deep research tasks. To make RL effective for small agents in this setting, we build on IGPO and design turn-level rewards based on information gain and format-aware regularization, thereby enhancing supervision density and turn-level credit assignment. Built entirely on roughly 10K open-data, DR-Venus-4B significantly outperforms prior agentic models under 9B parameters on multiple deep research benchmarks, while also narrowing the gap to much larger 30B-class systems. Our further analysis shows that 4B agents already possess surprisingly strong performance potential, highlighting both the deployment promise of small models and the value of test-time scaling in this setting. We release our models, code, and key recipes to support reproducible research on edge-scale deep research agents.

AI.tldr: DR-Venus is a 4B parameter deep research agent for edge deployment, trained on only 10K open data samples using a two-stage approach combining agentic SFT and RL with turn-level rewards, achieving strong performance comparable to larger models.
AI.motivation: Edge-scale deep research agents based on small language models are attractive for real-world deployment due to advantages in cost, latency, and privacy, but training strong small agents under limited open-data availability is challenging.
AI.method: Two-stage training: 1) Agentic supervised fine-tuning with strict data cleaning and resampling of long-horizon trajectories; 2) Agentic reinforcement learning with turn-level rewards based on information gain and format-aware regularization to enhance supervision density and credit assignment.
AI.result: DR-Venus-4B significantly outperforms prior agentic models under 9B parameters on multiple deep research benchmarks and narrows the gap to much larger 30B-class systems, demonstrating surprising performance potential in 4B agents.
AI.conclusion: Small 4B agents already possess strong performance potential, highlighting the deployment promise of edge-scale models and the value of test-time scaling, with models, code, and recipes released for reproducible research.

title: Inference Headroom Ratio: A Diagnostic and Control Framework for Inference Stability Under Constraint
comment: Resubmission with revisions addressing moderator concerns regarding distinction from signal-to-noise metrics and structural dependence in simulation design. See updated Section 4.4 for clarification
summary: We present a simulation-based evaluation of the Inference Headroom Ratio (IHR), a dimensionless diagnostic quantity for characterizing inference stability in constrained decision systems. IHR formalizes the relationship between a system's effective inferential capacity C and the combined uncertainty and constraint load U + K imposed by its operating environment, and is intended to capture proximity to an inference stability boundary rather than output-level performance. Across three controlled experiments, we show that IHR functions as: (1) a quantifiable risk indicator whose relationship to collapse probability follows a well-fitted logistic curve with estimated critical threshold IHR* approx. 1.19, (2) a sensitive indicator of proximity to the inference stability boundary under environmental noise, and (3) a viable control variable whose active regulation reduces system collapse rate from 79.4% to 58.7% and IHR variance by 70.4% across 300 Monte Carlo runs. These results position IHR as a prospective, system-level complement to standard performance, drift, and uncertainty metrics, enabling estimation of remaining inferential margin before overt failure in AI systems operating under distributional shift and constraint.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Inference Headroom Ratio (IHR) as a diagnostic metric for assessing inference stability in constrained AI systems, showing it predicts collapse risk and can be used for active control.
AI.motivation: Current metrics focus on output-level performance, drift, and uncertainty, but lack a system-level diagnostic for inference stability that quantifies proximity to collapse boundaries in constrained decision systems operating under distributional shift.
AI.method: Developed IHR as a dimensionless ratio of system's inferential capacity to combined uncertainty and constraint load. Conducted three controlled simulation experiments: 1) quantifying IHR's relationship to collapse probability via logistic curve fitting, 2) testing sensitivity to environmental noise, and 3) implementing IHR as a control variable in Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.result: IHR functions as: 1) quantifiable risk indicator with critical threshold IHR* ≈ 1.19, 2) sensitive indicator of proximity to inference stability boundary under noise, and 3) viable control variable reducing collapse rate from 79.4% to 58.7% and IHR variance by 70.4% across 300 Monte Carlo runs.
AI.conclusion: IHR serves as a system-level complement to traditional metrics, enabling estimation of remaining inferential margin before overt failure in AI systems operating under distributional shift and constraints.

title: Fundamental Tradeoff in Movable Antenna Systems: How Long to Move Before Transmission?
summary: The movable antenna (MA) technology enables flexible reconfiguration of wireless channels through adaptive antenna deployment, offering significant potential for enhancing communication performance. However, antenna movement requires a certain duration within which communication may be compromised due to factors such as channel fluctuation and Doppler effect. This leads to a fundamental tradeoff: A longer movement duration allows antennas to reach more favorable positions for better channel conditions, but it inevitably reduces the time available for data transmission. To characterize the aforementioned tradeoff, we focus on the MAs-enabled multiuser downlink scenario, and jointly optimize the movement duration and antenna deployment at the base station to maximize the effective throughput. The formulated problem is highly non-convex. The general solutions require an one-dimensional search over movement durations, each with optimized antenna deployment. To reduce complexity, we propose a fitting method that samples only a few rate-duration pairs, yielding a closed-form expression that captures the rate trend and enables a favorable solution immediately. We further derive a closed-form condition on the maximum antenna movement speed: When the speed is below a certain threshold, the optimal strategy is to keep antennas stationary throughout the transmission period. The fundamental tradeoff and the effectiveness of the proposed solutions are examined in a special case with two MAs and two users. Finally, numerical simulations validate the efficacy of the proposed schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the tradeoff between antenna movement duration and data transmission time in movable antenna (MA) systems, proposing optimization methods to maximize effective throughput in multiuser downlink scenarios.
AI.motivation: Movable antenna technology offers channel reconfiguration benefits but requires movement time that reduces data transmission time, creating a fundamental tradeoff between achieving better channel conditions and maximizing transmission opportunities.
AI.method: The authors formulate a joint optimization problem for movement duration and antenna deployment, propose a fitting method that samples rate-duration pairs for closed-form solutions, and derive a condition on maximum antenna movement speed for stationary operation.
AI.result: The paper provides optimization frameworks for MA systems, including a low-complexity fitting method and a threshold condition for antenna movement speed, validated through numerical simulations showing the proposed schemes' efficacy.
AI.conclusion: There exists a fundamental tradeoff between antenna movement and data transmission in MA systems, and the proposed optimization methods effectively balance this tradeoff to maximize throughput, with practical speed thresholds determining when stationary operation is optimal.

title: ETac: A Lightweight and Efficient Tactile Simulation Framework for Learning Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Tactile sensors are increasingly integrated into dexterous robotic manipulators to enhance contact perception. However, learning manipulation policies that rely on tactile sensing remains challenging, primarily due to the trade-off between fidelity and computational cost of soft-body simulations. To address this, we present ETac, a tactile simulation framework that models elastomeric soft-body interactions with both high fidelity and efficiency. ETac employs a lightweight data-driven deformation propagation model to capture soft-body contact dynamics, achieving high simulation quality and boosting efficiency that enables large-scale policy training. When serving as the simulation backend, ETac produces surface deformation estimates comparable to FEM and demonstrates applicability for modeling real tactile sensors. Then, we showcase its capability in training a blind grasping policy that leverages large-area tactile feedback to manipulate diverse objects. Running on a single RTX 4090 GPU, ETac supports reinforcement learning across 4,096 parallel environments, achieving a total throughput of 869 FPS. The resulting policy reaches an average success rate of 84.45% across four object types, underscoring ETac's potential to make tactile-based skill learning both efficient and scalable.

AI.tldr: ETac is a high-fidelity, efficient tactile simulation framework for soft-body interactions that enables large-scale policy training for tactile-based robotic manipulation.
AI.motivation: There's a trade-off between fidelity and computational cost in soft-body simulations for tactile sensing, making it challenging to learn manipulation policies that rely on tactile feedback.
AI.method: ETac uses a lightweight data-driven deformation propagation model to capture soft-body contact dynamics, achieving high simulation quality while maintaining computational efficiency for large-scale training.
AI.result: ETac produces surface deformation estimates comparable to FEM, supports 4,096 parallel environments with 869 FPS throughput on a single RTX 4090 GPU, and enables a blind grasping policy achieving 84.45% success rate across four object types.
AI.conclusion: ETac makes tactile-based skill learning both efficient and scalable, demonstrating potential for advancing tactile perception in robotic manipulation.

title: SceneOrchestra: Efficient Agentic 3D Scene Synthesis via Full Tool-Call Trajectory Generation
summary: Recent agentic frameworks for 3D scene synthesis have advanced realism and diversity by integrating heterogeneous generation and editing tools. These tools are organized into workflows orchestrated by an off-the-shelf LLM. Current approaches typically adopt an execute-review-reflect loop: at each step, the orchestrator executes a tool, renders intermediate results for review, and then decides on the tool and its parameters for the next step. However, this design has two key limitations. First, next-step tool selection and parameter configuration are driven by heuristic rules, which can lead to suboptimal execution flows, unnecessary tool invocations, degraded output quality, and increased runtime. Second, rendering and reviewing intermediate results after each step introduces additional latency. To address these issues, we propose SceneOrchestra, a trainable orchestration framework that optimizes the tool-call execution flow and eliminates the step-by-step review loop, improving both efficiency and output quality. SceneOrchestra consists of an orchestrator and a discriminator, which we fine-tune with a two-phase training strategy. In the first phase, the orchestrator learns context-aware tool selection and complete tool-call trajectory generation, while the discriminator is trained to assess the quality of full trajectories, enabling it to select the best trajectory from multiple candidates. In the second phase, we perform interleaved training, where the discriminator adapts to the orchestrator's evolving trajectory distribution and distills its discriminative capability back into the orchestrator. At inference, we only use the orchestrator to generate and execute full tool-call trajectories from instructions, without requiring the discriminator. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art scene quality while reducing runtime compared to previous work.

AI.tldr: SceneOrchestra: A trainable orchestration framework that optimizes 3D scene synthesis workflows by learning context-aware tool selection and generating complete tool-call trajectories, eliminating the inefficient step-by-step review loop used in current methods.
AI.motivation: Current agentic frameworks for 3D scene synthesis use heuristic-based tool selection and require rendering intermediate results after each step, leading to suboptimal execution flows, unnecessary tool invocations, degraded quality, and increased runtime.
AI.method: SceneOrchestra consists of an orchestrator and discriminator trained with a two-phase strategy: Phase 1 trains the orchestrator for context-aware tool selection and full trajectory generation, while the discriminator learns to assess trajectory quality. Phase 2 uses interleaved training where the discriminator adapts to the orchestrator's evolving distribution and distills its discriminative capability back into the orchestrator.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art scene quality while reducing runtime compared to previous work. At inference, only the orchestrator is needed to generate and execute full tool-call trajectories without the discriminator.
AI.conclusion: SceneOrchestra addresses key limitations of current approaches by optimizing tool-call execution flows and eliminating the inefficient step-by-step review loop, improving both efficiency and output quality in 3D scene synthesis.

title: Super Apriel: One Checkpoint, Many Speeds
