comment: Models: https://huggingface.co/ServiceNow-AI/SuperApriel-15B-Base and https://huggingface.co/ServiceNow-AI/SuperApriel-15B-Instruct . Dev model: https://huggingface.co/ServiceNow-AI/SuperApriel-0.5B-Base . Training code: https://github.com/ServiceNow/Fast-LLM . Async RL: https://github.com/ServiceNow/pipeline-rl . Training logs: https://wandb.ai/servicenow-team/Super_Apriel
summary: We release Super Apriel, a 15B-parameter supernet in which every decoder layer provides four trained mixer choices -- Full Attention (FA), Sliding Window Attention (SWA), Kimi Delta Attention (KDA), and Gated DeltaNet (GDN). A placement selects one mixer per layer; placements can be switched between requests at serving time without reloading weights, enabling multiple speed presets from a single checkpoint. The shared checkpoint also enables speculative decoding without a separate draft model. The all-FA preset matches the Apriel 1.6 teacher on all reported benchmarks; recommended hybrid presets span $2.9\times$ to $10.7\times$ decode throughput at 96% to 77% quality retention, with throughput advantages that compound at longer context lengths. With four mixer types across 48 layers, the configuration space is vast. A surrogate that predicts placement quality from the per-layer mixer assignment makes the speed-quality landscape tractable and identifies the best tradeoffs at each speed level. We investigate whether the best configurations at each speed level can be identified early in training or only after convergence. Rankings stabilize quickly at 0.5B scale, but the most efficient configurations exhibit higher instability at 15B, cautioning against extrapolation from smaller models. Super Apriel is trained by stochastic distillation from a frozen Apriel 1.6 teacher, followed by supervised fine-tuning. We release the supernet weights, Fast-LLM training code, vLLM serving code, and a placement optimization toolkit.

AI.tldr: Super Apriel is a 15B-parameter supernet with four trained mixer choices per layer (FA, SWA, KDA, GDN) that enables dynamic placement switching at serving time for multiple speed presets from a single checkpoint, plus speculative decoding without separate draft models.
AI.motivation: To create a single model checkpoint that can serve multiple speed-quality tradeoffs dynamically at inference time, eliminating the need for separate specialized models and enabling flexible deployment across different latency/quality requirements.
AI.method: Train a 15B supernet with four mixer types per decoder layer, use surrogate modeling to predict placement quality, employ stochastic distillation from frozen Apriel 1.6 teacher, followed by supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: All-FA preset matches teacher performance; hybrid presets achieve 2.9× to 10.7× decode throughput with 96% to 77% quality retention. Throughput advantages compound at longer contexts. Placement rankings stabilize quickly at 0.5B but show instability at 15B scale.
AI.conclusion: Super Apriel demonstrates efficient multi-speed inference from a single checkpoint, with practical tools for placement optimization and serving, though caution is needed when extrapolating small-scale findings to larger models.

title: EvoForest: A Novel Machine-Learning Paradigm via Open-Ended Evolution of Computational Graphs
summary: Modern machine learning is still largely organized around a single recipe: choose a parameterized model family and optimize its weights. Although highly successful, this paradigm is too narrow for many structured prediction problems, where the main bottleneck is not parameter fitting but discovering what should be computed from the data. Success often depends on identifying the right transformations, statistics, invariances, interaction structures, temporal summaries, gates, or nonlinear compositions, especially when objectives are non-differentiable, evaluation is cross-validation-based, interpretability matters, or continual adaptation is required. We present EvoForest, a hybrid neuro-symbolic system for end-to-end open-ended evolution of computation. Rather than merely generating features, EvoForest jointly evolves reusable computational structure, callable function families, and trainable low-dimensional continuous components inside a shared directed acyclic graph. Intermediate nodes store alternative implementations, callable nodes encode reusable transformation families such as projections, gates, and activations, output nodes define candidate predictive computations, and persistent global parameters can be refined by gradient descent. For each graph configuration, EvoForest evaluates the discovered computation and uses a lightweight Ridge-based readout to score the resulting representation against a non-differentiable cross-validation target. The evaluator also produces structured feedback that guides future LLM-driven mutations. In the 2025 ADIA Lab Structural Break Challenge, EvoForest reached 94.13% ROC-AUC after 600 evolution steps, exceeding the publicly reported winning score of 90.14% under the same evaluation protocol.

AI.tldr: EvoForest is a neuro-symbolic system that evolves computational structures, function families, and continuous components in a graph to solve structured prediction problems where discovering what to compute matters more than parameter optimization.
AI.motivation: Traditional ML focuses on optimizing model weights, but this is insufficient for structured prediction where the main challenge is discovering the right computational transformations, statistics, invariances, and structures, especially with non-differentiable objectives, cross-validation evaluation, interpretability needs, or continual adaptation requirements.
AI.method: EvoForest evolves reusable computational structure, callable function families, and trainable continuous components in a shared directed acyclic graph. It uses intermediate nodes for alternative implementations, callable nodes for reusable transformations, output nodes for predictive computations, and global parameters refined by gradient descent. The system evaluates each graph configuration with Ridge-based readout against non-differentiable cross-validation targets and uses structured feedback to guide LLM-driven mutations.
AI.result: In the 2025 ADIA Lab Structural Break Challenge, EvoForest achieved 94.13% ROC-AUC after 600 evolution steps, surpassing the publicly reported winning score of 90.14% under the same evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: EvoForest demonstrates that open-ended evolution of computational structure can effectively address complex structured prediction problems where traditional parameter optimization approaches are insufficient, achieving state-of-the-art performance on challenging real-world tasks.

title: Near-Field Wideband Channel Estimation for XL-MIMO Systems via Denoising Diffusion Model
summary: Extremely large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (XL-MIMO) is a key enabling technology for sixth-generation (6G) communication systems. Nevertheless, the increase in array aperture and signal bandwidth brings new challenges to wideband channel estimation in XL-MIMO systems. Motivated by recent advances in deep generative modeling, we propose a diffusion model-based method for near-field wideband channel estimation in XL-MIMO systems. We first analyze the statistical correlation of wideband channel and show that near-field wideband channel exhibits both spatial non-stationarity and beam split effects. Based on these observations, the channel estimation problem is formulated as a Bayesian posterior inference task, in which a diffusion model is employed to learn the prior distribution of the channel. To further enhance the representation of complex spatial-frequency channel structures, we design a denoising network with a multi-scale attention mechanism. In particular, the network extracts multi-scale spatial-frequency features via parallel convolutional branches with different receptive fields, and combines feature attention and spatial attention modules to adaptively emphasize critical channel features. This design enables more accurate modeling of near-field wideband channel distributions and consequently improves channel estimation performance. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits superior robustness to existing baseline schemes for XL-MIMO wideband channel estimation under different experimental settings.

AI.tldr: Proposes a diffusion model-based method for near-field wideband channel estimation in XL-MIMO systems, addressing spatial non-stationarity and beam split effects through a multi-scale attention denoising network.
AI.motivation: XL-MIMO is key for 6G, but increased array aperture and bandwidth create challenges for wideband channel estimation. Near-field wideband channels exhibit spatial non-stationarity and beam split effects that need to be addressed.
AI.method: Formulates channel estimation as Bayesian posterior inference using a diffusion model to learn channel prior distribution. Designs a denoising network with multi-scale attention mechanism: extracts multi-scale spatial-frequency features via parallel convolutional branches with different receptive fields, and combines feature attention and spatial attention modules to emphasize critical channel features.
AI.result: The proposed method demonstrates superior robustness compared to existing baseline schemes for XL-MIMO wideband channel estimation under different experimental settings.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion model-based approach with multi-scale attention effectively addresses near-field wideband channel estimation challenges in XL-MIMO systems, outperforming existing methods through better modeling of complex spatial-frequency channel structures.

title: A New Paradigm Towards Reconfigurable Environment: Reconfigurable Distributed Antennas and Reflecting Surface
comment: 12 pages, 9 figures. This manuscript has been accepted by Journal of Communications and Information Networks
summary: Reconfigurable distributed antennas and reflecting surface (RDARS) has emerged as a transformative solution to address the stringent requirements of future wireless networks. By combining distributed active antennas with reconfigurable passive reflecting surfaces, RDARS integrates the advantages of both active transmission and passive wave control in a cost-effective and energy-efficient manner. This hybrid architecture enables enhanced coverage, improved spectral efficiency, and seamless support for integrated communication and sensing. In this article, we first introduce the fundamental architecture and working principles of RDARS, followed by practical benefits and comparisons with recently proposed intelligent surface variants. We then highlight the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gains in representative applications of RDARS-aided communication and sensing scenarios, where RDARS demonstrates clear advantages over conventional reconfigurable intelligent surfaces. Finally, we outline key challenges related to practical implementation and resource allocation, and discuss potential research directions. With its unique hybrid mode synergy, RDARS is envisioned to play a pivotal role in shaping the evolution of next-generation intelligent communication systems.

AI.tldr: RDARS combines distributed active antennas with reconfigurable passive reflecting surfaces to create a hybrid architecture that enhances wireless network performance through both active transmission and passive wave control.
AI.motivation: Future wireless networks require enhanced coverage, improved spectral efficiency, and integrated communication/sensing capabilities. Existing solutions like conventional reconfigurable intelligent surfaces have limitations that RDARS aims to overcome through its hybrid architecture.
AI.method: RDARS integrates distributed active antennas with reconfigurable passive reflecting surfaces, creating a hybrid system that combines active transmission capabilities with passive wave control. This enables both signal transmission and reflection/refraction control in a single platform.
AI.result: RDARS demonstrates clear SNR gains over conventional reconfigurable intelligent surfaces in communication and sensing applications. The hybrid architecture provides enhanced coverage, improved spectral efficiency, and seamless support for integrated communication and sensing.
AI.conclusion: RDARS represents a transformative solution for future wireless networks with its unique hybrid mode synergy. While practical implementation and resource allocation challenges remain, RDARS is poised to play a pivotal role in shaping next-generation intelligent communication systems.

title: AdaTracker: Learning Adaptive In-Context Policy for Cross-Embodiment Active Visual Tracking
summary: Realizing active visual tracking with a single unified model across diverse robots is challenging, as the physical constraints and motion dynamics vary drastically from one platform to another. Existing approaches typically train separate models for each embodiment, leading to poor scalability and limited generalization. To address this, we propose AdaTracker, an adaptive in-context policy learning framework that robustly tracks targets on diverse robot morphologies. Our key insight is to explicitly model embodiment-specific constraints through an Embodiment Context Encoder, which infers embodiment-specific constraints from history. This contextual representation dynamically modulates a Context-Aware Policy, enabling it to infer optimal control actions for unseen embodiments in a zero-shot manner. To enhance robustness, we introduce two auxiliary objectives to ensure accurate context identification and temporal consistency. Experiments in both simulation and the real world demonstrate that AdaTracker significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in cross-embodiment generalization, sample efficiency, and zero-shot adaptation.

AI.tldr: AdaTracker: Adaptive in-context policy learning framework for visual tracking across diverse robot embodiments using embodiment context encoding for zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Current approaches require separate models for each robot embodiment due to varying physical constraints and motion dynamics, leading to poor scalability and limited generalization. There's a need for a unified model that can work across diverse robot platforms.
AI.method: Proposes AdaTracker with an Embodiment Context Encoder that infers embodiment-specific constraints from history, and a Context-Aware Policy that uses this contextual representation to dynamically modulate control actions. Includes two auxiliary objectives for accurate context identification and temporal consistency.
AI.result: AdaTracker significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in cross-embodiment generalization, sample efficiency, and zero-shot adaptation in both simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables robust visual tracking across diverse robot morphologies through adaptive in-context learning, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization capabilities without requiring separate training for each embodiment.

title: UniCon3R: Contact-aware 3D Human-Scene Reconstruction from Monocular Video
summary: We introduce UniCon3R (Unified Contact-aware 3D Reconstruction), a unified feed-forward framework for online human-scene 4D reconstruction from monocular videos. Recent feed-forward methods enable real-time world-coordinate human motion and scene reconstruction, but they often produce physically implausible artifacts such as bodies floating above the ground or penetrating parts of the scene. The key reason is that existing approaches fail to model physical interactions between the human and the environment. A natural next step is to predict human-scene contact as an auxiliary output -- yet we find this alone is not sufficient: contact must actively correct the reconstruction. To address this, we explicitly model interaction by inferring 3D contact from the human pose and scene geometry and use the contact as a corrective cue for generating the final pose. This enables UniCon3R to jointly recover high-fidelity scene geometry and spatially aligned 3D humans within the scene. Experiments on standard human-centric video benchmarks such as RICH, EMDB, 3DPW and SLOPER4D show that UniCon3R outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on physical plausibility and global human motion estimation while achieving real-time online inference. We experimentally demonstrate that contact serves as a powerful internal prior rather than just an external metric, thus establishing a new paradigm for physically grounded joint human-scene reconstruction. Project page is available at https://surtantheta.github.io/UniCon3R .

AI.tldr: UniCon3R is a unified feed-forward framework for real-time 4D human-scene reconstruction from monocular videos that uses 3D contact prediction to correct physically implausible artifacts in human-scene interactions.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward methods for human motion and scene reconstruction produce physically implausible artifacts like floating bodies or scene penetrations because they fail to model physical interactions between humans and environments. Simply predicting contact as an auxiliary output isn't sufficient - contact must actively correct the reconstruction.
AI.method: UniCon3R explicitly models human-scene interaction by inferring 3D contact from human pose and scene geometry, then uses this contact information as a corrective cue for generating the final pose. This enables joint recovery of high-fidelity scene geometry and spatially aligned 3D humans within the scene.
AI.result: Experiments on RICH, EMDB, 3DPW and SLOPER4D benchmarks show UniCon3R outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on physical plausibility and global human motion estimation while achieving real-time online inference. Contact serves as a powerful internal prior rather than just an external metric.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a new paradigm for physically grounded joint human-scene reconstruction by demonstrating that contact prediction can actively correct reconstruction artifacts, leading to more physically plausible results in real-time 4D reconstruction from monocular videos.

title: A Multi-Plant Machine Learning Framework for Emission Prediction, Forecasting, and Control in Cement Manufacturing
summary: Cement production is among the largest contributors to industrial air pollution, emitting ~3 Mt NOx/year. The industry-standard mitigation approach, selective non-catalytic reduction (SNCR), exhibits low NH3 utilization efficiency, resulting in operational inefficiencies and increased reagent costs. Here, we develop a data-driven framework for emission control using large-scale operational data from four cement plants worldwide. Benchmarking nine machine learning architectures, we observe that prediction error varies ~3-5x across plants due to variation in data richness. Incorporating short-term process history nearly triples NOx prediction accuracy, revealing that NOx formation carries substantial process memory, a timescale dependence that is absent in CO and CO2. Further, we develop models that forecast NOx overshoots as early as nine minutes, providing a buffer for operational adjustments. The developed framework controls NOx formation at the source, reducing NH3 consumption in downstream SNCR. Surrogate model projections estimate a ~34-64% reduction in NOx while preserving clinker quality, corresponding to a reduction of ~290 t NOx/year and ~58,000 USD/year in NH3 savings. This work establishes a generalizable framework for data-driven emission control, offering a pathway toward low-emission operation without structural modifications or additional hardware, with potential applicability to other hard-to-abate industries such as steel, glass, and lime.

AI.tldr: Data-driven framework for cement plant NOx emission control using machine learning on operational data, achieving 34-64% NOx reduction and significant cost savings without hardware modifications.
AI.motivation: Cement production is a major industrial air polluter (~3 Mt NOx/year). Current SNCR technology has low NH3 utilization efficiency, leading to operational inefficiencies and high reagent costs. There's a need for better emission control methods.
AI.method: Developed data-driven framework using large-scale operational data from four cement plants worldwide. Benchmarked nine machine learning architectures. Incorporated short-term process history to capture NOx formation dynamics. Created models that forecast NOx overshoots up to nine minutes in advance.
AI.result: Prediction error varied 3-5x across plants due to data richness differences. Incorporating process history nearly tripled NOx prediction accuracy. Models forecast NOx overshoots 9 minutes ahead. Surrogate model projections estimate 34-64% NOx reduction while preserving clinker quality, saving ~290 t NOx/year and ~$58,000/year in NH3 costs.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a generalizable framework for data-driven emission control that reduces NOx at the source, improving downstream SNCR efficiency. Offers pathway to low-emission operation without structural modifications, with potential applicability to other hard-to-abate industries like steel, glass, and lime.

title: From Actions to Understanding: Conformal Interpretability of Temporal Concepts in LLM Agents
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents capable of reasoning, planning, and acting within interactive environments. Despite their growing capability to perform multi-step reasoning and decision-making tasks, internal mechanisms guiding their sequential behavior remain opaque. This paper presents a framework for interpreting the temporal evolution of concepts in LLM agents through a step-wise conformal lens. We introduce the conformal interpretability framework for temporal tasks, which combines step-wise reward modeling with conformal prediction to statistically label model's internal representation at each step as successful or failing. Linear probes are then trained on these representations to identify directions of temporal concepts - latent directions in the model's activation space that correspond to consistent notions of success, failure or reasoning drift. Experimental results on two simulated interactive environments, namely ScienceWorld and AlfWorld, demonstrate that these temporal concepts are linearly separable, revealing interpretable structures aligned with task success. We further show preliminary results on improving an LLM agent's performance by leveraging the proposed framework for steering the identified successful directions inside the model. The proposed approach, thus, offers a principled method for early failure detection as well as intervention in LLM-based agents, paving the path towards trustworthy autonomous language models in complex interactive settings.

AI.tldr: A framework for interpreting temporal concept evolution in LLM agents using step-wise conformal prediction to identify success/failure directions in activation space, enabling early failure detection and performance improvement.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for reasoning and decision-making in interactive environments, but their internal mechanisms guiding sequential behavior remain opaque. There's a need to understand how concepts evolve temporally during agent execution to enable trustworthy autonomous systems.
AI.method: Proposes a conformal interpretability framework combining step-wise reward modeling with conformal prediction to statistically label model's internal representations as successful or failing at each step. Linear probes are then trained on these representations to identify "temporal concepts" - latent directions in activation space corresponding to consistent notions of success, failure, or reasoning drift.
AI.result: Experimental results on ScienceWorld and AlfWorld environments show that temporal concepts are linearly separable, revealing interpretable structures aligned with task success. Preliminary results demonstrate improved LLM agent performance by steering identified successful directions inside the model.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a principled method for early failure detection and intervention in LLM-based agents, paving the way toward trustworthy autonomous language models in complex interactive settings by making their temporal reasoning processes more interpretable and controllable.

title: Hiding Secrets in the CSI Quotient: A Robust Wi-Fi CSI Steganography System
summary: Physical layer (PHY) steganography conceals secrets by making subtle modifications to transmitted radio waveforms, which can be applied to establish covert communication systems. Given the widespread deployment of Wi-Fi infrastructures, hiding secrets within Wi-Fi transmissions exhibits significant covertness and has attracted increasing research attention. Recent advances in Wi-Fi steganography have focused on embedding secrets within channel state information (CSI) by applying artificial finite impulse response (FIR) filters to outgoing signals. These methods can emulate natural wireless propagation effects, thereby evading detection by eavesdroppers. However, existing CSI-based approaches suffer from two critical limitations: vulnerability to environmental variations and limited steganographic capacity. This work presents a Wi-Fi steganography system that mitigates these constraints. Specifically, we introduce a CSI division mechanism to decouple artificial CSI components from natural wireless channel responses. In essence, secrets are embedded within the quotient of two consecutive CSI measurements. Furthermore, we propose an encoder-decoder neural network framework that automatically learns optimal strategies for FIR filter generation and secret recovery, substantially enhancing steganographic capacity. We implemented a prototype using commercial off-the-shelf hardware, including a software-defined radio (SDR) transmitter and two receiver platforms: ANTSDR and ESP32. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the system achieves robust performance under dynamic environmental conditions while significantly improving steganographic capacity.

AI.tldr: Wi-Fi steganography system that embeds secrets in CSI using neural networks and CSI division mechanism for improved robustness and capacity.
AI.motivation: Existing CSI-based Wi-Fi steganography methods are vulnerable to environmental variations and have limited steganographic capacity, limiting their practical deployment.
AI.method: 1) CSI division mechanism to decouple artificial CSI components from natural channel responses by embedding secrets in the quotient of consecutive CSI measurements. 2) Encoder-decoder neural network framework that automatically learns optimal FIR filter generation and secret recovery strategies.
AI.result: Implemented prototype using commercial hardware (SDR transmitter, ANTSDR, ESP32 receivers). Achieves robust performance under dynamic environmental conditions while significantly improving steganographic capacity compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system overcomes key limitations of existing CSI-based Wi-Fi steganography methods, providing both environmental robustness and enhanced steganographic capacity through innovative CSI division and neural network optimization.

title: Improved Chase-Pyndiah Decoding for Product Codes with Scaled Messages
summary: We propose an enhanced Chase-Pyndiah decoder that scales extrinsic messages based on decoder confidence of the component decoder, achieving a 0.1 dB gain over the original with negligible complexity increase.

AI.tldr: Enhanced Chase-Pyndiah decoder with confidence-based extrinsic message scaling achieves 0.1 dB gain over original with minimal complexity overhead.
AI.motivation: To improve the performance of Chase-Pyndiah decoders for error correction codes by better utilizing decoder confidence information during the iterative decoding process.
AI.method: Proposes scaling extrinsic messages based on the component decoder's confidence level, enhancing the original Chase-Pyndiah decoder algorithm.
AI.result: Achieves 0.1 dB performance gain over the original Chase-Pyndiah decoder with negligible increase in computational complexity.
AI.conclusion: The confidence-based extrinsic message scaling technique provides a simple yet effective enhancement to Chase-Pyndiah decoders, offering performance improvement with minimal complexity penalty.

title: A Vision-Language-Action Model for Adaptive Ultrasound-Guided Needle Insertion and Needle Tracking
summary: Ultrasound (US)-guided needle insertion is a critical yet challenging procedure due to dynamic imaging conditions and difficulties in needle visualization. Many methods have been proposed for automated needle insertion, but they often rely on hand-crafted pipelines with modular controllers, whose performance degrades in challenging cases. In this paper, a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model is proposed for adaptive and automated US-guided needle insertion and tracking on a robotic ultrasound (RUS) system. This framework provides a unified approach to needle tracking and needle insertion control, enabling real-time, dynamically adaptive adjustment of insertion based on the obtained needle position and environment awareness. To achieve real-time and end-to-end tracking, a Cross-Depth Fusion (CDF) tracking head is proposed, integrating shallow positional and deep semantic features from the large-scale vision backbone. To adapt the pretrained vision backbone for tracking tasks, a Tracking-Conditioning (TraCon) register is introduced for parameter-efficient feature conditioning. After needle tracking, an uncertainty-aware control policy and an asynchronous VLA pipeline are presented for adaptive needle insertion control, ensuring timely decision-making for improved safety and outcomes. Extensive experiments on both needle tracking and insertion show that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art trackers and manual operation, achieving higher tracking accuracy, improved insertion success rates, and reduced procedure time, highlighting promising directions for RUS-based intelligent intervention.

AI.tldr: A Vision-Language-Action model for adaptive robotic ultrasound-guided needle insertion with real-time tracking and control, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Ultrasound-guided needle insertion is challenging due to dynamic imaging conditions and poor needle visualization. Existing automated methods rely on hand-crafted pipelines with modular controllers that degrade in challenging cases.
AI.method: Proposes a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model with: 1) Cross-Depth Fusion tracking head integrating shallow positional and deep semantic features, 2) Tracking-Conditioning register for parameter-efficient feature conditioning, 3) Uncertainty-aware control policy and asynchronous VLA pipeline for adaptive needle insertion control.
AI.result: Method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art trackers and manual operation, achieving higher tracking accuracy, improved insertion success rates, and reduced procedure time.
AI.conclusion: The VLA framework provides a promising direction for robotic ultrasound-based intelligent intervention, enabling real-time adaptive adjustment and unified needle tracking/control.

title: Infection-Reasoner: A Compact Vision-Language Model for Wound Infection Classification with Evidence-Grounded Clinical Reasoning
summary: Assessing chronic wound infection from photographs is challenging because visual appearance varies across wound etiologies, anatomical locations, and imaging conditions. Prior image-based deep learning methods have mainly focused on classification with limited interpretability, despite the need for evidence-grounded explanations to support point-of-care decision making. We present Infection-Reasoner, a compact 4B-parameter reasoning vision-language model for chronic wound infection classification and rationale generation. To address the scarcity of expert-labeled wound images with reasoning annotations, Infection-Reasoner is trained using a two-stage pipeline: (1) reasoning distillation, in which GPT-5.1 generates chain-of-thought rationales for unlabeled wound images to initialize wound-specific reasoning in a smaller student model (Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking), and (2) reinforcement learning post-training with Group Relative Policy Optimization on a small labeled infection dataset to refine classification reasoning. On a held-out heterogeneous wound dataset, Infection-Reasoner achieved 86.8\% accuracy, 86.4\% sensitivity, and 87.1\% specificity, outperforming several strong baselines, including GPT-5.1. Rationale quality was further evaluated using both multimodal large language model (MLLM) judges and wound expert review. Across four MLLM judges, visual-support agreement scores ranged from 0.722 to 0.903, while expert review rated 61.8\% of rationales as Correct and 32.4\% as Partially Correct.

AI.tldr: Infection-Reasoner: A 4B-parameter vision-language model for chronic wound infection classification with rationale generation, trained via reasoning distillation from GPT-5.1 and reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Chronic wound infection assessment from photos is difficult due to visual variability across wound types, locations, and imaging conditions. Existing deep learning methods lack interpretability, while clinical decision-making requires evidence-based explanations.
AI.method: Two-stage training: (1) Reasoning distillation - GPT-5.1 generates chain-of-thought rationales for unlabeled wound images to initialize wound-specific reasoning in Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking student model; (2) Reinforcement learning post-training with Group Relative Policy Optimization on small labeled infection dataset to refine classification reasoning.
AI.result: Achieved 86.8% accuracy, 86.4% sensitivity, and 87.1% specificity on held-out heterogeneous wound dataset, outperforming strong baselines including GPT-5.1. Rationale quality: MLLM judges gave visual-support agreement scores 0.722-0.903; expert review rated 61.8% as Correct and 32.4% as Partially Correct.
AI.conclusion: Infection-Reasoner demonstrates effective chronic wound infection classification with interpretable rationales, addressing the need for evidence-grounded explanations in clinical decision support through a compact reasoning vision-language model.

title: Physics-Guided Dimension Reduction for Simulation-Free Operator Learning of Stiff Differential--Algebraic Systems
summary: Neural surrogates for stiff differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) face two key challenges: soft-constraint methods leave algebraic residuals that stiffness amplifies into large errors, while hard-constraint methods require trajectory data from computationally expensive stiff integrators. We introduce an extended Newton implicit layer that enforces algebraic consistency and quasi-steady-state reduction within a single differentiable solve. Given slow-state predictions from a physics-informed DeepONet, the proposed layer recovers fast and algebraic states, eliminates the stiffness-amplification pathway within each time window, and reduces the output dimension to the slow states alone. Gradients derived via the implicit function theorem capture a stiffness-scaled coupling term that is absent in penalty-based approaches. Cascaded implicit layers further extend the framework to multi-component systems with provable convergence. On a grid-forming inverter DAE (21 states), the proposed method (7 outputs, 1.42 percent error) significantly outperforms penalty methods (39.3 percent), standard Newton approaches (57.0 percent), and augmented Lagrangian or feedback linearization baselines, which fail to converge. Two independently trained models compose into a 44-state system without retraining, achieving 0.72 to 1.16 percent error with zero algebraic residual. Conformal prediction further provides 90 percent coverage in-distribution and enables automatic out-of-distribution detection.

AI.tldr: Proposed extended Newton implicit layer for neural surrogates of stiff DAEs that enforces algebraic consistency and quasi-steady-state reduction, eliminating stiffness-amplification errors while avoiding expensive stiff integrator data.
AI.motivation: Neural surrogates for stiff differential-algebraic equations face two key challenges: soft-constraint methods leave algebraic residuals that stiffness amplifies into large errors, while hard-constraint methods require trajectory data from computationally expensive stiff integrators.
AI.method: Introduced extended Newton implicit layer that enforces algebraic consistency and quasi-steady-state reduction within a single differentiable solve. Uses slow-state predictions from physics-informed DeepONet, recovers fast and algebraic states, eliminates stiffness-amplification pathway, and reduces output dimension to slow states alone. Gradients derived via implicit function theorem capture stiffness-scaled coupling term. Cascaded implicit layers extend to multi-component systems with provable convergence.
AI.result: On 21-state grid-forming inverter DAE: proposed method (7 outputs, 1.42% error) significantly outperforms penalty methods (39.3%), standard Newton approaches (57.0%), and augmented Lagrangian/feedback linearization baselines (fail to converge). Two independently trained models compose into 44-state system without retraining, achieving 0.72-1.16% error with zero algebraic residual. Conformal prediction provides 90% coverage in-distribution and enables automatic out-of-distribution detection.
AI.conclusion: The extended Newton implicit layer provides an effective framework for neural surrogates of stiff DAEs, overcoming stiffness-amplification issues while avoiding expensive stiff integrator data requirements, with strong performance on complex engineering systems and composability properties.

title: Using Learning Theories to Evolve Human-Centered XAI: Future Perspectives and Challenges
comment: Accepted at the CHI 2023 Human-Centered XAI workshop
summary: As Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems continue to grow in size and complexity, so does the difficulty of the quest for AI transparency. In a world of large models and complex AI systems, why do we explain AI and what should we explain? While explanations serve multiple functions, in the face of complexity humans have used and continue to use explanations to foster learning. In this position paper, we discuss how learning theories can be infused in the XAI lifecycle, as well as the key opportunities and challenges when adopting a learner-centered approach to assess, design and evaluate AI explanations. Building on past work, we argue that a learner-centered approach to Explainable AI (XAI) can enhance human agency and ease XAI risks mitigation, helping evolve the practice of human-centered XAI.

AI.tldr: This position paper argues for adopting learner-centered approaches in Explainable AI (XAI) by integrating learning theories into the XAI lifecycle to enhance human agency and mitigate risks.
AI.motivation: As AI systems grow larger and more complex, achieving transparency becomes increasingly difficult. The paper questions why we explain AI and what should be explained, noting that explanations serve multiple functions but historically have been used to foster learning in complex situations.
AI.method: The paper proposes infusing learning theories into the XAI lifecycle through a learner-centered approach. This involves using educational and cognitive science principles to assess, design, and evaluate AI explanations, building on past work in human-centered XAI.
AI.result: The paper identifies key opportunities and challenges when adopting a learner-centered approach to XAI, though specific empirical results are not provided as this is a position paper rather than an experimental study.
AI.conclusion: A learner-centered approach to XAI can enhance human agency and help mitigate XAI risks, evolving the practice of human-centered XAI by focusing on how explanations facilitate learning and understanding.

title: Multi-Objective RIS Deployment Optimization for Physical Layer Security in ISAC Networks
summary: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) have emerged as a key enabler for programmable wireless environments in future Beyond-5G (B5G) and 6G networks. In the meantime, Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) and Physical-Layer Security (PLS) are becoming essential functionalities for next-generation wireless systems, particularly in safety and mission-critical applications. However, jointly optimizing RIS-assisted systems to support communication, sensing, and security introduces complex and often conflicting design trade-offs. In this work, a multi-objective optimization framework for RIS-assisted networks is proposed, aiming to jointly analyze communication performance, sensing accuracy, and security-related channel properties in a unified system perspective. The proposed model jointly considers RIS deployment location, orientation, surface size, and an ISAC configuration weight that controls the allocation of RIS reflection gain between communication and sensing tasks. Simulation results reveal inherent trade-offs among communication reliability, sensing accuracy, and security performance. The proposed framework provides valuable insights into the interplay between communication, sensing, and security, and enables the design of efficient RIS deployment and configuration strategies for secure ISAC-enabled 6G wireless networks.

AI.tldr: A multi-objective optimization framework for RIS-assisted networks that jointly analyzes communication, sensing, and security trade-offs in 6G wireless systems.
AI.motivation: RIS enables programmable wireless environments for B5G/6G, while ISAC and PLS are essential for safety-critical applications. However, jointly optimizing RIS for communication, sensing, and security involves complex, conflicting trade-offs that need systematic analysis.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-objective optimization framework that jointly considers RIS deployment location, orientation, surface size, and an ISAC configuration weight controlling RIS reflection gain allocation between communication and sensing tasks.
AI.result: Simulation results reveal inherent trade-offs among communication reliability, sensing accuracy, and security performance. The framework provides insights into the interplay between these three aspects.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables efficient RIS deployment and configuration strategies for secure ISAC-enabled 6G wireless networks by systematically addressing the complex trade-offs between communication, sensing, and security.

title: Minimum Energy per Bit of Unsourced Multiple Access with Location-Based Codebook Partitioning
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure; accepted for presentation at ISIT 2026
summary: We derive finite-blocklength bounds on the minimum achievable energy per bit over a Gaussian unsourced multiple access (UMA) channel in the presence of heterogeneous path-loss conditions. We consider a setting in which the path loss is known to the users, which enables the use of location-based codebook partitioning [Çakmak et al., 2025]. Through numerical simulations and a large-system analysis based on the replica method, we quantify the performance gain of this strategy relative to the conventional UMA approach in which all users employ a common codebook.

AI.tldr: Finite-blocklength analysis of energy efficiency in Gaussian unsourced multiple access with heterogeneous path loss, comparing location-based codebook partitioning vs common codebook approach.
AI.motivation: To analyze the energy efficiency of unsourced multiple access (UMA) channels under realistic heterogeneous path-loss conditions, where conventional common codebook approaches may be suboptimal.
AI.method: Derive finite-blocklength bounds on minimum achievable energy per bit, use location-based codebook partitioning (where path loss is known to users), conduct numerical simulations, and perform large-system analysis using replica method.
AI.result: Quantify performance gain of location-based codebook partitioning strategy relative to conventional common codebook approach in UMA channels.
AI.conclusion: Location-based codebook partitioning provides measurable performance improvements over common codebook approaches in heterogeneous path-loss UMA scenarios, with gains quantified through both simulations and theoretical analysis.

title: Bimanual Robot Manipulation via Multi-Agent In-Context Learning
summary: Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful reasoning engines for embodied control. In particular, In-Context Learning (ICL) enables off-the-shelf, text-only LLMs to predict robot actions without any task-specific training while preserving their generalization capabilities. Applying ICL to bimanual manipulation remains challenging, as the high-dimensional joint action space and tight inter-arm coordination constraints rapidly overwhelm standard context windows. To address this, we introduce BiCICLe (Bimanual Coordinated In-Context Learning), the first framework that enables standard LLMs to perform few-shot bimanual manipulation without fine-tuning. BiCICLe frames bimanual control as a multi-agent leader-follower problem, decoupling the action space into sequential, conditioned single-arm predictions. This naturally extends to Arms' Debate, an iterative refinement process, and to the introduction of a third LLM-as-Judge to evaluate and select the most plausible coordinated trajectories. Evaluated on 13 tasks from the TWIN benchmark, BiCICLe achieves up to 71.1% average success rate, outperforming the best training-free baseline by 6.7 percentage points and surpassing most supervised methods. We further demonstrate strong few-shot generalization on novel tasks.

AI.tldr: BiCICLe enables standard LLMs to perform few-shot bimanual manipulation without fine-tuning by framing it as a multi-agent leader-follower problem with iterative refinement and LLM-as-Judge evaluation.
AI.motivation: Applying In-Context Learning (ICL) to bimanual manipulation is challenging due to high-dimensional joint action space and tight inter-arm coordination constraints that overwhelm standard context windows.
AI.method: BiCICLe frames bimanual control as multi-agent leader-follower problem, decoupling action space into sequential single-arm predictions. Uses Arms' Debate for iterative refinement and LLM-as-Judge to evaluate coordinated trajectories.
AI.result: Achieves up to 71.1% average success rate on 13 TWIN benchmark tasks, outperforming best training-free baseline by 6.7 percentage points and surpassing most supervised methods. Shows strong few-shot generalization on novel tasks.
AI.conclusion: BiCICLe successfully enables standard text-only LLMs to perform complex bimanual manipulation through innovative multi-agent framing and iterative refinement, achieving state-of-the-art performance without task-specific training.

title: CrackForward: Context-Aware Severity Stage Crack Synthesis for Data Augmentation
summary: Reliable crack detection and segmentation are vital for structural health monitoring, yet the scarcity of well-annotated data constitutes a major challenge. To address this limitation, we propose a novel context-aware generative framework designed to synthesize realistic crack growth patterns for data augmentation. Unlike existing methods that primarily manipulate textures or background content, CrackForward explicitly models crack morphology by combining directional crack elongation with learned thickening and branching. Our framework integrates two key innovations: (i) a contextually guided crack expansion module, which uses local directional cues and adaptive random walk to simulate realistic propagation paths; and (ii) a two-stage U-Net-style generator that learns to reproduce spatially varying crack characteristics such as thickness, branching, and growth. Experimental results show that the generated samples preserve target-stage saturation and thickness characteristics and improve the performance of several crack segmentation architectures. These results indicate that structure-aware synthetic crack generation can provide more informative training data than conventional augmentation alone.

AI.tldr: CrackForward: A context-aware generative framework that synthesizes realistic crack growth patterns for data augmentation in structural health monitoring, improving crack segmentation performance.
AI.motivation: The scarcity of well-annotated crack data in structural health monitoring creates a major challenge for reliable crack detection and segmentation. Existing methods that primarily manipulate textures or background content are insufficient for modeling realistic crack morphology.
AI.method: A novel context-aware generative framework that explicitly models crack morphology by combining directional crack elongation with learned thickening and branching. Key innovations include: (1) a contextually guided crack expansion module using local directional cues and adaptive random walk to simulate realistic propagation paths, and (2) a two-stage U-Net-style generator that learns to reproduce spatially varying crack characteristics like thickness, branching, and growth.
AI.result: Experimental results show that generated samples preserve target-stage saturation and thickness characteristics and improve the performance of several crack segmentation architectures. The structure-aware synthetic crack generation provides more informative training data than conventional augmentation alone.
AI.conclusion: CrackForward demonstrates that context-aware generative modeling of crack morphology can effectively address data scarcity in structural health monitoring, producing realistic synthetic crack patterns that enhance crack segmentation model performance beyond what conventional data augmentation can achieve.

title: Generalization and Membership Inference Attack a Practical Perspective
summary: With the emergence of new evaluation metrics and attack methodologies for Membership Inference Attacks (MIA), it becomes essential to reevaluate previously accepted assumptions. In this paper, we revisit the longstanding debate regarding the correlation between MIA success rates and model generalization using an empirical approach. We focused on employing augmentation techniques and early stopping to enhance model generalization and examined their impact on MIA success rates. We found that utilizing advanced generalization techniques can significantly decrease attack performance, potentially by up to 100 times. Moreover, combining these methods not only improves model generalization but also reduces attack effectiveness by introducing randomness during training. Additionally, our study confirmed the direct impact of generalization on MIA performance through an analysis of over 1K models in a controlled environment.

AI.tldr: Advanced generalization techniques like augmentation and early stopping can reduce MIA success rates by up to 100x, challenging previous assumptions about MIA-generalization correlation.
AI.motivation: To reevaluate the longstanding debate about correlation between Membership Inference Attack (MIA) success rates and model generalization using empirical evidence, as new evaluation metrics and attack methodologies have emerged.
AI.method: Empirical approach focusing on augmentation techniques and early stopping to enhance model generalization, analyzing their impact on MIA success rates. Studied over 1K models in controlled environments.
AI.result: Advanced generalization techniques significantly decrease MIA attack performance (up to 100 times reduction). Combining methods improves generalization while reducing attack effectiveness through introduced training randomness.
AI.conclusion: Generalization directly impacts MIA performance, and employing advanced generalization techniques can substantially enhance privacy protection against membership inference attacks.

title: From Data to Theory: Autonomous Large Language Model Agents for Materials Science
summary: We present an autonomous large language model (LLM) agent for end-to-end, data-driven materials theory development. The model can choose an equation form, generate and run its own code, and test how well the theory matches the data without human intervention. The framework combines step-by-step reasoning with expert-supplied tools, allowing the agent to adjust its approach as needed while keeping a clear record of its decisions. For well-established materials relationships such as the Hall-Petch equation and Paris law, the agent correctly identifies the governing equation and makes reliable predictions on new datasets. For more specialized relationships, such as Kuhn's equation for the HOMO-LUMO gap of conjugated molecules as a function of length, performance depends more strongly on the underlying model, with GPT-5 showing better recovery of the correct equation. Beyond known theories, the agent can also suggest new predictive relationships, illustrated here by a strain-dependent law for changes in the HOMO-LUMO gap. At the same time, the results show that careful validation remains essential, because the agent can still return incorrect, incomplete, or inconsistent equations even when the numerical fit appears strong. Overall, these results highlight both the promise and the current limitations of autonomous LLM agents for AI-assisted scientific modeling and discovery.

AI.tldr: Autonomous LLM agent for materials theory development that can choose equations, generate code, test theories, and suggest new relationships without human intervention.
AI.motivation: To develop AI systems that can autonomously discover and validate scientific theories in materials science, reducing human intervention in the theory development process.
AI.method: Combines step-by-step reasoning with expert-supplied tools, allowing the agent to choose equation forms, generate and run code, and test theory-data matches while maintaining decision records.
AI.result: Successfully identifies established relationships (Hall-Petch, Paris law) and makes reliable predictions; performance on specialized relationships depends on underlying model (GPT-5 better for Kuhn's equation); can suggest new relationships (strain-dependent HOMO-LUMO gap law).
AI.conclusion: Autonomous LLM agents show promise for AI-assisted scientific modeling but require careful validation due to potential for incorrect/incomplete equations even with strong numerical fits.

title: Enhancing the physical layer security with bending beams
summary: Wavefront engineering for applications in near-field wireless connectivity is gradually becoming common ground. In this landscape, beams that propagate on bent paths are ideal candidates for dynamic blockage avoidance and suppression of potential eavesdropping. In this work we study the physical layer security offered by bending beams, and we demonstrate their capabilities for line-of-sight and non-line-of-sight eavesdropping. We analyze the dependencies between the possible locations of an eavesdropper and the design parameters of such beams, and we introduce metrics to assess their physical layer security performance. Our results demonstrate their superiority with respect to beams generated with conventional beam-forming.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the physical layer security benefits of bending beams for wireless communications, showing they outperform conventional beamforming for eavesdropping prevention.
AI.motivation: As wavefront engineering becomes more common in near-field wireless connectivity, there's a need for beams that can follow bent paths to avoid dynamic blockages and suppress potential eavesdropping. Bending beams offer advantages for both line-of-sight and non-line-of-sight scenarios where security is a concern.
AI.method: The study analyzes the dependencies between possible eavesdropper locations and the design parameters of bending beams. The researchers introduce metrics to assess physical layer security performance and compare bending beams against conventional beam-forming approaches.
AI.result: The results demonstrate that bending beams offer superior physical layer security performance compared to beams generated with conventional beam-forming techniques, particularly for both line-of-sight and non-line-of-sight eavesdropping scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Bending beams provide enhanced physical layer security for wireless communications by enabling dynamic blockage avoidance and suppressing potential eavesdropping, making them promising candidates for secure near-field wireless connectivity applications.

title: Wideband Direct Satellite Uplink Enabled by Pilot-less Sparse Superposition Codes
summary: Direct satellite uplink is severely constrained by limited link budgets, which hinder the exploitation of wideband resources, and ultimately limit the throughout. This paper presents a pilot-less coded modulation scheme based on sparse superposition coding (SSC) to enable efficient wideband usage in coverage-limited scenarios. This scheme leverages the structured Zadoff-Chu quasi-orthogonal (ZC-QO) dictionary to support scalable transmission. To address decoding complexity, the SSC transmitted signal embeds root index information via indicator sequences, allowing the receiver to restrict the decoding search space. In addition, a multi-codeword transmission framework with repetition and stop-feedback is developed, enabling reliable communication and better resource utilization. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme achieves throughput gains compared to a more conventional narrow-band multi-dimensional constellation-based approach.

AI.tldr: Proposes pilot-less coded modulation using sparse superposition coding with Zadoff-Chu quasi-orthogonal dictionary for efficient wideband satellite uplinks in coverage-limited scenarios.
AI.motivation: Direct satellite uplinks face severe link budget constraints that limit wideband resource exploitation and throughput. Coverage-limited scenarios need efficient wideband usage solutions.
AI.method: Uses sparse superposition coding with ZC-QO dictionary for scalable transmission. Embeds root index information via indicator sequences to reduce decoding complexity. Develops multi-codeword framework with repetition and stop-feedback for reliability.
AI.result: Simulation shows throughput gains compared to conventional narrow-band multi-dimensional constellation-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pilot-less SSC scheme enables efficient wideband usage in coverage-limited satellite uplinks with improved throughput.

title: Benefits of Low-Cost Bio-Inspiration in the Age of Overparametrization
summary: While Central Pattern Generators (CPGs) and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP) are widely used paradigms in robot control, few systematic studies have been performed on the relative merits of large parameter spaces. In contexts where input and output spaces are small and performance is bounded, having more parameters to optimize may actively hinder the learning process instead of empowering it. To empirically measure this, we submit a given robot morphology, with limited proprioceptive capabilities, to controller optimization under two bio-inspired paradigms (CPGs and MLPs) with evolutionary- and reinforcement- trainer protocols. By varying parameter spaces across multiple reward functions, we observe that shallow MLPs and densely connected CPGs result in better performance when compared to deeper MLPs or Actor-Critic architectures. To account for the relationship between said performance and the number of parameters, we introduce a Parameter Impact metric which demonstrates that the additional parameters required by the reinforcement technique do not translate into better performance, thus favouring evolutionary strategies.

AI.tldr: Small parameter spaces outperform large ones in robot control when input/output spaces are limited; evolutionary strategies with shallow MLPs or dense CPGs work better than deep networks or Actor-Critic architectures.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of systematic studies comparing CPGs and MLPs in robot control regarding parameter space size. The paper investigates whether larger parameter spaces actually help or hinder learning when input/output spaces are small and performance is bounded.
AI.method: Used a robot morphology with limited proprioception and tested controller optimization under CPGs and MLPs with both evolutionary and reinforcement learning trainers. Varied parameter spaces across multiple reward functions and introduced a Parameter Impact metric to measure relationship between performance and parameter count.
AI.result: Shallow MLPs and densely connected CPGs performed better than deeper MLPs or Actor-Critic architectures. Reinforcement learning's additional parameters didn't translate to better performance, favoring evolutionary strategies instead.
AI.conclusion: In constrained robot control scenarios with limited input/output spaces, smaller parameter spaces and evolutionary optimization strategies are more effective than large parameter spaces and complex reinforcement learning architectures.

title: Visual Reasoning through Tool-supervised Reinforcement Learning
summary: In this paper, we investigate the problem of how to effectively master tool-use to solve complex visual reasoning tasks for Multimodal Large Language Models. To achieve that, we propose a novel Tool-supervised Reinforcement Learning (ToolsRL) framework, with direct tool supervision for more effective tool-use learning. We focus on a series of simple, native, and interpretable visual tools, including zoom-in, rotate, flip, and draw point/line, whose tool supervision is easy to collect. A reinforcement learning curriculum is developed, where the first stage is solely optimized by a set of well motivated tool-specific rewards, and the second stage is trained with the accuracy targeted rewards while allowing calling tools. In this way, tool calling capability is mastered before using tools to complete visual reasoning tasks, avoiding the potential optimization conflict among those heterogeneous tasks. Our experiments have shown that the tool-supervised curriculum training is efficient and ToolsRL can achieve strong tool-use capabilities for complex visual reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: ToolsRL: A tool-supervised reinforcement learning framework that teaches multimodal LLMs to effectively use visual tools (zoom, rotate, flip, draw) for complex visual reasoning tasks through curriculum learning.
AI.motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models need to effectively master tool-use to solve complex visual reasoning tasks, but current approaches lack effective supervision for learning tool manipulation skills.
AI.method: Proposes ToolsRL framework with direct tool supervision using simple, native visual tools. Uses two-stage reinforcement learning curriculum: first stage optimized by tool-specific rewards to master tool calling, second stage trained with accuracy rewards while allowing tool use.
AI.result: The tool-supervised curriculum training is efficient and ToolsRL achieves strong tool-use capabilities for complex visual reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ToolsRL framework effectively teaches multimodal LLMs to use visual tools through supervised curriculum learning, separating tool mastery from task completion to avoid optimization conflicts.

title: Are LLM Uncertainty and Correctness Encoded by the Same Features? A Functional Dissociation via Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Large language models can be uncertain yet correct, or confident yet wrong, raising the question of whether their output-level uncertainty and their actual correctness are driven by the same internal mechanisms or by distinct feature populations. We introduce a 2x2 framework that partitions model predictions along correctness and confidence axes, and uses sparse autoencoders to identify features associated with each dimension independently. Applying this to Llama-3.1-8B and Gemma-2-9B, we identify three feature populations that play fundamentally different functional roles. Pure uncertainty features are functionally essential: suppressing them severely degrades accuracy. Pure incorrectness features are functionally inert: despite showing statistically significant activation differences between correct and incorrect predictions, the majority produce near-zero change in accuracy when suppressed. Confounded features that encode both signals are detrimental to output quality, and targeted suppression of them yields a 1.1% accuracy improvement and a 75% entropy reduction, with effects transferring across the ARC-Challenge and RACE benchmarks. The feature categories are also informationally distinct: the activations of just 3 confounded features from a single mid-network layer predict model correctness (AUROC ~0.79), enabling selective abstention that raises accuracy from 62% to 81% at 53% coverage. The results demonstrate that uncertainty and correctness are distinct internal phenomena, with implications for interpretability and targeted inference-time intervention.

AI.tldr: LLMs have distinct internal features for uncertainty vs. correctness - suppressing uncertainty features hurts accuracy, suppressing incorrectness features does little, while suppressing confounded features improves accuracy and reduces entropy.
AI.motivation: LLMs can be uncertain yet correct or confident yet wrong, raising the question of whether uncertainty and correctness are driven by the same or different internal mechanisms. The paper aims to disentangle these two dimensions.
AI.method: Introduced a 2x2 framework partitioning predictions along correctness and confidence axes, used sparse autoencoders on Llama-3.1-8B and Gemma-2-9B to identify features associated with each dimension independently, then analyzed three feature populations through targeted suppression experiments.
AI.result: Identified three distinct feature populations: 1) Pure uncertainty features (functionally essential), 2) Pure incorrectness features (functionally inert), 3) Confounded features (detrimental). Suppressing confounded features improved accuracy by 1.1% and reduced entropy by 75%. Just 3 confounded features predicted correctness with AUROC ~0.79, enabling selective abstention that raised accuracy from 62% to 81% at 53% coverage.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty and correctness are distinct internal phenomena in LLMs, with implications for interpretability and targeted inference-time interventions. The findings enable better understanding of model internals and potential improvements through feature-level manipulation.

title: Hidden Reliability Risks in Large Language Models: Systematic Identification of Precision-Induced Output Disagreements
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed under diverse numerical precision configurations, including standard floating-point formats (e.g., bfloat16 and float16) and quantized integer formats (e.g., int16 and int8), to meet efficiency and resource constraints. However, minor inconsistencies between LLMs of different precisions are difficult to detect and are often overlooked by existing evaluation methods. In this paper, we present PrecisionDiff, an automated differential testing framework for systematically detecting precision-induced behavioral disagreements in LLMs. PrecisionDiff generates precision-sensitive test inputs and performs cross-precision comparative analysis to uncover subtle divergences that remain hidden under conventional testing strategies. To demonstrate its practical significance, we instantiate PrecisionDiff on the alignment verification task, where precision-induced disagreements manifest as jailbreak divergence-inputs that are rejected under one precision may produce harmful responses under another. Experimental results show that such behavioral disagreements are widespread across multiple open-source aligned LLMs and precision settings, and that PrecisionDiff significantly outperforms vanilla testing methods in detecting these issues. Our work enables automated precision-sensitive test generation, facilitating effective pre-deployment evaluation and improving precision robustness during training.

AI.tldr: PrecisionDiff is an automated differential testing framework that detects precision-induced behavioral disagreements in LLMs across different numerical precision configurations (float16, int8, etc.), revealing subtle divergences that conventional testing misses.
AI.motivation: LLMs are deployed with various numerical precision formats for efficiency, but minor inconsistencies between different precision versions are hard to detect and often overlooked by existing evaluation methods, potentially leading to safety issues.
AI.method: PrecisionDiff generates precision-sensitive test inputs and performs cross-precision comparative analysis through differential testing, specifically applied to alignment verification to detect jailbreak divergence where harmful responses appear under different precisions.
AI.result: Experimental results show that precision-induced behavioral disagreements are widespread across multiple open-source aligned LLMs and precision settings, and PrecisionDiff significantly outperforms vanilla testing methods in detecting these issues.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables automated precision-sensitive test generation, facilitating effective pre-deployment evaluation and improving precision robustness during LLM training, addressing a critical gap in current testing methodologies.

title: Sample entropy for graph signals: An approach to nonlinear analysis of graph signals
comment: This work was accepted to be presented at the Graph Signal Processing Workshop 2026
summary: We introduce a graph-signal generalisation of Sample Entropy, denoted SampEn$_{G}$, to quantify irregularity of graph signals on a continuous state space, complementing existing methods on symbolic dynamics. Our approach replaces the temporal delay embedding of classical SampEn with a multi-hop graph-based embedding: for each node, we aggregate patterns from local walk-weighted neighbourhood averages computed via powers of the graph shift operator. We show empirically that SampEn$_{G}$ reduces to classical 1D SampEn on directed path graphs, and validate its nonlinear sensitivity using the logistic map. Experiments on directed Erdős--Rényi graph signals further characterise its behaviour with connectivity and pattern length $m$, with practical runtimes on the order of thousands of nodes. We expect SampEn$_{G}$ to open up new ways to analyse graph signals, generalising SampEn and the concept of conditional entropy to extending nonlinear analysis to a wide variety of network data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SampEn_G, a graph-signal generalization of Sample Entropy that quantifies irregularity of graph signals on continuous state spaces, extending nonlinear analysis to network data.
AI.motivation: To extend nonlinear signal analysis methods like Sample Entropy from traditional 1D time series to graph-structured data, enabling irregularity quantification for network signals where existing methods focus on symbolic dynamics.
AI.method: Replaces temporal delay embedding with multi-hop graph-based embedding: for each node, aggregates patterns from local walk-weighted neighborhood averages computed via powers of the graph shift operator.
AI.result: SampEn_G reduces to classical 1D SampEn on directed path graphs, shows nonlinear sensitivity with logistic map validation, and exhibits practical runtime scaling to thousands of nodes on directed Erdős-Rényi graphs.
AI.conclusion: SampEn_G generalizes Sample Entropy and conditional entropy concepts to graph signals, opening new ways for nonlinear analysis of diverse network data beyond traditional time series.

title: DNA storage approaching the information-theoretic ceiling
summary: Synthetic DNA approaches 227.5 exabytes per gram of storage density with stability over millennial timescales. Realising this capacity requires error-correction codes that recover data from substantial synthesis and sequencing errors. Existing codecs convert noisy sequencer output into discrete base calls before error correction, discarding probabilistic information about which positions are reliable. Here we present a coding scheme that retains the sequencer's per-position posterior distributions through an integrated decoder of profile hidden Markov model alignment, log-product fusion across reads, and ordered-statistics decoding. On the DT4DDS channel simulator, the codec recovers 155.8 and 25.9 exabytes per gram of dsDNA under high- and low-fidelity conditions, exceeding the highest prior-art density on each channel by 11 and 52 percent. Under a single-encode-then-degrade protocol mapped to depurination kinetics at 25 °C in the dry state, the codec projects 282 years of decodable storage at 17.1 exabytes per gram. These results place DNA storage density within reach of the Shannon bound of the underlying channel.

AI.tldr: DNA storage codec using probabilistic sequencing data achieves record storage densities approaching Shannon bound.
AI.motivation: DNA storage offers massive density (227.5 exabytes/gram) and millennial stability, but requires error correction for noisy synthesis/sequencing. Existing methods discard valuable probabilistic sequencing information.
AI.method: Integrated decoder using profile hidden Markov model alignment, log-product fusion across reads, and ordered-statistics decoding that retains sequencer's per-position posterior distributions.
AI.result: Achieves 155.8 and 25.9 exabytes/gram under high/low-fidelity conditions (11% and 52% improvements). Projects 282 years of decodable storage at 17.1 exabytes/gram under depurination kinetics.
AI.conclusion: The codec brings DNA storage density close to Shannon bound by effectively using probabilistic sequencing information for error correction.

title: OVPD: A Virtual-Physical Fusion Testing Dataset of OnSite Auton-omous Driving Challenge
summary: The rapid iteration of autonomous driving algorithms has created a growing demand for high-fidelity, replayable, and diagnosable testing data. However, many public datasets lack real vehicle dynamics feedback and closed-loop interaction with surrounding traffic and road infrastructure, limiting their ability to reflect deployment readiness. To address this gap, we present OVPD (OnSite Virtual-Physical Dataset), a virtual-physical fusion testing dataset released from the 2025 OnSite Autonomous Driving Challenge. Centered on real-vehicle-in-the-loop testing, OVPD integrates virtual background traffic with vehicle-infrastructure perception to build controllable and interactive closed-loop test environments on a proving ground. The dataset contains 20 testing clips from 20 teams over a scenario chain of 15 atomic scenarios, totaling nearly 3 hours of multi-modal data, including vehicle trajectories and states, control commands, and digital-twin-rendered surround-view observations. OVPD supports long-tail planning and decision-making validation, open-loop or platform-enabled closed-loop evaluation, and comprehensive assessment across safety, efficiency, comfort, rule compliance, and traffic impact, providing actionable evidence for failure diagnosis and iterative improvement. The dataset is available via: https://huggingface.co/datasets/Yuhang253820/Onsite_OPVD

AI.tldr: OVPD is a virtual-physical fusion dataset for autonomous driving testing that combines real vehicle dynamics with virtual traffic and infrastructure to create closed-loop, interactive test environments.
AI.motivation: Existing public datasets lack real vehicle dynamics feedback and closed-loop interaction with traffic/infrastructure, limiting their ability to assess deployment readiness of autonomous driving algorithms.
AI.method: Real-vehicle-in-the-loop testing integrated with virtual background traffic and vehicle-infrastructure perception on a proving ground, creating controllable closed-loop test environments.
AI.result: 20 testing clips from 20 teams over 15 atomic scenarios, totaling nearly 3 hours of multi-modal data including trajectories, control commands, and digital-twin-rendered surround-view observations.
AI.conclusion: OVPD enables comprehensive autonomous driving validation including long-tail planning, closed-loop evaluation, and multi-dimensional assessment (safety, efficiency, comfort, etc.), providing actionable evidence for failure diagnosis and improvement.

title: Camera Control for Text-to-Image Generation via Learning Viewpoint Tokens
summary: Current text-to-image models struggle to provide precise camera control using natural language alone. In this work, we present a framework for precise camera control with global scene understanding in text-to-image generation by learning parametric camera tokens. We fine-tune image generation models for viewpoint-conditioned text-to-image generation on a curated dataset that combines 3D-rendered images for geometric supervision and photorealistic augmentations for appearance and background diversity. Qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while preserving image quality and prompt fidelity. Unlike prior methods that overfit to object-specific appearance correlations, our viewpoint tokens learn factorized geometric representations that transfer to unseen object categories. Our work shows that text-vision latent spaces can be endowed with explicit 3D camera structure, offering a pathway toward geometrically-aware prompts for text-to-image generation. Project page: https://randdl.github.io/viewtoken_control/

AI.tldr: A framework for precise camera control in text-to-image generation using learned parametric camera tokens that achieve state-of-the-art viewpoint accuracy while maintaining image quality.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-image models struggle with precise camera control using natural language alone, lacking explicit 3D geometric understanding for viewpoint specification.
AI.method: Fine-tune image generation models with parametric camera tokens on a curated dataset combining 3D-rendered images for geometric supervision and photorealistic augmentations for appearance diversity.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art camera control accuracy while preserving image quality and prompt fidelity, with learned viewpoint tokens that transfer to unseen object categories.
AI.conclusion: Text-vision latent spaces can be endowed with explicit 3D camera structure, offering a pathway toward geometrically-aware prompts for text-to-image generation.

title: Fast Amortized Fitting of Scientific Signals Across Time and Ensembles via Transferable Neural Fields
summary: Neural fields, also known as implicit neural representations (INRs), offer a powerful framework for modeling continuous geometry, but their effectiveness in high-dimensional scientific settings is limited by slow convergence and scaling challenges. In this study, we extend INR models to handle spatiotemporal and multivariate signals and show how INR features can be transferred across scientific signals to enable efficient and scalable representation across time and ensemble runs in an amortized fashion.
