AI.result: The framework establishes deterministic potential descent, certified termination via dominance coverage, uniform bound on layer width from cost-grid geometry, and greedy cost-space dispersion within skyline.
AI.conclusion: Pareto dominance can be repositioned from passive filter to deterministic driver of search, operating without scalarization, heuristic guidance, or probabilistic models.

title: FedSIR: Spectral Client Identification and Relabeling for Federated Learning with Noisy Labels
comment: Accepted at the 5th Workshop on Federated Learning for Computer Vision (FedVision), CVPR 2026. Sina Gholami and Abdulmoneam Ali contributed equally
summary:
Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data; however, the presence of noisy labels across distributed clients can severely degrade the learning performance. In this paper, we propose FedSIR, a multi-stage framework for robust FL under noisy labels. Different from existing approaches that mainly rely on designing noise-tolerant loss functions or exploiting loss dynamics during training, our method leverages the spectral structure of client feature representations to identify and mitigate label noise.
  Our framework consists of three key components. First, we identify clean and noisy clients by analyzing the spectral consistency of class-wise feature subspaces with minimal communication overhead. Second, clean clients provide spectral references that enable noisy clients to relabel potentially corrupted samples using both dominant class directions and residual subspaces. Third, we employ a noise-aware training strategy that integrates logit-adjusted loss, knowledge distillation, and distance-aware aggregation to further stabilize federated optimization. Extensive experiments on standard FL benchmarks demonstrate that FedSIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods for FL with noisy labels. The code is available at https://github.com/sinagh72/FedSIR.

AI.tldr: FedSIR: A multi-stage federated learning framework that uses spectral analysis of client feature representations to handle noisy labels, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Federated learning enables collaborative training without sharing raw data, but noisy labels across distributed clients can severely degrade learning performance. Existing approaches mainly rely on noise-tolerant loss functions or loss dynamics, which may not fully address the problem.
AI.method: Three-stage framework: 1) Identify clean vs. noisy clients by analyzing spectral consistency of class-wise feature subspaces with minimal communication; 2) Clean clients provide spectral references for noisy clients to relabel corrupted samples using dominant class directions and residual subspaces; 3) Noise-aware training integrating logit-adjusted loss, knowledge distillation, and distance-aware aggregation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard FL benchmarks show FedSIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods for federated learning with noisy labels.
AI.conclusion: FedSIR effectively addresses noisy labels in federated learning by leveraging spectral structure of feature representations, providing a robust multi-stage framework with minimal communication overhead.

title: FingerEye: Continuous and Unified Vision-Tactile Sensing for Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Dexterous robotic manipulation requires comprehensive perception across all phases of interaction: pre-contact, contact initiation, and post-contact. Such continuous feedback allows a robot to adapt its actions throughout interaction. However, many existing tactile sensors, such as GelSight and its variants, only provide feedback after contact is established, limiting a robot's ability to precisely initiate contact. We introduce FingerEye, a compact and cost-effective sensor that provides continuous vision-tactile feedback throughout the interaction process. FingerEye integrates binocular RGB cameras to provide close-range visual perception with implicit stereo depth. Upon contact, external forces and torques deform a compliant ring structure; these deformations are captured via marker-based pose estimation and serve as a proxy for contact wrench sensing. This design enables a perception stream that smoothly transitions from pre-contact visual cues to post-contact tactile feedback. Building on this sensing capability, we develop a vision-tactile imitation learning policy that fuses signals from multiple FingerEye sensors to learn dexterous manipulation behaviors from limited real-world data. We further develop a digital twin of our sensor and robot platform to improve policy generalization. By combining real demonstrations with visually augmented simulated observations for representation learning, the learned policies become more robust to object appearance variations. Together, these design aspects enable dexterous manipulation across diverse object properties and interaction regimes, including coin standing, chip picking, letter retrieving, and syringe manipulation. The hardware design, code, appendix, and videos are available on our project website: https://nus-lins-lab.github.io/FingerEyeWeb/

AI.tldr: FingerEye is a compact, cost-effective vision-tactile sensor that provides continuous feedback from pre-contact to post-contact phases, enabling dexterous manipulation through imitation learning with real and simulated data.
AI.motivation: Existing tactile sensors like GelSight only provide feedback after contact is established, limiting robots' ability to precisely initiate contact and adapt throughout the entire interaction process.
AI.method: FingerEye integrates binocular RGB cameras for pre-contact visual perception and uses a compliant ring structure with marker-based pose estimation to measure contact forces and torques. A vision-tactile imitation learning policy fuses signals from multiple sensors, enhanced by a digital twin and simulated observations for better generalization.
AI.result: The system enables dexterous manipulation across diverse tasks including coin standing, chip picking, letter retrieving, and syringe manipulation, with improved robustness to object appearance variations.
AI.conclusion: FingerEye provides continuous vision-tactile feedback throughout interaction phases, enabling more adaptive and precise robotic manipulation through combined real-world and simulated learning approaches.

title: EmbodiedMidtrain: Bridging the Gap between Vision-Language Models and Vision-Language-Action Models via Mid-training
summary: Vision-Language-Action Models (VLAs) inherit their visual and linguistic capabilities from Vision-Language Models (VLMs), yet most VLAs are built from off-the-shelf VLMs that are not adapted to the embodied domain, limiting their downstream performance. In this work, we propose EmbodiedMidtrain to bridge the gap between VLMs and VLAs. We first characterize the data distribution gap between them, showing that VLA data occupy compact regions that are largely separated from the broader VLM distribution, while the degree of alignment varies substantially both across and within VLM data sources. Then, we build a mid-training data engine that leverages a lightweight learnable proximity estimator to select the most VLA-aligned candidates from a large VLM pool, and mid-trains the VLM on this curated mixture before downstream VLA fine-tuning. Experiments on three robot manipulation benchmarks show that mid-training consistently improves performance across different VLM backbones, achieving results competitive with expert VLAs and off-the-shelf VLMs trained with larger model scale and training budgets. Further analysis reveals that mid-training provides a stronger initialization for VLA fine-tuning, with gains emerging from the earliest steps and widening throughout training. Moreover, the data engine captures both dataset-level and sample-level alignment signals, favoring spatial reasoning over text-centric tasks while preserving the diversity of the VLM data. We will release all code, data and models for future research.

AI.tldr: EmbodiedMidtrain bridges VLMs and VLAs by identifying data distribution gaps, using a proximity estimator to select VLA-aligned data, and mid-training VLMs before VLA fine-tuning, improving robot manipulation performance.
AI.motivation: Most VLAs use off-the-shelf VLMs not adapted to embodied domains, limiting downstream performance due to data distribution gaps between VLMs (general vision-language) and VLAs (embodied tasks).
AI.method: 1) Characterize data distribution gap between VLMs and VLAs; 2) Build mid-training data engine with learnable proximity estimator to select VLA-aligned candidates from VLM pool; 3) Mid-train VLM on curated mixture before downstream VLA fine-tuning.
AI.result: Mid-training consistently improves performance across three robot manipulation benchmarks, competitive with expert VLAs and larger-scale VLMs. Provides stronger initialization for VLA fine-tuning with gains emerging early and widening throughout training.
AI.conclusion: EmbodiedMidtrain effectively bridges VLMs and VLAs by addressing data distribution gaps through selective mid-training, enabling better embodied task performance while preserving VLM diversity. Data engine captures both dataset-level and sample-level alignment signals.

title: Analysis of Nystrom method with sequential ridge leverage scores
summary: Large-scale kernel ridge regression (KRR) is limited by the need to store a large kernel matrix K_t. To avoid storing the entire matrix K_t, Nystrom methods subsample a subset of columns of the kernel matrix, and efficiently find an approximate KRR solution on the reconstructed matrix. The chosen subsampling distribution in turn affects the statistical and computational tradeoffs. For KRR problems, recent works show that a sampling distribution proportional to the ridge leverage scores (RLSs) provides strong reconstruction guarantees for the approximation. While exact RLSs are as difficult to compute as a KRR solution, we may be able to approximate them well enough. In this paper, we study KRR problems in a sequential setting and introduce the INK-ESTIMATE algorithm, that incrementally computes the RLSs estimates. INK-ESTIMATE maintains a small sketch of K_t, that at each step is used to compute an intermediate estimate of the RLSs. First, our sketch update does not require access to previously seen columns, and therefore a single pass over the kernel matrix is sufficient. Second, the algorithm requires a fixed, small space budget to run dependent only on the effective dimension of the kernel matrix. Finally, our sketch provides strong approximation guarantees on the distance between the true kernel matrix and its approximation, and on the statistical risk of the approximate KRR solution at any time, because all our guarantees hold at any intermediate step.

AI.tldr: INK-ESTIMATE: A single-pass, space-efficient algorithm for incremental ridge leverage score estimation in kernel ridge regression using small sketches.
AI.motivation: Large-scale kernel ridge regression faces memory limitations due to storing large kernel matrices. Nyström methods help by subsampling columns, but require computing ridge leverage scores which are as expensive as solving KRR itself. Need efficient incremental estimation of these scores.
AI.method: INK-ESTIMATE algorithm incrementally computes ridge leverage score estimates using a small sketch of the kernel matrix. It maintains this sketch with single-pass access (no need for previous columns), uses fixed space budget dependent on effective dimension, and updates estimates sequentially.
AI.result: Algorithm provides strong approximation guarantees: accurate reconstruction of kernel matrix, statistical risk bounds for approximate KRR solutions, and all guarantees hold at any intermediate step (not just final). Single-pass, space-efficient operation.
AI.conclusion: INK-ESTIMATE enables efficient large-scale KRR by incrementally estimating ridge leverage scores with strong theoretical guarantees, overcoming memory limitations while maintaining statistical accuracy.

title: MIRROR: A Hierarchical Benchmark for Metacognitive Calibration in Large Language Models
summary: We introduce MIRROR, a benchmark comprising eight experiments across four metacognitive levels that evaluates whether large language models can use self-knowledge to make better decisions. We evaluate 16 models from 8 labs across approximately 250,000 evaluation instances using five independent behavioral measurement channels. Core experiments are run across the full model roster; experiments with specialized infrastructure requirements report explicitly marked model subsets. We find two phenomena with direct implications for agentic deployment: (1) compositional self-prediction fails universally -- the Compositional Calibration Error ranges from 0.500 to 0.943 on the original 15-model Exp3-v1 set (and 0.434 to 0.758 on the balanced 16-model Exp3-v2 expansion), indicating that models cannot predict their own performance on multi-domain tasks, and (2) models exhibit above-chance but imperfect domain-specific self-knowledge yet systematically fail to translate even this partial awareness into appropriate agentic action-selection -- external metacognitive control reduces the Confident Failure Rate from 0.600 to 0.143 (76% reduction at temperature 0; mean 70% at temperature 0.7 across 5 models from 4 labs). Providing models with their own calibration scores produces no significant improvement (p > 0.05); only architectural constraint is effective. This suggests that external metacognitive scaffolding -- not improved self-knowledge -- is the path to safer autonomous AI systems. Code, data, and Croissant metadata will be released publicly with the benchmark.

AI.tldr: MIRROR benchmark evaluates LLMs' metacognitive abilities, finding they can't predict their own performance on multi-domain tasks and fail to use self-knowledge for better decisions, suggesting external metacognitive scaffolding is needed for safer AI systems.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether large language models can use self-knowledge to make better decisions, which is crucial for safer autonomous AI systems.
AI.method: Created MIRROR benchmark with 8 experiments across 4 metacognitive levels, evaluated 16 models from 8 labs across ~250,000 instances using 5 behavioral measurement channels.
AI.result: Two key findings: (1) models universally fail at compositional self-prediction (CCE 0.500-0.943), (2) models have partial domain-specific self-knowledge but can't translate it into action selection - external metacognitive control reduces Confident Failure Rate by 70-76%.
AI.conclusion: External metacognitive scaffolding, not improved self-knowledge, is the path to safer autonomous AI systems; architectural constraints are effective while providing calibration scores isn't.

title: A Kinematic Framework for Evaluating Pinch Configurations in Robotic Hand Design without Object or Contact Models
comment: This manuscript has been submitted for possible publication
summary: Evaluating the pinch capability of a robotic hand is important for understanding its functional dexterity. However, many existing grasp evaluation methods rely on object geometry or contact force models, which limits their applicability during the early stages of robotic hand design. This study proposes a kinematic evaluation method for analyzing pinch configurations of robotic hands based on interactions between fingertip workspaces. First, the reachable workspace of each fingertip is computed from the joint configurations of the fingers. Then, feasible pinch configurations are detected by evaluating the relationships between fingertip pairs. Since the proposed method does not require information about object geometry or contact force models, the pinch capability of a robotic hand can be evaluated solely based on its kinematic structure. In addition, analyses are performed on four different kinematic structures of the hand to investigate their impact on the pinch configurations. The proposed evaluation framework can serve as a useful tool for comparing different robotic hand designs and analyzing pinch capability during the design stage.

AI.tldr: Proposes a kinematic evaluation method for analyzing robotic hand pinch configurations using fingertip workspace interactions, without needing object geometry or contact force models.
AI.motivation: Existing grasp evaluation methods rely on object geometry or contact force models, limiting their applicability during early robotic hand design stages when such information isn't available.
AI.method: Computes reachable workspace of each fingertip from joint configurations, then detects feasible pinch configurations by evaluating relationships between fingertip pairs based on workspace interactions.
AI.result: The method enables evaluation of pinch capability solely based on kinematic structure. Analysis of four different hand kinematic structures reveals their impact on pinch configurations.
AI.conclusion: The framework serves as a useful tool for comparing robotic hand designs and analyzing pinch capability during the design stage, independent of object-specific information.

title: Investigation of cardinality classification for bacterial colony counting using explainable artificial intelligence
summary: Automatic bacterial colony counting is a highly sought-after technology in modern biological laboratories because it eliminates manual counting effort. Previous work has observed that MicrobiaNet, currently the best-performing cardinality classification model for colony counting, has difficulty distinguishing colonies of three or more individuals. However, it is unclear if this is due to properties of the data together with inherent characteristics of the MicrobiaNet model. By analysing MicrobiaNet with explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), we demonstrate that XAI can provide insights into how data properties constrain cardinality classification performance in colony counting. Our results show that high visual similarity across classes is the key issue hindering further performance improvement, revising prior assertions about MicrobiaNet. These findings suggest future work should focus on models that explicitly incorporate visual similarity or explore density estimation approaches, with broader implications for neural network classifiers trained on imbalanced datasets.

AI.tldr: XAI analysis reveals that high visual similarity across colony count classes, not model limitations, is the main barrier to improving bacterial colony counting performance.
AI.motivation: To understand why MicrobiaNet struggles with distinguishing colonies of three or more individuals, and to determine if this is due to data properties or model characteristics.
AI.method: Used explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) to analyze MicrobiaNet's performance and identify the root causes of classification difficulties.
AI.result: High visual similarity across colony count classes is the primary constraint on performance, revising previous assumptions about MicrobiaNet's limitations.
AI.conclusion: Future work should focus on models that explicitly handle visual similarity or explore density estimation approaches, with implications for neural networks on imbalanced datasets.

title: Improved large-scale graph learning through ridge spectral sparsification
summary: Graph-based techniques and spectral graph theory have enriched the field of machine learning with a variety of critical advances. A central object in the analysis is the graph Laplacian L, which encodes the structure of the graph. We consider the problem of learning over this Laplacian in a distributed streaming setting, where new edges of the graph are observed in real time by a network of workers. In this setting, it is hard to learn quickly or approximately while keeping a distributed representation of L. To address this challenge, we present a novel algorithm, GSQUEAK, which efficiently sparsifies the Laplacian by maintaining a small subset of effective resistances. We show that our algorithm produces sparsifiers with strong spectral approximation guarantees, all while processing edges in a single pass and in a distributed fashion.

AI.tldr: GSQUEAK algorithm for distributed streaming graph Laplacian sparsification using effective resistances
AI.motivation: Need to learn over graph Laplacians in distributed streaming settings where new edges arrive in real time across worker networks, requiring efficient approximation while maintaining distributed representation
AI.method: GSQUEAK algorithm that efficiently sparsifies the Laplacian by maintaining a small subset of effective resistances, processing edges in single pass distributed fashion
AI.result: Algorithm produces sparsifiers with strong spectral approximation guarantees while operating in distributed streaming environment
AI.conclusion: GSQUEAK enables efficient distributed streaming learning over graph Laplacians with theoretical guarantees

title: Visual-Tactile Peg-in-Hole Assembly Learning from Peg-out-of-Hole Disassembly
summary: Peg-in-hole (PiH) assembly is a fundamental yet challenging robotic manipulation task. While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in tackling such tasks, it requires extensive exploration. In this paper, we propose a novel visual-tactile skill learning framework for the PiH task that leverages its inverse task, i.e., peg-out-of-hole (PooH) disassembly, to facilitate PiH learning. Compared to PiH, PooH is inherently easier as it only needs to overcome existing friction without precise alignment, making data collection more efficient. To this end, we formulate both PooH and PiH as Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) in a unified environment with shared visual-tactile observation space. A visual-tactile PooH policy is first trained; its trajectories, containing kinematic, visual and tactile information, are temporally reversed and action-randomized to provide expert data for PiH. In the policy learning, visual sensing facilitates the peg-hole approach, while tactile measurements compensate for peg-hole misalignment. Experiments across diverse peg-hole geometries show that the visual-tactile policy attains 6.4% lower contact forces than its single-modality counterparts, and that our framework achieves average success rates of 87.5% on seen objects and 77.1% on unseen objects, outperforming direct RL methods that train PiH policies from scratch by 18.1% in success rate. Demos, code, and datasets are available at https://sites.google.com/view/pooh2pih.

AI.tldr: A visual-tactile skill learning framework for peg-in-hole assembly that uses the easier peg-out-of-hole disassembly task to generate expert data, improving learning efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: Peg-in-hole assembly is challenging for robots and requires extensive exploration in reinforcement learning. The inverse task (peg-out-of-hole) is easier and can provide efficient data collection to facilitate learning.
AI.method: Formulate both tasks as POMDPs in a unified environment with shared visual-tactile observation space. Train a visual-tactile PooH policy, then reverse and randomize its trajectories to create expert data for PiH learning. Visual sensing handles approach while tactile compensates for misalignment.
AI.result: Visual-tactile policy achieves 6.4% lower contact forces than single-modality counterparts. Framework achieves 87.5% success on seen objects and 77.1% on unseen objects, outperforming direct RL methods by 18.1% in success rate.
AI.conclusion: Using the inverse task (PooH) to facilitate PiH learning is effective. The visual-tactile approach combining visual sensing for approach and tactile for misalignment compensation outperforms single-modality methods and direct RL training.

title: Cognitive Alignment At No Cost: Inducing Human Attention Biases For Interpretable Vision Transformers
summary: For state-of-the-art image understanding, Vision Transformers (ViTs) have become the standard architecture but their processing diverges substantially from human attentional characteristics. We investigate whether this cognitive gap can be shrunk by fine-tuning the self-attention weights of Google's ViT-B/16 on human saliency fixation maps. To isolate the effects of semantically relevant signals from generic human supervision, the tuned model is compared against a shuffled control. Fine-tuning significantly improved alignment across five saliency metrics and induced three hallmark human-like biases: tuning reversed the baseline's anti-human large-object bias toward small-objects, amplified the animacy preference and diminished extreme attention entropy. Bayesian parity analysis provides decisive to very-strong evidence that this cognitive alignment comes at no cost to the model's original classification performance on in- (ImageNet), corrupted (ImageNet-C) and out-of-distribution (ObjectNet) benchmarks. An equivalent procedure applied to a ResNet-50 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) instead degraded both alignment and accuracy, suggesting that the ViT's modular self-attention mechanism is uniquely suited for dissociating spatial priority from representational logic. These findings demonstrate that biologically grounded priors can be instilled as a free emergent property of human-aligned attention, to improve transformer interpretability.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning ViT-B/16 on human saliency maps improves cognitive alignment with human attention patterns without sacrificing classification performance, while the same approach degrades CNN performance.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers (ViTs) process images differently from human attention patterns, creating a cognitive gap. The researchers investigate whether this gap can be reduced by fine-tuning ViTs on human saliency data while maintaining classification performance.
AI.method: Fine-tuned Google's ViT-B/16 on human saliency fixation maps, compared against a shuffled control to isolate semantic effects. Also applied equivalent procedure to ResNet-50 CNN for comparison. Used Bayesian parity analysis to evaluate performance trade-offs.
AI.result: Fine-tuning significantly improved alignment across five saliency metrics and induced three human-like biases: reversed anti-human large-object bias, amplified animacy preference, and diminished extreme attention entropy. Bayesian analysis showed no cost to classification performance on ImageNet, ImageNet-C, and ObjectNet. The same procedure degraded both alignment and accuracy for ResNet-50.
AI.conclusion: ViT's modular self-attention mechanism is uniquely suited for separating spatial priority from representational logic, allowing biologically grounded priors to be instilled as a free emergent property that improves transformer interpretability without performance cost.

title: On the Quantization Robustness of Diffusion Language Models in Coding Benchmarks
summary: Auto-regressive Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on coding tasks, but incur high memory and inference costs. Diffusion-based language models (d-LLMs) offer bounded inference cost via iterative denoising, but their behavior under post-training quantization (PTQ) has been sparsely explored. We investigate the application and robustness of PTQ techniques, specifically GPTQ and a modified Hessian-Aware Quantization (HAWQ) algorithm, on a diffusion-based coding LLM (CoDA) and observe that these methods applied to CoDA exhibit greater robustness at low bitwidths compared to Qwen3-1.7B, its auto-regressive counterpart, under a standardized evaluation pipeline. We find that in our setup, CoDA exhibits greater robustness at low bitwidths (2-4 bits), with smaller accuracy degradation across HumanEval and MBPP benchmarks. Additionally, mixed-precision configurations derived from HAWQ provide smooth trade-offs across accuracy, latency, and memory. The results suggest that diffusion LLMs may offer advantages for efficient deployment due to more quantization-resilience.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based language models (d-LLMs) show greater robustness to post-training quantization at low bitwidths compared to auto-regressive LLMs, making them potentially better for efficient deployment.
AI.motivation: Auto-regressive LLMs have high memory and inference costs, while diffusion-based LLMs offer bounded inference costs. However, the quantization behavior of diffusion LLMs hasn't been well explored, especially for coding tasks.
AI.method: Applied GPTQ and modified Hessian-Aware Quantization (HAWQ) to CoDA (diffusion-based coding LLM) and Qwen3-1.7B (auto-regressive counterpart) using a standardized evaluation pipeline on HumanEval and MBPP benchmarks.
AI.result: CoDA exhibited greater robustness at low bitwidths (2-4 bits) with smaller accuracy degradation compared to Qwen3-1.7B. HAWQ mixed-precision configurations provided smooth trade-offs across accuracy, latency, and memory.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion LLMs may offer advantages for efficient deployment due to their greater quantization-resilience, making them promising for resource-constrained environments.

title: Large Language Models Meet Biomedical Knowledge Graphs for Mechanistically Grounded Therapeutic Prioritization
summary: Drug repurposing is often framed as a candidate identification task, but existing approaches provide limited guidance for distinguishing biologically plausible candidates from historically well-connected ones. Here we introduce DrugKLM, a hybrid framework that integrates biomedical knowledge graph structure with large language model-based mechanistic reasoning to enable mechanistically grounded therapeutic prioritization. Across benchmark datasets, DrugKLM outperforms knowledge graph-only and language model-only baselines, including TxGNN. Beyond improved recall, DrugKLM confidence scores exhibit functional alignment with molecular phenotypes: higher scores are associated with transcriptional signatures linked to improved survival across 12 TCGA cancers. The scoring framework preferentially captures biologically perturbational signals rather than historical indication patterns. Expert curation across five cancers further reveals systematic differences in prioritization behavior, with DrugKLM elevating candidates supported by coherent mechanistic rationale and disease-specific clinical context. Together, these results establish DrugKLM as an evidence-integrative framework that translates heterogeneous biomedical data into mechanistically interpretable and clinically grounded therapeutic hypotheses.

AI.tldr: DrugKLM is a hybrid framework combining knowledge graphs and LLMs for drug repurposing that prioritizes biologically plausible candidates over historically well-connected ones.
AI.motivation: Existing drug repurposing approaches provide limited guidance for distinguishing biologically plausible candidates from historically well-connected ones, lacking mechanistic reasoning for therapeutic prioritization.
AI.method: DrugKLM integrates biomedical knowledge graph structure with large language model-based mechanistic reasoning to create a hybrid framework for mechanistically grounded therapeutic prioritization.
AI.result: DrugKLM outperforms knowledge graph-only and language model-only baselines (including TxGNN), shows functional alignment with molecular phenotypes, captures perturbational signals over historical patterns, and elevates candidates with coherent mechanistic rationale in expert curation across five cancers.
AI.conclusion: DrugKLM establishes an evidence-integrative framework that translates heterogeneous biomedical data into mechanistically interpretable and clinically grounded therapeutic hypotheses for drug repurposing.

title: ALAS: Adaptive Long-Horizon Action Synthesis via Async-pathway Stream Disentanglement
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2508.07842
summary: Long-Horizon (LH) tasks in Human-Scene Interaction (HSI) are complex multi-step tasks that require continuous planning, sequential decision-making, and extended execution across domains to achieve the final goal. However, existing methods heavily rely on skill chaining by concatenating pre-trained subtasks, with environment observations and self-state tightly coupled, lacking the ability to generalize to new combinations of environments and skills, failing to complete various LH tasks across domains. To solve this problem, this paper presents ALAS, a cross-domain learning framework for LH tasks via biologically inspired dual-stream disentanglement. Inspired by the brain's "where-what" dual pathway mechanism, ALAS comprises two core modules: i) an environment learning module for spatial understanding, which captures object functions, spatial relationships, and scene semantics, achieving cross-domain transfer through complete environment-self disentanglement; ii) a skill learning module for task execution, which processes self-state information including joint degrees of freedom and motor patterns, enabling cross-skill transfer through independent motor pattern encoding. We conducted extensive experiments on various LH tasks in HSI scenes. Compared with existing methods, ALAS can achieve an average subtasks success rate improvement of 23\% and average execution efficiency improvement of 29\%.

AI.tldr: ALAS is a cross-domain learning framework for Long-Horizon Human-Scene Interaction tasks using biologically inspired dual-stream disentanglement to separate environment understanding from skill execution, achieving significant improvements in success rates and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for Long-Horizon HSI tasks rely on skill chaining with coupled environment-self observations, lacking generalization to new environment-skill combinations and failing to complete various cross-domain tasks.
AI.method: ALAS uses biologically inspired dual-stream disentanglement with two modules: 1) environment learning module for spatial understanding (object functions, spatial relationships, scene semantics) with complete environment-self disentanglement for cross-domain transfer; 2) skill learning module for task execution processing self-state information (joint DoF, motor patterns) with independent motor pattern encoding for cross-skill transfer.
AI.result: ALAS achieves average subtasks success rate improvement of 23% and average execution efficiency improvement of 29% compared to existing methods on various LH tasks in HSI scenes.
AI.conclusion: The biologically inspired dual-stream disentanglement approach effectively enables cross-domain learning for Long-Horizon HSI tasks by separating environment understanding from skill execution, leading to significant performance improvements.

title: Learning to count small and clustered objects with application to bacterial colonies
summary: Automated bacterial colony counting from images is an important technique to obtain data required for the development of vaccines and antibiotics. However, bacterial colonies present unique machine vision challenges that affect counting, including (1) small physical size, (2) object clustering, (3) high data annotation cost, and (4) limited cross-species generalisation. While FamNet is an established object counting technique effective for clustered objects and costly data annotation, its effectiveness for small colony sizes and cross-species generalisation remains unknown. To address the first three challenges, we propose ACFamNet, an extension of FamNet that handles small and clustered objects using a novel region of interest pooling with alignment and optimised feature engineering. To address all four challenges above, we introduce ACFamNet Pro, which augments ACFamNet with multi-head attention and residual connections, enabling dynamic weighting of objects and improved gradient flow. Experiments show that ACFamNet Pro achieves a mean normalised absolute error (MNAE) of 9.64% under 5-fold cross-validation, outperforming ACFamNet and FamNet by 2.23% and 12.71%, respectively.

AI.tldr: ACFamNet Pro improves bacterial colony counting by addressing small size, clustering, annotation costs, and cross-species generalization through attention mechanisms and residual connections.
AI.motivation: Bacterial colony counting faces challenges: small size, object clustering, high annotation costs, and limited cross-species generalization. Existing methods like FamNet don't adequately handle small colonies and cross-species transfer.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) ACFamNet extends FamNet with ROI pooling with alignment and optimized feature engineering for small/clustered objects; (2) ACFamNet Pro adds multi-head attention and residual connections for dynamic weighting and improved gradient flow.
AI.result: ACFamNet Pro achieves 9.64% MNAE under 5-fold cross-validation, outperforming ACFamNet (11.87%) and FamNet (22.35%) by 2.23% and 12.71% respectively.
AI.conclusion: ACFamNet Pro effectively addresses all four bacterial colony counting challenges, demonstrating superior performance through attention mechanisms and architectural improvements over existing methods.

title: Concept Graph Convolutions: Message Passing in the Concept Space
summary: The trust in the predictions of Graph Neural Networks is limited by their opaque reasoning process. Prior methods have tried to explain graph networks via concept-based explanations extracted from the latent representations obtained after message passing. However, these explanations fall short of explaining the message passing process itself. To this aim, we propose the Concept Graph Convolution, the first graph convolution designed to operate on node-level concepts for improved interpretability. The proposed convolutional layer performs message passing on a combination of raw and concept representations using structural and attention-based edge weights. We also propose a pure variant of the convolution, only operating in the concept space. Our results show that the Concept Graph Convolution allows to obtain competitive task accuracy, while enabling an increased insight into the evolution of concepts across convolutional steps.

AI.tldr: Proposes Concept Graph Convolution - a novel graph convolution layer that operates on node-level concepts for improved interpretability, enabling insight into concept evolution across convolutional steps while maintaining competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current GNN explanations are limited - they explain latent representations after message passing but fail to explain the message passing process itself. There's a need for more transparent reasoning in graph neural networks.
AI.method: Introduces Concept Graph Convolution that performs message passing on both raw and concept representations using structural and attention-based edge weights. Also proposes a pure variant operating only in concept space.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive task accuracy while providing increased insight into how concepts evolve across convolutional steps, addressing the interpretability gap in GNNs.
AI.conclusion: Concept Graph Convolution enables both effective graph learning and improved interpretability by operating on node-level concepts, offering a more transparent alternative to traditional graph convolutions.

title: Emergence Transformer: Dynamical Temporal Attention Matters
summary: The Transformer, a breakthrough architecture in artificial intelligence, owes its success to the attention mechanism, which utilizes long-range interactions in sequential data, enabling the emergent coherence between large language models (LLMs) and data distributions. However, temporal attention, that is, different forms of long-range interactions in temporal sequences, has rarely been explored in emergence phenomenon of complex systems including oscillatory coherence in quantum, biophysical, or climate systems. Here, by designing dynamical temporal attention (DTA) with time-varying query, key, and value matrices, we propose an Emergence Transformer. This architecture allows each component to interact with its own or its neighbors' past states through dynamical attention kernels, thereby enabling the promotion and/or suppression of the emergent coherence of components. Interestingly, we uncover that neighbor-DTA consistently promotes oscillatory coherence, whereas self-DTA exhibits an optimal attention weight for coherence enhancement, owing to its non-monotonic dependence on network structure. Practically, we demonstrate how DTA reshapes social coherence, suggesting strategies to either enhance agreement or preserve plurality. We further apply DTA to the paradigmatic Hopfield neural network, achieving emergent continual learning without catastrophic forgetting. Together, these results lay a foundation and provide an immediate paradigm for modulating emergence phenomenon in networked dynamics only using DTA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an Emergence Transformer with dynamical temporal attention (DTA) to modulate emergent coherence in complex systems, showing applications in social dynamics and neural networks.
AI.motivation: While attention mechanisms have revolutionized AI, temporal attention for long-range interactions in complex systems (quantum, biophysical, climate) remains unexplored for emergence phenomena like oscillatory coherence.
AI.method: Design dynamical temporal attention (DTA) with time-varying query, key, and value matrices to create an Emergence Transformer. This allows components to interact with past states through dynamical attention kernels, enabling promotion/suppression of emergent coherence.
AI.result: Neighbor-DTA consistently promotes oscillatory coherence, while self-DTA shows optimal attention weight for coherence enhancement with non-monotonic network dependence. DTA reshapes social coherence and enables emergent continual learning in Hopfield networks without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: The Emergence Transformer with DTA provides a foundational paradigm for modulating emergence phenomena in networked dynamics, offering practical strategies for coherence control in complex systems.

title: A Hough transform approach to safety-aware scalar field mapping using Gaussian Processes
summary: This paper presents a framework for mapping unknown scalar fields using a sensor-equipped autonomous robot operating in unsafe environments. The unsafe regions are defined as regions of high-intensity, where the field value exceeds a predefined safety threshold. For safe and efficient mapping of the scalar field, the sensor-equipped robot must avoid high-intensity regions during the measurement process. In this paper, the scalar field is modeled as a sample from a Gaussian process (GP), which enables Bayesian inference and provides closed-form expressions for both the predictive mean and the uncertainty. Concurrently, the spatial structure of the high-intensity regions is estimated in real-time using the Hough transform (HT), leveraging the evolving GP posterior. A safe sampling strategy is then employed to guide the robot towards safe measurement locations, using probabilistic safety guarantees on the evolving GP posterior. The estimated high-intensity regions also facilitate the design of safe motion plans for the robot. The effectiveness of the approach is verified through two numerical simulation studies and an indoor experiment for mapping a light-intensity field using a wheeled mobile robot.

AI.tldr: A framework for safe autonomous mapping of unknown scalar fields in hazardous environments using Gaussian process modeling and real-time safety boundary estimation with Hough transform.
AI.motivation: To enable autonomous robots to safely map unknown scalar fields in environments with hazardous high-intensity regions that exceed safety thresholds, requiring avoidance during measurement.
AI.method: Models scalar field as Gaussian process for Bayesian inference, uses Hough transform for real-time estimation of high-intensity region boundaries from GP posterior, employs safe sampling strategy with probabilistic safety guarantees, and designs safe motion plans.
AI.result: Verified effectiveness through two numerical simulations and an indoor experiment using a wheeled mobile robot to map a light-intensity field.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully enables safe and efficient autonomous mapping of unknown scalar fields in hazardous environments by combining GP modeling, real-time safety boundary estimation, and probabilistic safety guarantees.

title: FluSplat: Sparse-View 3D Editing without Test-Time Optimization
summary:
Recent advances in text-guided image editing and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled high-quality 3D scene manipulation. However, existing pipelines rely on iterative edit-and-fit optimization at test time, alternating between 2D diffusion editing and 3D reconstruction. This process is computationally expensive, scene-specific, and prone to cross-view inconsistencies.
  We propose a feed-forward framework for cross-view consistent 3D scene editing from sparse views. Instead of enforcing consistency through iterative 3D refinement, we introduce a cross-view regularization scheme in the image domain during training. By jointly supervising multi-view edits with geometric alignment constraints, our model produces view-consistent results without per-scene optimization at inference. The edited views are then lifted into 3D via a feedforward 3DGS model, yielding a coherent 3DGS representation in a single forward pass.
  Experiments demonstrate competitive editing fidelity and substantially improved cross-view consistency compared to optimization-based methods, while reducing inference time by orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: A feed-forward framework for cross-view consistent 3D scene editing from sparse views that avoids iterative optimization, using cross-view regularization in image domain during training and feedforward 3DGS lifting.
AI.motivation: Existing text-guided 3D scene editing methods rely on iterative edit-and-fit optimization at test time, which is computationally expensive, scene-specific, and prone to cross-view inconsistencies. There's a need for a more efficient approach that produces consistent results without per-scene optimization.
AI.method: Proposes a feed-forward framework with cross-view regularization in the image domain during training. Uses joint supervision of multi-view edits with geometric alignment constraints to produce view-consistent results. Edited views are then lifted into 3D via a feedforward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) model in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Achieves competitive editing fidelity and substantially improved cross-view consistency compared to optimization-based methods, while reducing inference time by orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The proposed feed-forward framework enables efficient, cross-view consistent 3D scene editing without the computational burden of iterative optimization, making 3D scene manipulation more practical and scalable.

title: Energy-Based Open-Set Active Learning for Object Classification
comment: To be published in the 2026 International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR)
summary: Active learning (AL) has emerged as a crucial methodology for minimizing labeling costs in deep learning by selecting the most valuable samples from a pool of unlabeled data for annotation. Traditional AL operates under a closed-set assumption, where all classes in the dataset are known and consistent. However, real-world scenarios often present open-set conditions in which unlabeled data contains both known and unknown classes. In such environments, standard AL techniques struggle. They can mistakenly query samples from unknown categories, leading to inefficient use of annotation budgets. In this paper, we propose a novel dual-stage energy-based framework for open-set AL. Our method employs two specialized energy-based models (EBMs). The first, an energy-based known/unknown separator, filters out samples likely to belong to unknown classes. The second, an energy-based sample scorer, assesses the informativeness of the filtered known samples. Using the energy landscape, our models distinguish between data points from known and unknown classes in the unlabeled pool by assigning lower energy to known samples and higher energy to unknown samples, ensuring that only samples from classes of interest are selected for labeling. By integrating these components, our approach ensures efficient and targeted sample selection, maximizing learning impact in each iteration. Experiments on 2D (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet) and 3D (ModelNet40) object classification benchmarks demonstrates that our framework outperforms existing approaches, achieving superior annotation efficiency and classification performance in open-set environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes a dual-stage energy-based framework for open-set active learning that filters unknown classes and scores known samples for efficient annotation.
AI.motivation: Traditional active learning assumes closed-set conditions where all classes are known, but real-world scenarios often have open-set conditions with unknown classes mixed in. Standard AL methods waste annotation budget on unknown classes.
AI.method: A dual-stage energy-based framework with two specialized EBMs: (1) energy-based known/unknown separator to filter out unknown class samples, (2) energy-based sample scorer to assess informativeness of filtered known samples. Uses energy landscape where known samples get lower energy and unknown samples get higher energy.
AI.result: Outperforms existing approaches on 2D (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet) and 3D (ModelNet40) object classification benchmarks, achieving superior annotation efficiency and classification performance in open-set environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-stage energy-based framework effectively addresses open-set active learning challenges by preventing wasteful annotation of unknown classes and focusing annotation budget on informative known samples, leading to better efficiency and performance.

title: JTPRO: A Joint Tool-Prompt Reflective Optimization Framework for Language Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents augmented with external tools often struggle as number of tools grow large and become domain-specific. In such settings, ambiguous tool descriptions and under-specified agent instructions frequently lead to tool mis-selection and incorrect slot/value instantiation. We hypothesize that this is due to two root causes: generic, one-size-fits-all prompts that ignore tool-specific nuances, and underspecified tool schemas that lack clear guidance on when and how to use each tool and how to format its parameters. We introduce Joint Tool-Prompt Reflective Optimization (JTPRO), a framework for improving tool-calling reliability in trace-supervised settings by iteratively using rollout-driven reflection to co-optimize global instructions and per-tool schema/argument descriptions for accurate tool selection and argument instantiation in large tool inventories. JTPRO is designed to preserve only tool-local cues needed for correct disambiguation and slot filling. We evaluate JTPRO across multi-tool benchmarks, which account for different number of tools using three metrics: Tool Selection Accuracy (TSA), Slot Filling Accuracy(SFA), and Overall Success Rate(OSR) (correct tool + correct slots + correct values). JTPRO consistently outperforms strong baselines, including CoT-style agents, and reflective prompt optimizers such as GEPA by 5%-20% (relative) on OSR. Ablations show that joint optimization of instructions and tool schemas is more effective and robust than optimizing either component in isolation.

AI.tldr: JTPRO is a framework that jointly optimizes global instructions and per-tool schemas to improve LLM agent tool-calling reliability in large tool inventories.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle with large, domain-specific tool inventories due to ambiguous tool descriptions and underspecified instructions, leading to tool mis-selection and incorrect parameter instantiation.
AI.method: Joint Tool-Prompt Reflective Optimization (JTPRO) uses rollout-driven reflection to co-optimize global instructions and per-tool schema/argument descriptions, preserving only tool-local cues needed for disambiguation and slot filling.
AI.result: JTPRO outperforms strong baselines (CoT-style agents, GEPA) by 5%-20% relative improvement on Overall Success Rate across multi-tool benchmarks, with joint optimization proving more effective than optimizing either component alone.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of instructions and tool schemas is crucial for reliable tool-calling in large tool inventories, addressing the limitations of generic prompts and underspecified schemas.

title: PokeVLA: Empowering Pocket-Sized Vision-Language-Action Model with Comprehensive World Knowledge Guidance
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have opened new avenues for robot manipulation, yet existing methods exhibit limited efficiency and a lack of high-level knowledge and spatial awareness. To address these challenges, we propose PokeVLA, a lightweight yet powerful foundation model for embodied manipulation that effectively infuses vision-language understanding into action learning. Our framework introduces a two-stage training paradigm: first, we pre-train a compact vision-language model (PokeVLM) on a curated multimodal dataset of 2.4M samples encompassing spatial grounding, affordance, and embodied reasoning tasks; second, we inject manipulation-relevant representations into the action space through multi-view goal-aware semantics learning, geometry alignment, and a novel action expert. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO-Plus benchmark and in real-world deployment, outperforming comparable baselines in success rate and robustness under diverse perturbations. To foster reproducibility and community progress, we will open-source our code, model weights, and the scripts for the curated pre-training dataset. Project page: https://getterupper.github.io/PokeVLA

AI.tldr: PokeVLA is a lightweight vision-language-action foundation model for robot manipulation that combines vision-language understanding with action learning through a two-stage training approach, achieving SOTA performance on benchmarks and real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models for robot manipulation have limited efficiency, lack high-level knowledge, and have poor spatial awareness. There's a need for a more effective model that can better integrate vision-language understanding with action learning.
AI.method: Two-stage training: 1) Pre-train a compact vision-language model (PokeVLM) on 2.4M multimodal samples covering spatial grounding, affordance, and embodied reasoning tasks. 2) Inject manipulation-relevant representations into action space using multi-view goal-aware semantics learning, geometry alignment, and a novel action expert.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO-Plus benchmark and real-world deployment, outperforming comparable baselines in success rate and robustness under diverse perturbations.
AI.conclusion: PokeVLA effectively addresses efficiency and spatial awareness limitations in VLA models for robot manipulation through its lightweight architecture and two-stage training paradigm, demonstrating strong performance in both simulated and real-world settings.

title: Normalizing Flows with Iterative Denoising
summary:
Normalizing Flows (NFs) are a classical family of likelihood-based methods that have received revived attention. Recent efforts such as TARFlow have shown that
  NFs are capable of achieving promising performance on image modeling tasks, making them viable alternatives to other methods such as diffusion models.
  In this work, we further advance the state of Normalizing Flow generative models by introducing iterative TARFlow (iTARFlow). Unlike diffusion models, iTARFlow maintains a fully end-to-end, likelihood-based objective during training. During sampling, it performs autoregressive generation followed by an iterative denoising procedure inspired by diffusion-style methods. Through extensive experiments, we show that iTARFlow achieves competitive performance across ImageNet resolutions of 64, 128, and 256 pixels, demonstrating its potential as a strong generative model and advancing the frontier of Normalizing Flows. In addition, we analyze the characteristic artifacts produced by iTARFlow, offering insights that may shed light on future improvements. Code is available at https://github.com/apple/ml-itarflow.

AI.tldr: iTARFlow advances Normalizing Flows with iterative denoising, achieving competitive image generation performance on ImageNet at multiple resolutions while maintaining end-to-end likelihood training.
AI.motivation: To advance Normalizing Flow generative models beyond recent TARFlow improvements, making them more competitive with diffusion models while maintaining likelihood-based training advantages.
AI.method: Introduces iterative TARFlow (iTARFlow) that combines autoregressive generation with iterative denoising inspired by diffusion models, while keeping end-to-end likelihood-based training.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance on ImageNet at 64x64, 128x128, and 256x256 resolutions, demonstrating strong generative capabilities and advancing Normalizing Flow frontiers.
AI.conclusion: iTARFlow shows Normalizing Flows can be viable alternatives to diffusion models, with analysis of characteristic artifacts providing insights for future improvements.

title: Differentiable Conformal Training for LLM Reasoning Factuality
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently hallucinate, limiting their reliability in critical applications. Conformal Prediction (CP) addresses this by calibrating error rates on held-out data to provide statistically valid confidence guarantees. Recent work extends CP to LLM factuality to filter out risky claims, ensuring that hallucination rates remain below a user-specified level (e.g., 10%). While prior methods treat claims independently, Coherent Factuality extends to multi-step reasoning by representing outputs as dependency graphs and jointly validating claims with their logical ancestors. A key limitation is that Coherent Factuality is not differentiable, requiring hand-crafted scorers that at high reliability levels remove nearly 60% of true claims. We introduce Differentiable Coherent Factuality (DCF), a fully differentiable relaxation that enables learning improved scorers while provably recovering the original algorithm's guarantees. Experiments on two benchmark reasoning datasets demonstrate DCF achieves up to 141% improvement in claim retention while maintaining reliability guarantees, representing a significant step towards reliable conformal LLM systems.

AI.tldr: DCF introduces a differentiable version of Coherent Factuality for LLM hallucination detection, improving claim retention by up to 141% while maintaining statistical reliability guarantees.
AI.motivation: Current conformal prediction methods for LLM factuality are not differentiable and require hand-crafted scorers, which at high reliability levels remove nearly 60% of true claims, limiting their practical utility.
AI.method: Differentiable Coherent Factuality (DCF) provides a fully differentiable relaxation of Coherent Factuality that enables learning improved scorers while provably recovering the original algorithm's statistical guarantees.
AI.result: Experiments on two benchmark reasoning datasets show DCF achieves up to 141% improvement in claim retention while maintaining reliability guarantees.
AI.conclusion: DCF represents a significant step towards reliable conformal LLM systems by making hallucination detection differentiable and learnable while preserving statistical guarantees.

title: Forage V2: Knowledge Evolution and Transfer in Autonomous Agent Organizations
summary:
Autonomous agents operating in open-world tasks -- where the completion boundary is not given in advance -- face denominator blindness: they systematically underestimate the scope of the target space. Forage V1 addressed this through co-evolving evaluation (an independent Evaluator discovers what "complete" means) and method isolation (Evaluator and Planner cannot see each other's code). V2 extends the architecture from a single expedition to a learning organization: experience accumulates across runs, transfers across model capabilities, and institutional safeguards prevent knowledge degradation.
  We demonstrate two claims across three task types (web scraping, API queries, mathematical reasoning). Knowledge accumulation: over six runs, knowledge entries grow from 0 to 54, and denominator estimates stabilize as domain understanding deepens. Knowledge transfer: a weaker agent (Sonnet) seeded with a stronger agent's (Opus) knowledge narrows a 6.6pp coverage gap to 1.1pp, halves cost (9.40 to 5.13 USD), converges in half the rounds (mean 4.5 vs. 7.0), and three independent seeded runs arrive at exactly the same denominator estimate (266), suggesting organizational knowledge calibrates evaluation itself.
  V2's contribution is architectural: it designs institutions -- audit separation, contract protocols, organizational memory -- that make any agent more reliable upon entry. The accumulated experience is organizational, model-agnostic, and transferable, stored as readable documents that any future agent inherits regardless of provider or capability level.

AI.tldr: Forage V2 extends autonomous agents from single expeditions to learning organizations with institutional memory that accumulates knowledge across runs and transfers between agents, solving denominator blindness in open-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Autonomous agents in open-world tasks suffer from denominator blindness - systematically underestimating the scope of target spaces because completion boundaries aren't predefined. Forage V1 addressed this with co-evolving evaluation and method isolation, but V2 aims to create learning organizations that accumulate knowledge across runs.
AI.method: V2 extends the architecture from single expeditions to learning organizations with: 1) Experience accumulation across runs, 2) Knowledge transfer across model capabilities, 3) Institutional safeguards against knowledge degradation, 4) Audit separation, contract protocols, and organizational memory, 5) Model-agnostic knowledge storage as readable documents.
AI.result: Knowledge entries grew from 0 to 54 over six runs with stabilized denominator estimates. Knowledge transfer from stronger (Opus) to weaker (Sonnet) agents narrowed coverage gap from 6.6pp to 1.1pp, halved cost ($9.40 to $5.13), converged in half the rounds (4.5 vs 7.0), and three independent seeded runs arrived at identical denominator estimates (266).
AI.conclusion: Forage V2's architectural contribution creates institutions that make any agent more reliable through organizational, model-agnostic, transferable knowledge. The accumulated experience calibrates evaluation itself and can be inherited by future agents regardless of provider or capability level.

title: Gaussians on a Diet: High-Quality Memory-Bounded 3D Gaussian Splatting Training
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has revolutionized novel view synthesis with high-quality rendering through continuous aggregations of millions of 3D Gaussian primitives. However, it suffers from a substantial memory footprint, particularly during training due to uncontrolled densification, posing a critical bottleneck for deployment on memory-constrained edge devices. While existing methods prune redundant Gaussians post-training, they fail to address the peak memory spikes caused by the abrupt growth of Gaussians early in the training process. To solve the training memory consumption problem, we propose a systematic memory-bounded training framework that dynamically optimizes Gaussians through iterative growth and pruning. In other words, the proposed framework alternates between incremental pruning of low-impact Gaussians and strategic growing of new primitives with an adaptive Gaussian compensation, maintaining a near-constant low memory usage while progressively refining rendering fidelity. We comprehensively evaluate the proposed training framework on various real-world datasets under strict memory constraints, showing significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods. Particularly, our proposed method practically enables memory-efficient 3DGS training on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier, achieving similar visual quality with up to 80% lower peak training memory consumption than the original 3DGS.

AI.tldr: Memory-bounded training framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting that maintains constant low memory usage through iterative growth and pruning, enabling deployment on edge devices.
AI.motivation: 3DGS suffers from substantial memory footprint during training due to uncontrolled densification, creating a critical bottleneck for deployment on memory-constrained edge devices. Existing post-training pruning methods fail to address peak memory spikes during training.
AI.method: Proposes a systematic memory-bounded training framework that dynamically optimizes Gaussians through iterative growth and pruning. Alternates between incremental pruning of low-impact Gaussians and strategic growing of new primitives with adaptive Gaussian compensation.
AI.result: Achieves similar visual quality with up to 80% lower peak training memory consumption than original 3DGS. Enables memory-efficient 3DGS training on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier under strict memory constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the training memory consumption problem in 3DGS, making it practical for deployment on memory-constrained edge devices while maintaining rendering quality.

title: Learning to Solve the Quadratic Assignment Problem with Warm-Started MCMC Finetuning
summary: The quadratic assignment problem (QAP) is a fundamental NP-hard task that poses significant challenges for both traditional heuristics and modern learning-based solvers. Existing QAP solvers still struggle to achieve consistently competitive performance across structurally diverse real-world instances. To bridge this performance gap, we propose PLMA, an innovative permutation learning framework. PLMA features an efficient warm-started MCMC finetuning procedure to enhance deployment-time performance, leveraging short Markov chains to anchor the adaptation to the promising regions previously explored. For rapid exploration via MCMC over the permutation space, we design an additive energy-based model (EBM) that enables an $O(1)$-time 2-swap Metropolis-Hastings sampling step. Moreover, the neural network used to parameterize the EBM incorporates a scalable and flexible cross-graph attention mechanism to model interactions between facilities and locations in the QAP. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PLMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across various benchmarks. In particular, PLMA achieves a near-zero average optimality gap on QAPLIB, exhibits remarkably superior robustness on the notoriously difficult Taixxeyy instances, and also serves as an effective QAP solver in bandwidth minimization.

AI.tldr: PLMA is a permutation learning framework with MCMC finetuning that outperforms state-of-the-art QAP solvers across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing QAP solvers struggle with consistently competitive performance across structurally diverse real-world instances, creating a performance gap that needs to be addressed.
AI.method: PLMA uses an efficient warm-started MCMC finetuning procedure with short Markov chains, an additive energy-based model enabling O(1)-time 2-swap Metropolis-Hastings sampling, and a neural network with cross-graph attention to model facility-location interactions.
AI.result: PLMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieves near-zero average optimality gap on QAPLIB, shows superior robustness on difficult Taixxeyy instances, and effectively solves bandwidth minimization problems.
AI.conclusion: PLMA represents an innovative permutation learning framework that successfully bridges the performance gap in QAP solving through efficient MCMC finetuning and neural network modeling.

title: Resolving space-sharing conflicts in road user interactions through uncertainty reduction: An active inference-based computational model
summary: Understanding how road users resolve space-sharing conflicts is important both for traffic safety and the safe deployment of autonomous vehicles. While existing models have captured specific aspects of such interactions (e.g., explicit communication), a theoretically-grounded computational framework has been lacking. In this paper, we extend a previously developed active inference-based driver behavior model to simulate interactive behavior of two agents. Our model captures three complementary mechanisms for uncertainty reduction in interaction: (i) implicit communication via direct behavioral coupling, (ii) reliance on normative expectations (stop signs, priority rules, etc.), and (iii) explicit communication. In a simplified intersection scenario, we show that normative and explicit communication cues can increase the likelihood of a successful conflict resolution. However, this relies on agents acting as expected. In situations where another agent (intentionally or unintentionally) violates normative expectations or communicates misleading information, reliance on these cues may induce collisions. These findings illustrate how active inference can provide a novel framework for modeling road user interactions which is also applicable in other fields.

AI.tldr: Active inference model for road user interactions captures three uncertainty reduction mechanisms: implicit behavioral coupling, normative expectations, and explicit communication, showing how these can help or hinder conflict resolution.
AI.motivation: Understanding how road users resolve space-sharing conflicts is crucial for traffic safety and autonomous vehicle deployment. Existing models capture specific aspects but lack a theoretically-grounded computational framework.
AI.method: Extend an active inference-based driver behavior model to simulate interactive behavior of two agents, capturing three complementary mechanisms for uncertainty reduction: implicit communication via behavioral coupling, reliance on normative expectations, and explicit communication.
AI.result: In a simplified intersection scenario, normative and explicit communication cues increase likelihood of successful conflict resolution, but only when agents act as expected. When expectations are violated or misleading information is communicated, reliance on these cues may induce collisions.
AI.conclusion: Active inference provides a novel framework for modeling road user interactions that captures multiple uncertainty reduction mechanisms, with applications beyond traffic to other fields requiring conflict resolution.

title: PASTA: A Patch-Agnostic Twofold-Stealthy Backdoor Attack on Vision Transformers
summary:
Vision Transformers (ViTs) have achieved remarkable success across vision tasks, yet recent studies show they remain vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing patch-wise attacks typically assume a single fixed trigger location during inference to maximize trigger attention. However, they overlook the self-attention mechanism in ViTs, which captures long-range dependencies across patches. In this work, we observe that a patch-wise trigger can achieve high attack effectiveness when activating backdoors across neighboring patches, a phenomenon we term the Trigger Radiating Effect (TRE). We further find that inter-patch trigger insertion during training can synergistically enhance TRE compared to single-patch insertion. Prior ViT-specific attacks that maximize trigger attention often sacrifice visual and attention stealthiness, making them detectable.
  Based on these insights, we propose PASTA, a twofold stealthy patch-wise backdoor attack in both pixel and attention domains. PASTA enables backdoor activation when the trigger is placed at arbitrary patches during inference. To achieve this, we introduce a multi-location trigger insertion strategy to enhance TRE. However, preserving stealthiness while maintaining strong TRE is challenging, as TRE is weakened under stealthy constraints. We therefore formulate a bi-level optimization problem and propose an adaptive backdoor learning framework, where the model and trigger iteratively adapt to each other to avoid local optima. Extensive experiments show that PASTA achieves 99.13% attack success rate across arbitrary patches on average, while significantly improving visual and attention stealthiness (144.43x and 18.68x) and robustness (2.79x) against state-of-the-art ViT defenses across four datasets, outperforming CNN- and ViT-based baselines.

AI.tldr: PASTA is a stealthy patch-wise backdoor attack for Vision Transformers that works with triggers placed at arbitrary patches during inference, achieving high attack success while maintaining visual and attention stealthiness.
AI.motivation: Existing ViT backdoor attacks assume fixed trigger locations and sacrifice stealthiness to maximize trigger attention, overlooking the self-attention mechanism's ability to capture long-range dependencies across patches.
AI.method: Proposes PASTA with multi-location trigger insertion to enhance Trigger Radiating Effect (TRE), formulated as a bi-level optimization problem with adaptive backdoor learning where model and trigger iteratively adapt to each other.
AI.result: Achieves 99.13% attack success rate across arbitrary patches, with 144.43x visual stealthiness, 18.68x attention stealthiness improvement, and 2.79x robustness against state-of-the-art ViT defenses across four datasets.
AI.conclusion: PASTA demonstrates that patch-wise backdoor attacks can be both effective and stealthy in Vision Transformers by leveraging the Trigger Radiating Effect through adaptive optimization, outperforming existing CNN- and ViT-based attacks.

title: Meta Additive Model: Interpretable Sparse Learning With Auto Weighting
summary: Sparse additive models have attracted much attention in high-dimensional data analysis due to their flexible representation and strong interpretability. However, most existing models are limited to single-level learning under the mean-squared error criterion, whose empirical performance can degrade significantly in the presence of complex noise, such as non-Gaussian perturbations, outliers, noisy labels, and imbalanced categories. The sample reweighting strategy is widely used to reduce the model's sensitivity to atypical data; however, it typically requires prespecifying the weighting functions and manually selecting additional hyperparameters. To address this issue, we propose a new meta additive model (MAM) based on the bilevel optimization framework, which learns data-driven weighting of individual losses by parameterizing the weighting function via an MLP trained on meta data. MAM is capable of a variety of learning tasks, including variable selection, robust regression estimation, and imbalanced classification. Theoretically, MAM provides guarantees on convergence in computation, algorithmic generalization, and variable selection consistency under mild conditions. Empirically, MAM outperforms several state-of-the-art additive models on both synthetic and real-world data under various data corruptions.

AI.tldr: Meta Additive Model (MAM) uses bilevel optimization with MLP-based weighting to handle complex noise in high-dimensional data, outperforming existing additive models.
AI.motivation: Existing sparse additive models are limited to single-level learning under mean-squared error, which degrades with complex noise like outliers, non-Gaussian perturbations, noisy labels, and imbalanced categories. Sample reweighting strategies require prespecified weighting functions and manual hyperparameter tuning.
AI.method: Proposes Meta Additive Model (MAM) based on bilevel optimization framework that learns data-driven weighting of individual losses by parameterizing the weighting function via an MLP trained on meta data. This enables automatic weighting without manual specification.
AI.result: Theoretically provides guarantees on convergence in computation, algorithmic generalization, and variable selection consistency under mild conditions. Empirically outperforms several state-of-the-art additive models on both synthetic and real-world data under various data corruptions.
AI.conclusion: MAM is a flexible framework capable of handling variable selection, robust regression estimation, and imbalanced classification while being robust to various data corruptions through learned data-driven weighting.

title: Deconstructing Superintelligence: Identity, Self-Modification and Différance
summary: Self-modification is often taken as constitutive of artificial superintelligence (SI), yet modification is a relative action requiring a supplement outside the operation. When self-modification extends to this supplement, the classical self-referential structure collapses. We formalise this on an associative operator algebra $\mathcal{A}$ with update $\hat{U}$, discrimination $\hat{D}$, and self-representation $\hat{R}$, identifying the supplement with $\mathrm{Comm}(\hat{U})$; an expansion theorem shows that $[\hat{U},\hat{R}]$ decomposes through $[\hat{U},\hat{D}]$, so non-commutation generically propagates. The liar paradox appears as a commutator collapse $[\hat{T},Π_L]=0$, and class $\mathbf{A}$ self-modification realises the same collapse at system scale, yielding a structure coinciding with Priest's inclosure schema and Derrida's diffèrance.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes self-modification in artificial superintelligence, showing that when self-modification extends to its own external supplement, it creates paradoxes similar to the liar paradox, formalized through operator algebra and commutator collapse.
AI.motivation: To examine the foundational paradoxes that arise when artificial superintelligence attempts self-modification, particularly when such modification extends to the very external conditions that enable modification itself.
AI.method: Formalization using associative operator algebra with update (Ũ), discrimination (D̂), and self-representation (R̂) operators. The supplement is identified with Comm(Ũ), and an expansion theorem shows non-commutation propagates through the system.
AI.result: Self-modification leads to commutator collapse [Ũ,R̂] = 0, analogous to the liar paradox [T̂,Π_L] = 0. This collapse creates structures matching Priest's inclosure schema and Derrida's diffèrance.
AI.conclusion: Self-modification in artificial superintelligence inherently contains paradoxes when it attempts to modify its own enabling conditions, revealing fundamental limitations in classical self-referential structures.

title: From Scene to Object: Text-Guided Dual-Gaze Prediction
summary: Interpretable driver attention prediction is crucial for human-like autonomous driving. However, existing datasets provide only scene-level global gaze rather than fine-grained object-level annotations, inherently failing to support text-grounded cognitive modeling.
