AI.motivation: Fault location in distribution grids is challenging due to partial observability from sparse measurements. While RNNs and GNNs show promise, many modern GNN architectures remain untested, and existing approaches haven't explored alternative GNN topology definitions beyond simply using the full grid topology.
AI.method: The authors: (1) systematically compare a new "measured-only" graph-forming strategy to traditional full-topology approach, (2) introduce STGNN models based on GraphSAGE and improved Graph Attention (GATv2), and (3) benchmark them against state-of-the-art STGNN and RNN baselines on the IEEE 123-bus feeder.
AI.result: All STGNN variants achieve high performance and consistently outperform pure RNN baselines (up to 11 percentage points F1 improvement). STGNNs show superior stability with tight confidence intervals (±1.4%) vs RNN (±7.5%). The measured-only graph topology reduces training time 6-fold and improves performance up to 11 points F1.
AI.conclusion: Measured-only graphs offer a more practical, efficient, and robust framework for fault location in partially observable distribution grids, demonstrating clear benefits over traditional full-topology approaches.

title: Diagnosing CFG Interpretation in LLMs
summary: As LLMs are increasingly integrated into agentic systems, they must adhere to dynamically defined, machine-interpretable interfaces. We evaluate LLMs as in-context interpreters: given a novel context-free grammar, can LLMs generate syntactically valid, behaviorally functional, and semantically faithful outputs? We introduce RoboGrid, a framework that disentangles syntax, behavior, and semantics through controlled stress-tests of recursion depth, expression complexity, and surface styles. Our experiments reveal a consistent hierarchical degradation: LLMs often maintain surface syntax but fail to preserve structural semantics. Despite the partial mitigation provided by CoT reasoning, performance collapses under structural density, specifically deep recursion and high branching, with semantic alignment vanishing at extreme depths. Furthermore, "Alien" lexicons reveal that LLMs rely on semantic bootstrapping from keywords rather than pure symbolic induction. These findings pinpoint critical gaps in hierarchical state-tracking required for reliable, grammar-agnostic agents.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle with hierarchical state-tracking when interpreting novel context-free grammars, showing consistent failure patterns in deep recursion and complex branching despite maintaining surface syntax.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in agentic systems, they need to adhere to dynamically defined, machine-interpretable interfaces. The paper aims to evaluate whether LLMs can effectively interpret novel context-free grammars to generate syntactically valid, behaviorally functional, and semantically faithful outputs.
AI.method: Introduces RoboGrid framework that disentangles syntax, behavior, and semantics through controlled stress-tests. Tests include recursion depth, expression complexity, and surface styles. Uses CoT (Chain-of-Thought) reasoning and "Alien" lexicons to examine semantic bootstrapping vs. pure symbolic induction.
AI.result: LLMs show consistent hierarchical degradation: they often maintain surface syntax but fail to preserve structural semantics. CoT reasoning provides partial mitigation but performance collapses under structural density (deep recursion and high branching). Semantic alignment vanishes at extreme depths. "Alien" lexicons reveal LLMs rely on semantic bootstrapping from keywords rather than pure symbolic induction.
AI.conclusion: The findings pinpoint critical gaps in hierarchical state-tracking required for reliable, grammar-agnostic agents. LLMs' current limitations in handling complex structural patterns highlight challenges for their use in agentic systems requiring robust grammar interpretation.

title: SignDATA: Data Pipeline for Sign Language Translation
summary: Sign-language datasets are difficult to preprocess consistently because they vary in annotation schema, clip timing, signer framing, and privacy constraints. Existing work usually reports downstream models, while the preprocessing pipeline that converts raw video into training-ready pose or video artifacts remains fragmented, backend-specific, and weakly documented. We present SignDATA, a config-driven preprocessing toolkit that standardizes heterogeneous sign-language corpora into comparable outputs for learning. The system supports two end-to-end recipes: a pose recipe that performs acquisition, manifesting, person localization, clipping, cropping, landmark extraction, normalization, and WebDataset export, and a video recipe that replaces pose extraction with signer-cropped video packaging. SignDATA exposes interchangeable MediaPipe and MMPose backends behind a common interface, typed job schemas, experiment-level overrides, and per-stage checkpointing with config- and manifest-aware hashes. We validate the toolkit through a research-oriented evaluation design centered on backend comparison, preprocessing ablations, and privacy-aware video generation on datasets. Our contribution is a reproducible preprocessing layer for sign-language research that makes extractor choice, normalization policy, and privacy tradeoffs explicit, configurable, and empirically comparable.Code is available at https://github.com/balaboom123/signdata-slt.

AI.tldr: SignDATA is a config-driven preprocessing toolkit that standardizes heterogeneous sign-language datasets into comparable outputs for machine learning, supporting both pose and video processing pipelines with interchangeable backends and reproducible workflows.
AI.motivation: Sign-language datasets are difficult to preprocess consistently due to variations in annotation schema, clip timing, signer framing, and privacy constraints. Existing preprocessing pipelines are fragmented, backend-specific, and poorly documented, making it hard to compare results across studies.
AI.method: SignDATA provides a config-driven preprocessing toolkit with two end-to-end recipes: 1) pose recipe (acquisition, manifesting, person localization, clipping, cropping, landmark extraction, normalization, WebDataset export), and 2) video recipe (signer-cropped video packaging). The system features interchangeable MediaPipe and MMPose backends, typed job schemas, experiment-level overrides, and per-stage checkpointing with config-aware hashes.
AI.result: The toolkit is validated through research-oriented evaluation including backend comparison, preprocessing ablations, and privacy-aware video generation on datasets. It provides reproducible preprocessing that makes extractor choice, normalization policy, and privacy tradeoffs explicit and empirically comparable.
AI.conclusion: SignDATA contributes a reproducible preprocessing layer for sign-language research that addresses the fragmentation in existing pipelines, enabling standardized, configurable, and comparable preprocessing workflows across different datasets and research studies.

title: Calibrating conditional risk
summary: We introduce and study the problem of calibrating conditional risk, which involves estimating the expected loss of a prediction model conditional on input features. We analyze this problem in both classification and regression settings and show that it is fundamentally equivalent to a standard regression task. For classification settings, we further establish a connection between conditional risk calibration and individual/conditional probability calibration, and develop theoretical insights for the performance metric. This reveals that while conditional risk calibration is related to existing uncertainty quantification problems, it remains a distinct and standalone machine learning problem. Empirically, we validate our theoretical findings and demonstrate the practical implications of conditional risk calibration in the learning to defer (L2D) framework. Our systematic experiments provide both qualitative and quantitative assessments, offering guidance for future research in uncertainty-aware decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and studies conditional risk calibration - estimating a model's expected loss conditional on input features, showing it's equivalent to standard regression and distinct from existing uncertainty quantification problems.
AI.motivation: To address the problem of calibrating conditional risk, which involves estimating the expected loss of a prediction model given input features, and to understand its relationship with existing uncertainty quantification problems while establishing it as a distinct machine learning problem.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing conditional risk calibration is equivalent to standard regression tasks, establishing connections between conditional risk calibration and individual/conditional probability calibration in classification settings, and empirical validation through systematic experiments including qualitative and quantitative assessments in learning to defer (L2D) frameworks.
AI.result: Theoretical findings reveal conditional risk calibration is fundamentally equivalent to standard regression, distinct from existing uncertainty quantification problems, with practical implications demonstrated in learning to defer frameworks through systematic experiments.
AI.conclusion: Conditional risk calibration is a distinct and standalone machine learning problem with theoretical foundations and practical applications in uncertainty-aware decision-making, offering guidance for future research in this area.

title: ConeSep: Cone-based Robust Noise-Unlearning Compositional Network for Composed Image Retrieval
summary: The Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) task provides a flexible retrieval paradigm via a reference image and modification text, but it heavily relies on expensive and error-prone triplet annotations. This paper systematically investigates the Noisy Triplet Correspondence (NTC) problem introduced by annotations. We find that NTC noise, particularly ``hard noise'' (i.e., the reference and target images are highly similar but the modification text is incorrect), poses a unique challenge to existing Noise Correspondence Learning (NCL) methods because it breaks the traditional ``small loss hypothesis''. We identify and elucidate three key, yet overlooked, challenges in the NTC task, namely (C1) Modality Suppression, (C2) Negative Anchor Deficiency, and (C3) Unlearning Backlash. To address these challenges, we propose a Cone-based robuSt noisE-unlearning comPositional network (ConeSep). Specifically, we first propose Geometric Fidelity Quantization, theoretically establishing and practically estimating a noise boundary to precisely locate noisy correspondence. Next, we introduce Negative Boundary Learning, which learns a ``diagonal negative combination'' for each query as its explicit semantic opposite-anchor in the embedding space. Finally, we design Boundary-based Targeted Unlearning, which models the noisy correction process as an optimal transport problem, elegantly avoiding Unlearning Backlash. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets (FashionIQ and CIRR) demonstrate that ConeSep significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, which fully demonstrates the effectiveness and robustness of our method.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the Noisy Triplet Correspondence (NTC) problem in Composed Image Retrieval, where annotation errors create "hard noise" that breaks traditional noise learning assumptions. The authors propose ConeSep, a method that identifies noise boundaries, learns negative anchors, and uses optimal transport for targeted unlearning.
AI.motivation: Composed Image Retrieval relies on expensive triplet annotations that often contain errors. The paper identifies that existing Noise Correspondence Learning methods fail with "hard noise" where reference and target images are similar but modification text is incorrect, breaking the traditional "small loss hypothesis".
AI.method: Proposes ConeSep with three components: 1) Geometric Fidelity Quantization to estimate noise boundaries and locate noisy correspondences, 2) Negative Boundary Learning to create explicit semantic opposite-anchors via "diagonal negative combinations", and 3) Boundary-based Targeted Unlearning that models noisy correction as an optimal transport problem to avoid unlearning backlash.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on FashionIQ and CIRR benchmark datasets show ConeSep significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating effectiveness and robustness in handling noisy triplet correspondences.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the unique challenges of Noisy Triplet Correspondence in CIR by identifying three overlooked problems (Modality Suppression, Negative Anchor Deficiency, Unlearning Backlash) and providing a comprehensive solution through ConeSep's boundary-based approach.

title: Scalable AI Inference: Performance Analysis and Optimization of AI Model Serving
summary: AI research often emphasizes model design and algorithmic performance, while deployment and inference remain comparatively underexplored despite being critical for real-world use. This study addresses that gap by investigating the performance and optimization of a BentoML-based AI inference system for scalable model serving developed in collaboration with graphworks.ai. The evaluation first establishes baseline performance under three realistic workload scenarios. To ensure a fair and reproducible assessment, a pre-trained RoBERTa sentiment analysis model is used throughout the experiments. The system is subjected to traffic patterns following gamma and exponential distributions in order to emulate real-world usage conditions, including steady, bursty, and high-intensity workloads. Key performance metrics, such as latency percentiles and throughput, are collected and analyzed to identify bottlenecks in the inference pipeline. Based on the baseline results, optimization strategies are introduced at multiple levels of the serving stack to improve efficiency and scalability. The optimized system is then reevaluated under the same workload conditions, and the results are compared with the baseline using statistical analysis to quantify the impact of the applied improvements. The findings demonstrate practical strategies for achieving efficient and scalable AI inference with BentoML. The study examines how latency and throughput scale under varying workloads, how optimizations at the runtime, service, and deployment levels affect response time, and how deployment in a single-node K3s cluster influences resilience during disruptions.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates performance optimization of a BentoML-based AI inference system for scalable model serving, evaluating baseline performance under realistic workloads and implementing multi-level optimizations to improve efficiency and scalability.
AI.motivation: AI research often focuses on model design and algorithmic performance while deployment and inference remain underexplored despite being critical for real-world applications. This study addresses the gap by investigating practical optimization strategies for scalable AI inference systems.
AI.method: The study establishes baseline performance using a pre-trained RoBERTa sentiment analysis model under three realistic workload scenarios with traffic patterns following gamma and exponential distributions. After identifying bottlenecks, optimization strategies are introduced at multiple levels of the serving stack. The optimized system is then reevaluated under the same workload conditions and compared with baseline using statistical analysis.
AI.result: The findings demonstrate practical strategies for achieving efficient and scalable AI inference with BentoML, showing how latency and throughput scale under varying workloads, how optimizations at runtime, service, and deployment levels affect response time, and how deployment in a single-node K3s cluster influences resilience during disruptions.
AI.conclusion: The study provides valuable insights into optimizing AI inference systems for real-world deployment, offering practical strategies for improving efficiency and scalability in production environments, with implications for both research and industry applications.

title: Object Referring-Guided Scanpath Prediction with Perception-Enhanced Vision-Language Models
summary: Object Referring-guided Scanpath Prediction (ORSP) aims to predict the human attention scanpath when they search for a specific target object in a visual scene according to a linguistic description describing the object. Multimodal information fusion is a key point of ORSP. Therefore, we propose a novel model, ScanVLA, to first exploit a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to extract and fuse inherently aligned visual and linguistic feature representations from the input image and referring expression. Next, to enhance the ScanVLA's perception of fine-grained positional information, we not only propose a novel History Enhanced Scanpath Decoder (HESD) that directly takes historical fixations' position information as input to help predict a more reasonable position for the current fixation, but also adopt a frozen Segmentation LoRA as an auxiliary component to help localize the referred object more precisely, which improves the scanpath prediction task without incurring additional large computational and time costs. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that ScanVLA can significantly outperform existing scanpath prediction methods under object referring.

AI.tldr: ScanVLA is a novel model for Object Referring-guided Scanpath Prediction (ORSP) that uses Vision-Language Models for multimodal fusion, incorporates historical fixation positions, and leverages segmentation LoRA for precise object localization, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of predicting human attention scanpaths when searching for specific target objects described linguistically in visual scenes. The key motivation is to improve multimodal information fusion in ORSP tasks, which is crucial for accurate scanpath prediction when people look for referred objects.
AI.method: 1) Uses Vision-Language Model (VLM) to extract and fuse inherently aligned visual and linguistic features from input image and referring expression. 2) Proposes History Enhanced Scanpath Decoder (HESD) that directly takes historical fixations' position information as input to predict more reasonable current fixation positions. 3) Adopts frozen Segmentation LoRA as auxiliary component to help localize referred objects more precisely without adding large computational costs.
AI.result: Extensive experimental results demonstrate that ScanVLA significantly outperforms existing scanpath prediction methods under object referring conditions, showing superior performance in predicting human attention scanpaths when searching for linguistically described objects.
AI.conclusion: ScanVLA effectively addresses the ORSP problem by leveraging VLM for multimodal fusion, incorporating historical fixation information through HESD, and using segmentation LoRA for precise object localization, achieving state-of-the-art performance while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Unlocking the Forecasting Economy: A Suite of Datasets for the Full Lifecycle of Prediction Market: [Experiments \& Analysis]
summary: Prediction markets are markets for trading claims on future events, such as presidential elections, and their prices provide continuously updated signals of collective beliefs. In decentralized platforms such as Polymarket, the market lifecycle spans market creation, token registration, trading, oracle interaction, dispute, and final settlement, yet the corresponding data are fragmented across heterogeneous off-chain and on-chain sources. We present the first continuously maintained dataset suite for the full lifecycle of decentralized prediction markets, built on Polymarket. To address the challenges of large-scale cross-source integration, incomplete linkage, and continuous synchronization, we build a unified relational data system that integrates three canonical layers: market metadata, fill-level trading records, and oracle-resolution events, through identifier resolution, on-chain recovery, and incremental updates. The resulting dataset spans October 2020 to March 2026 and comprises more than 770 thousand market records, over 943 million fill records, and nearly 2 million oracle events. We describe the data model, collection pipeline, and consistency mechanisms that make the dataset reproducible and extensible, and we demonstrate its utility through descriptive analyses of market activity and two downstream case studies: NBA outcome calibration and CPI expectation reconstruction.

AI.tldr: First comprehensive dataset suite for decentralized prediction markets (Polymarket) covering full lifecycle from market creation to settlement, with over 770K markets, 943M trades, and 2M oracle events from 2020-2026.
AI.motivation: Prediction markets provide valuable signals of collective beliefs, but decentralized platforms like Polymarket have fragmented data across heterogeneous sources, making comprehensive analysis difficult.
AI.method: Built unified relational data system integrating three layers: market metadata, fill-level trading records, and oracle-resolution events, using identifier resolution, on-chain recovery, and incremental updates.
AI.result: Dataset spans October 2020 to March 2026 with 770K+ market records, 943M+ fill records, and nearly 2M oracle events, enabling reproducible and extensible analysis.
AI.conclusion: The dataset enables comprehensive analysis of decentralized prediction markets and demonstrates utility through case studies on NBA outcome calibration and CPI expectation reconstruction.

title: Mitigating Hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models without Performance Degradation
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) exhibit powerful generative capabilities but frequently produce hallucinations that compromise output reliability. Fine-tuning on annotated data devoid of hallucinations offers the most direct solution, while its high computational cost motivates recent representation-based methods, which focus on mitigating hallucinatory components within hidden representations. Though efficient, we empirically observe that these methods degrade general generation capacity due to incomplete extraction of hallucination components and non-selective parameter updates. To address these limitations, we propose MPD, a dual-stage framework for mitigating hallucinations without performance degradation. Specifically, our MPD relies on two essential factors: (1) semantic-aware component disentanglement to extract pure hallucination components, and (2) interpretable parameter updates that selectively modify parameters most relevant to hallucination. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MPD achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing hallucinations by 23.4\% while maintaining 97.4\% of general generative capability as evaluated on LLaVA-Bench and MME, with no additional computational cost.

AI.tldr: MPD is a dual-stage framework that reduces hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models while preserving general generation capabilities through semantic-aware component disentanglement and interpretable parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Current representation-based methods for mitigating hallucinations in LVLMs degrade general generation capacity due to incomplete extraction of hallucination components and non-selective parameter updates.
AI.method: MPD uses semantic-aware component disentanglement to extract pure hallucination components, followed by interpretable parameter updates that selectively modify only the parameters most relevant to hallucination.
AI.result: MPD reduces hallucinations by 23.4% while maintaining 97.4% of general generative capability on LLaVA-Bench and MME benchmarks, with no additional computational cost.
AI.conclusion: MPD achieves state-of-the-art performance by effectively mitigating hallucinations without compromising the general generation capabilities of LVLMs through targeted component disentanglement and selective parameter updates.

title: The Origin of Edge of Stability
summary: Full-batch gradient descent on neural networks drives the largest Hessian eigenvalue to the threshold $2/η$, where $η$ is the learning rate. This phenomenon, the Edge of Stability, has resisted a unified explanation: existing accounts establish self-regulation near the edge but do not explain why the trajectory is forced toward $2/η$ from arbitrary initialization. We introduce the edge coupling, a functional on consecutive iterate pairs whose coefficient is uniquely fixed by the gradient-descent update. Differencing its criticality condition yields a step recurrence with stability boundary $2/η$, and a second-order expansion yields a loss-change formula whose telescoping sum forces curvature toward $2/η$. The two formulas involve different Hessian averages, but the mean value theorem localizes each to the true Hessian at an interior point of the step segment, yielding exact forcing of the Hessian eigenvalue with no gap. Setting both gradients of the edge coupling to zero classifies fixed points and period-two orbits; near a fixed point, the problem reduces to a function of the half-amplitude alone, which determines which directions support period-two orbits and on which side of the critical learning rate they appear.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a unified explanation for the Edge of Stability phenomenon in neural network training, showing why gradient descent forces the largest Hessian eigenvalue to exactly 2/η through a novel edge coupling functional analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing explanations for the Edge of Stability phenomenon only describe self-regulation near the edge but fail to explain why gradient descent trajectories are forced toward the 2/η threshold from arbitrary initializations.
AI.method: Introduces an "edge coupling" functional on consecutive iterate pairs with coefficients fixed by gradient descent. Uses differencing of criticality conditions to derive step recurrences and loss-change formulas that telescope to force curvature toward 2/η. Applies mean value theorem to localize Hessian averages to true Hessian values.
AI.result: Provides exact forcing of Hessian eigenvalue to 2/η with no gap. Classifies fixed points and period-two orbits by setting both gradients of edge coupling to zero. Shows near fixed points, the problem reduces to a function of half-amplitude alone, determining which directions support period-two orbits and on which side of critical learning rate they appear.
AI.conclusion: The edge coupling framework offers a unified explanation for the Edge of Stability phenomenon, explaining both self-regulation near the edge and the forcing mechanism that drives trajectories to the 2/η threshold from arbitrary initializations.

title: LaplacianFormer:Rethinking Linear Attention with Laplacian Kernel
summary: The quadratic complexity of softmax attention presents a major obstacle for scaling Transformers to high-resolution vision tasks. Existing linear attention variants often replace the softmax with Gaussian kernels to reduce complexity, but such approximations lack theoretical grounding and tend to oversuppress mid-range token interactions. We propose LaplacianFormer, a Transformer variant that employs a Laplacian kernel as a principled alternative to softmax, motivated by empirical observations and theoretical analysis. To address expressiveness degradation under low-rank approximations, we introduce a provably injective feature map that retains fine-grained token information. For efficient computation, we adopt a Nyström approximation of the kernel matrix and solve the resulting system using Newton--Schulz iteration, avoiding costly matrix inversion and SVD. We further develop custom CUDA implementations for both the kernel and solver, enabling high-throughput forward and backward passes suitable for edge deployment. Experiments on ImageNet show that LaplacianFormer achieves strong performance-efficiency trade-offs while improving attention expressiveness.

AI.tldr: LaplacianFormer replaces softmax attention with Laplacian kernel for linear complexity, using Nyström approximation and custom CUDA implementation for efficient vision Transformers.
AI.motivation: Softmax attention has quadratic complexity that limits Transformer scaling to high-resolution vision tasks. Existing linear attention variants use Gaussian kernels but lack theoretical grounding and oversuppress mid-range token interactions.
AI.method: 1) Replace softmax with Laplacian kernel as principled alternative; 2) Introduce provably injective feature map to retain fine-grained token information; 3) Use Nyström approximation of kernel matrix; 4) Solve system with Newton-Schulz iteration avoiding matrix inversion/SVD; 5) Develop custom CUDA implementations for kernel and solver.
AI.result: LaplacianFormer achieves strong performance-efficiency trade-offs on ImageNet while improving attention expressiveness, suitable for edge deployment with high-throughput forward/backward passes.
AI.conclusion: LaplacianFormer provides a theoretically grounded, efficient alternative to softmax attention that maintains expressiveness while enabling linear complexity, making Transformers more scalable for high-resolution vision tasks.

title: Surrogate Functionals for Machine-Learned Orbital-Free Density Functional Theory
summary: We introduce surrogate functionals: machine-learned energy functionals for orbital-free density functional theory (OF-DFT) which are defined not by universal fidelity to a physical reference, but merely by the requirement that density optimization with a fixed procedure yields the true ground-state density. Helpfully, training surrogate functionals requires only ground-state densities, no energies or gradients away from the ground state. We here propose a gradient-descent-improvement loss that guarantees exponential convergence of the density to the ground state, and combine it with an adaptive sampling scheme that concentrates learning around the optimization trajectories actually visited during inference. On the QM9 and QMugs benchmarks, surrogate functionals achieve density errors competitive with or improving upon the state of the art for fully supervised machine-learned OF-DFT, while eliminating the need for the $O(N^3)$ orthononormalization step required by prior work, yielding improved runtime scaling for larger systems.

AI.tldr: Surrogate functionals are ML energy functionals for orbital-free DFT trained only on ground-state densities, achieving competitive accuracy while eliminating expensive orthonormalization steps.
AI.motivation: To develop machine-learned energy functionals for orbital-free DFT that don't require expensive O(N³) orthonormalization steps and can be trained with minimal data (only ground-state densities).
AI.method: Propose surrogate functionals trained with gradient-descent-improvement loss for exponential convergence, combined with adaptive sampling focused on optimization trajectories visited during inference.
AI.result: On QM9 and QMugs benchmarks, achieves density errors competitive with or better than state-of-the-art supervised ML-OF-DFT, while eliminating O(N³) orthonormalization for improved runtime scaling.
AI.conclusion: Surrogate functionals provide an efficient alternative to traditional ML-OF-DFT methods by requiring only ground-state densities for training and avoiding computational bottlenecks.

title: Self-supervised pretraining for an iterative image size agnostic vision transformer
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) dominate self-supervised learning (SSL). While they have proven highly effective for large-scale pretraining, they are computationally inefficient and scale poorly with image size. Consequently, foundational models like DINO are constrained to low-resolution processing. A recent foveal-inspired transformer achieves resolution agnosticism by iteratively processing a fixed-size context of multi-zoom patches. This model demonstrated promising results via supervised learning, utilizing a sequential, recurrent-like process without backpropagation through time. To unlock its potential as a foundational backbone, we introduce a novel sequential-to-global SSL framework based on DINO's self-distillation objective. Supported by an efficient integral-image patch extraction method, our approach enables large-scale pretraining for image-size agnostic vision encoders. We achieve competitive performance on ImageNet-1K and downstream classification tasks, maintaining a constant computational budget regardless of input resolution.

AI.tldr: A novel self-supervised learning framework that enables resolution-agnostic vision transformers using sequential processing and DINO's self-distillation objective.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers are computationally inefficient and scale poorly with image size, limiting foundational models like DINO to low-resolution processing. The authors aim to create a resolution-agnostic foundational backbone that maintains constant computational cost regardless of input size.
AI.method: Proposes a sequential-to-global SSL framework based on DINO's self-distillation objective, combined with an efficient integral-image patch extraction method. Uses a foveal-inspired transformer that processes fixed-size context of multi-zoom patches through sequential, recurrent-like processing without backpropagation through time.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance on ImageNet-1K and downstream classification tasks while maintaining constant computational budget regardless of input resolution.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully enables large-scale pretraining for image-size agnostic vision encoders, addressing the computational inefficiency and poor scaling of traditional Vision Transformers with image size.

title: Efficient Test-Time Inference via Deterministic Exploration of Truncated Decoding Trees
summary: Self-consistency boosts inference-time performance by sampling multiple reasoning traces in parallel and voting. However, in constrained domains like math and code, this strategy is compute-inefficient because it samples with replacement, repeatedly revisiting the same high-probability prefixes and duplicate completions. We propose Distinct Leaf Enumeration (DLE), a deterministic decoding method that treats truncated sampling as traversal of a pruned decoding tree and systematically enumerates distinct leaves instead of sampling with replacement. This strategy improves inference efficiency in two ways. Algorithmically, it increases coverage of the truncated search space under a fixed budget by exploring previously unvisited high-probability branches. Systemically, it reuses shared prefixes and reduces redundant token generation. Empirically, DLE explores higher-quality reasoning traces than stochastic self-consistency, yielding better performance on math, coding, and general reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: DLE is a deterministic decoding method that systematically enumerates distinct reasoning paths instead of sampling with replacement, improving inference efficiency and performance on reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Self-consistency sampling is compute-inefficient in constrained domains because it repeatedly revisits the same high-probability prefixes and generates duplicate completions through sampling with replacement.
AI.method: Distinct Leaf Enumeration (DLE) treats truncated sampling as traversal of a pruned decoding tree and systematically enumerates distinct leaves instead of sampling with replacement, reusing shared prefixes and reducing redundant token generation.
AI.result: DLE explores higher-quality reasoning traces than stochastic self-consistency, yielding better performance on math, coding, and general reasoning tasks while improving inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Systematic enumeration of distinct reasoning paths through DLE provides a more efficient and effective alternative to stochastic self-consistency for constrained reasoning domains.

title: MLG-Stereo: ViT Based Stereo Matching with Multi-Stage Local-Global Enhancement
summary: With the development of deep learning, ViT-based stereo matching methods have made significant progress due to their remarkable robustness and zero-shot ability. However, due to the limitations of ViTs in handling resolution sensitivity and their relative neglect of local information, the ability of ViT-based methods to predict details and handle arbitrary-resolution images is still weaker than that of CNN-based methods. To address these shortcomings, we propose MLG-Stereo, a systematic pipeline-level design that extends global modeling beyond the encoder stage. First, we propose a Multi-Granularity Feature Network to effectively balance global context and local geometric information, enabling comprehensive feature extraction from images of arbitrary resolution and bridging the gap between training and inference scales. Then, a Local-Global Cost Volume is constructed to capture both locally-correlated and global-aware matching information. Finally, a Local-Global Guided Recurrent Unit is introduced to iteratively optimize the disparity locally under the guidance of global information. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple benchmark datasets, demonstrating that our MLG-Stereo exhibits highly competitive performance on the Middlebury and KITTI-2015 benchmarks compared to contemporaneous leading methods, and achieves outstanding results in the KITTI-2012 dataset.

AI.tldr: MLG-Stereo: A ViT-based stereo matching method that addresses resolution sensitivity and local information neglect through multi-granularity feature extraction, local-global cost volume, and guided recurrent optimization.
AI.motivation: ViT-based stereo matching methods have shown good robustness and zero-shot ability but suffer from resolution sensitivity issues and neglect of local information, making them weaker than CNN-based methods in detail prediction and arbitrary-resolution handling.
AI.method: Three main components: 1) Multi-Granularity Feature Network for balancing global context and local geometric information, 2) Local-Global Cost Volume to capture both locally-correlated and global-aware matching information, 3) Local-Global Guided Recurrent Unit for iterative disparity optimization under global guidance.
AI.result: Highly competitive performance on Middlebury and KITTI-2015 benchmarks compared to contemporaneous leading methods, and outstanding results on KITTI-2012 dataset.
AI.conclusion: MLG-Stereo successfully addresses ViT limitations in stereo matching through systematic pipeline-level design that extends global modeling beyond the encoder, achieving strong performance across multiple benchmarks.

title: Explicit Dropout: Deterministic Regularization for Transformer Architectures
summary: Dropout is a widely used regularization technique in deep learning, but its effects are typically realized through stochastic masking rather than explicit optimization objectives. We propose a deterministic formulation that expresses dropout as an additive regularizer directly incorporated into the training loss. The framework derives explicit regularization terms for Transformer architectures, covering attention query, key, value, and feed-forward components with independently controllable strengths. This formulation removes reliance on stochastic perturbations while providing clearer and fine-grained control over regularization strength. Experiments across image classification, temporal action detection, and audio classification show that explicit dropout matches or outperforms conventional implicit methods, with consistent gains when applied to attention and feed-forward network layers. Ablation studies demonstrate stable performance and controllable regularization through regularization coefficients and dropout rates. Overall, explicit dropout offers a practical and interpretable alternative to stochastic regularization while maintaining architectural flexibility across diverse tasks.

AI.tldr: Explicit dropout reformulates stochastic dropout as deterministic additive regularization in Transformers, providing fine-grained control over regularization strength without random masking.
AI.motivation: Dropout is widely used but relies on stochastic masking rather than explicit optimization objectives, making it less interpretable and controllable.
AI.method: Proposes a deterministic formulation that expresses dropout as additive regularizer in training loss, deriving explicit regularization terms for Transformer components (attention query, key, value, feed-forward) with independent strength control.
AI.result: Explicit dropout matches or outperforms conventional implicit methods across image classification, temporal action detection, and audio classification, with consistent gains in attention and feed-forward layers. Ablation shows stable performance and controllable regularization.
AI.conclusion: Explicit dropout offers a practical, interpretable alternative to stochastic regularization while maintaining architectural flexibility across diverse tasks.

title: CHASM: Unveiling Covert Advertisements on Chinese Social Media
summary: Current benchmarks for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in social media moderation completely overlook a serious threat: covert advertisements, which disguise themselves as regular posts to deceive and mislead consumers into making purchases, leading to significant ethical and legal concerns. In this paper, we present the CHASM, a first-of-its-kind dataset designed to evaluate the capability of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in detecting covert advertisements on social media. CHASM is a high-quality, anonymized, manually curated dataset consisting of 4,992 instances, based on real-world scenarios from the Chinese social media platform Rednote. The dataset was collected and annotated under strict privacy protection and quality control protocols. It includes many product experience sharing posts that closely resemble covert advertisements, making the dataset particularly challenging.The results show that under both zero-shot and in-context learning settings, none of the current MLLMs are sufficiently reliable for detecting covert advertisements.Our further experiments revealed that fine-tuning open-source MLLMs on our dataset yielded noticeable performance gains. However, significant challenges persist, such as detecting subtle cues in comments and differences in visual and textual structures.We provide in-depth error analysis and outline future research directions. We hope our study can serve as a call for the research community and platform moderators to develop more precise defenses against this emerging threat.

AI.tldr: CHASM is the first dataset for evaluating MLLMs' ability to detect covert advertisements on social media, showing current models are unreliable for this task.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks overlook covert advertisements - disguised posts that deceive consumers into purchases, causing ethical and legal concerns. There's a need to evaluate MLLMs' capability to detect this emerging threat.
AI.method: Created CHASM dataset: 4,992 high-quality, anonymized, manually curated instances from Chinese social media platform Rednote. Collected under strict privacy protection and quality control. Includes product experience sharing posts resembling covert ads. Evaluated MLLMs under zero-shot and in-context learning settings, then fine-tuned open-source models.
AI.result: No current MLLMs are sufficiently reliable for detecting covert advertisements in zero-shot or in-context learning settings. Fine-tuning on CHASM yields noticeable performance gains, but significant challenges persist in detecting subtle cues in comments and visual/textual structure differences.
AI.conclusion: The study calls for research community and platform moderators to develop more precise defenses against covert advertisements. Provides error analysis and future research directions, highlighting the need for improved multimodal detection capabilities.

title: Fast-then-Fine: A Two-Stage Framework with Multi-Granular Representation for Cross-Modal Retrieval in Remote Sensing
summary: Remote sensing (RS) image-text retrieval plays a critical role in understanding massive RS imagery. However, the dense multi-object distribution and complex backgrounds in RS imagery make it difficult to simultaneously achieve fine-grained cross-modal alignment and efficient retrieval. Existing methods either rely on complex cross-modal interactions that lead to low retrieval efficiency, or depend on large-scale vision-language model pre-training, which requires massive data and computational resources. To address these issues, we propose a fast-then-fine (FTF) two-stage retrieval framework that decomposes retrieval into a text-agnostic recall stage for efficient candidate selection and a text-guided rerank stage for fine-grained alignment. Specifically, in the recall stage, text-agnostic coarse-grained representations are employed for efficient candidate selection; in the rerank stage, a parameter-free balanced text-guided interaction block enhances fine-grained alignment without introducing additional learnable parameters. Furthermore, an inter- and intra-modal loss is designed to jointly optimize cross-modal alignment across multi-granular representations. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate that the FTF achieves competitive retrieval accuracy while significantly improving retrieval efficiency compared with existing methods.

AI.tldr: FTF framework for RS image-text retrieval: two-stage approach with efficient recall + fine-grained rerank, achieving competitive accuracy with improved efficiency.
AI.motivation: RS image-text retrieval faces challenges due to dense multi-object distribution and complex backgrounds. Existing methods either have low retrieval efficiency from complex cross-modal interactions, or require massive data/computational resources from large-scale pre-training.
AI.method: Proposes Fast-then-Fine (FTF) two-stage retrieval framework: 1) Recall stage uses text-agnostic coarse-grained representations for efficient candidate selection; 2) Rerank stage uses parameter-free balanced text-guided interaction block for fine-grained alignment without additional learnable parameters. Also designs inter- and intra-modal loss for joint optimization across multi-granular representations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public benchmarks show FTF achieves competitive retrieval accuracy while significantly improving retrieval efficiency compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The FTF framework effectively addresses efficiency-accuracy trade-off in RS image-text retrieval by decomposing retrieval into efficient recall and fine-grained rerank stages, offering practical solution for real-world applications.

title: CCTVBench: Contrastive Consistency Traffic VideoQA Benchmark for Multimodal LLMs
summary: Safety-critical traffic reasoning requires contrastive consistency: models must detect true hazards when an accident occurs, and reliably reject plausible-but-false hypotheses under near-identical counterfactual scenes. We present CCTVBench, a Contrastive Consistency Traffic VideoQA Benchmark built on paired real accident videos and world-model-generated counterfactual counterparts, together with minimally different, mutually exclusive hypothesis questions. CCTVBench enforces a single structured decision pattern over each video question quadruple and provides actionable diagnostics that decompose failures into positive omission, positive swap, negative hallucination, and mutual-exclusivity violation, while separating video versus question consistency. Experiments across open-source and proprietary video LLMs reveal a large and persistent gap between standard per-instance QA metrics and quadruple-level contrastive consistency, with unreliable none-of-the-above rejection as a key bottleneck. Finally, we introduce C-TCD, a contrastive decoding approach leveraging a semantically exclusive counterpart video as the contrast input at inference time, improving both instance-level QA and contrastive consistency.

AI.tldr: CCTVBench is a traffic videoQA benchmark with paired real accident videos and counterfactual counterparts to test contrastive consistency in safety-critical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical traffic reasoning requires models to detect true hazards in accidents while reliably rejecting plausible-but-false hypotheses in near-identical counterfactual scenes. Current models lack robust contrastive consistency.
AI.method: Created CCTVBench with paired real accident videos and world-model-generated counterfactual counterparts, plus minimally different, mutually exclusive hypothesis questions. Introduces C-TCD (contrastive decoding) using semantically exclusive counterpart videos as contrast input at inference.
AI.result: Experiments show large gap between standard QA metrics and quadruple-level contrastive consistency, with unreliable "none-of-the-above" rejection as key bottleneck. C-TCD improves both instance-level QA and contrastive consistency.
AI.conclusion: CCTVBench provides actionable diagnostics for contrastive consistency failures, revealing fundamental limitations in current video LLMs for safety-critical reasoning. C-TCD offers promising approach to improve both accuracy and consistency.

title: A Hierarchical MARL-Based Approach for Coordinated Retail P2P Trading and Wholesale Market Participation of DERs
summary: The ongoing shift towards decentralization of the electric energy sector, driven by the growing electrification across end-use sectors, and widespread adoption of distributed energy resources (DERs), necessitates their active participation in the electricity markets to support grid operations. Furthermore, with bi-directional energy and communication flows becoming standard, intelligent, easy-to-deploy, resource-conservative demand-side participation is expected to play a critical role in securing power grid operational flexibility and market efficiency. This work proposes a market engagement framework that leverages a hierarchical multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MARL) approach to enable individual prosumers to participate in peer-to-peer retail auctions and further aggregate these intelligent prosumers to facilitate effective DER participation in wholesale markets. Ultimately, a Stackelberg game is proposed to coordinate this hierarchical MARL-based DER market participation framework toward enhanced market performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes hierarchical multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework for DER market participation, coordinating retail P2P auctions and wholesale markets via Stackelberg game.
AI.motivation: The shift toward decentralized energy sector with widespread DER adoption requires active DER participation in electricity markets to support grid operations and ensure market efficiency through intelligent demand-side participation.
AI.method: Hierarchical multi-agent deep reinforcement learning approach enabling individual prosumers to participate in peer-to-peer retail auctions, then aggregating these intelligent prosumers for wholesale market participation, coordinated via Stackelberg game.
AI.result: Framework facilitates effective DER participation in both retail and wholesale electricity markets through intelligent coordination.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical MARL-based DER market participation framework with Stackelberg game coordination enhances market performance by enabling intelligent prosumer participation across multiple market levels.

title: DynamicRad: Content-Adaptive Sparse Attention for Long Video Diffusion
summary: Leveraging the natural spatiotemporal energy decay in video diffusion offers a path to efficiency, yet relying solely on rigid static masks risks losing critical long-range information in complex dynamics. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{DynamicRad}, a unified sparse-attention paradigm that grounds adaptive selection within a radial locality prior. DynamicRad introduces a \textbf{dual-mode} strategy: \textit{static-ratio} for speed-optimized execution and \textit{dynamic-threshold} for quality-first filtering. To ensure robustness without online search overhead, we integrate an offline Bayesian Optimization (BO) pipeline coupled with a \textbf{semantic motion router}. This lightweight projection module maps prompt embeddings to optimal sparsity regimes with \textbf{minimal runtime overhead}. Unlike online profiling methods, our offline BO optimizes attention reconstruction error (MSE) on a physics-based proxy task, ensuring rapid convergence. Experiments on HunyuanVideo and Wan2.1-14B demonstrate that DynamicRad pushes the efficiency--quality Pareto frontier, achieving \textbf{1.7$\times$--2.5$\times$ inference speedups} with \textbf{over 80\% effective sparsity}. In some long-sequence settings, the dynamic mode even matches or exceeds the dense baseline, while mask-aware LoRA further improves long-horizon coherence. Code is available at https://github.com/Adamlong3/DynamicRad.

AI.tldr: DynamicRad is a unified sparse-attention paradigm for video diffusion models that uses adaptive radial locality selection with dual-mode strategy (static-ratio for speed, dynamic-threshold for quality) and offline Bayesian optimization for efficient inference.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches relying on rigid static masks for video diffusion efficiency risk losing critical long-range information in complex dynamics, creating a need for adaptive spatiotemporal attention that preserves important information while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes DynamicRad with dual-mode strategy: static-ratio for speed-optimized execution and dynamic-threshold for quality-first filtering. Uses offline Bayesian Optimization pipeline with semantic motion router that maps prompt embeddings to optimal sparsity regimes with minimal runtime overhead.
AI.result: Achieves 1.7×-2.5× inference speedups with over 80% effective sparsity on HunyuanVideo and Wan2.1-14B. Dynamic mode matches or exceeds dense baseline in some long-sequence settings, with mask-aware LoRA improving long-horizon coherence.
AI.conclusion: DynamicRad pushes the efficiency-quality Pareto frontier in video diffusion by combining adaptive radial locality selection with offline optimization, offering significant speedups while maintaining or improving quality compared to dense baselines.

title: Differentially Private Clustered Federated Learning with Privacy-Preserving Initialization and Normality-Driven Aggregation
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables training of a global model while keeping raw data on end-devices. Despite this, FL has shown to leak private user information and thus in practice, it is often coupled with methods such as differential privacy (DP) and secure vector sum to provide formal privacy guarantees to its participants. In realistic cross-device deployments, the data are highly heterogeneous, so vanilla federated learning converges slowly and generalizes poorly. Clustered federated learning (CFL) mitigates this by segregating users into clusters, leading to lower intra-cluster data heterogeneity. Nevertheless, coupling CFL with DP remains challenging: the injected DP noise makes individual client updates excessively noisy, and the server is unable to initialize cluster centroids with the less noisy aggregated updates. To address this challenge, we propose PINA, a two-stage framework that first lets each client fine-tune a lightweight low-rank adaptation (LoRA) adapter and privately share a compressed sketch of the update. The server leverages these sketches to construct robust cluster centroids. In the second stage, PINA introduces a normality-driven aggregation mechanism that improves convergence and robustness. Our method retains the benefits of clustered FL while providing formal privacy guarantees against an untrusted server. Extensive evaluations show that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art DP-FL algorithms by an average of 2.9% in accuracy for privacy budgets (epsilon in {2, 8}).

AI.tldr: PINA: A two-stage DP-CFL framework that uses LoRA adapters and compressed sketches to enable private clustered federated learning with improved accuracy under DP constraints.
AI.motivation: Clustered FL addresses data heterogeneity but faces challenges when combined with DP - DP noise makes client updates too noisy, preventing proper cluster centroid initialization and degrading performance.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Clients fine-tune LoRA adapters and privately share compressed sketches of updates; server uses sketches to construct robust cluster centroids. 2) Normality-driven aggregation mechanism improves convergence and robustness.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art DP-FL algorithms by average 2.9% accuracy for privacy budgets (ε ∈ {2, 8}) while maintaining formal privacy guarantees against untrusted server.
AI.conclusion: PINA successfully enables private clustered FL by addressing DP noise challenges through compressed sketching and normality-driven aggregation, preserving CFL benefits with formal privacy guarantees.

title: Video-ToC: Video Tree-of-Cue Reasoning
summary: Existing Video Large Language Models (Video LLMs) struggle with complex video understanding, exhibiting limited reasoning capabilities and potential hallucinations. In particular, these methods tend to perform reasoning solely relying on the pretrained inherent reasoning rationales whilst lacking perception-aware adaptation to the input video content. To address this, we propose \textbf{Video-ToC}, a novel video reasoning framework that enhances video understanding through tree-of-cue reasoning. Specifically, our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) A tree-guided visual cue localization mechanism, which endows the model with enhanced fine-grained perceptual capabilities through structured reasoning patterns; (2) A reasoning-demand reward mechanism, which dynamically adjusts the reward value for reinforcement learning (RL) based on the estimation of reasoning demands, enabling on-demand incentives for more effective reasoning strategies; and (3) An automated annotation pipeline that constructs the Video-ToC-SFT-1k and Video-ToC-RL-2k datasets for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and RL training, respectively. Extensive evaluations on six video understanding benchmarks and a video hallucination benchmark demonstrate the superiority of Video-ToC over baselines and recent methods. Code is available at https://github.com/qizhongtan/Video-ToC.

AI.tldr: Video-ToC enhances video understanding through tree-of-cue reasoning with visual cue localization, reasoning-demand rewards, and automated dataset creation.
AI.motivation: Existing Video LLMs struggle with complex video understanding, showing limited reasoning capabilities and hallucinations. They rely too much on pretrained reasoning patterns without adapting to actual video content.
AI.method: Proposes Video-ToC framework with three innovations: 1) Tree-guided visual cue localization for fine-grained perception, 2) Reasoning-demand reward mechanism that dynamically adjusts RL rewards based on reasoning complexity, 3) Automated annotation pipeline creating Video-ToC-SFT-1k and Video-ToC-RL-2k datasets.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on six video understanding benchmarks and a video hallucination benchmark demonstrate superiority over baselines and recent methods.
AI.conclusion: Video-ToC effectively addresses limitations in video reasoning by combining structured cue localization with adaptive reinforcement learning, improving both understanding accuracy and reducing hallucinations.

title: Too Sharp, Too Sure: When Calibration Follows Curvature
summary: Modern neural networks can achieve high accuracy while remaining poorly calibrated, producing confidence estimates that do not match empirical correctness. Yet calibration is often treated as a post-hoc attribute. We take a different perspective: we study calibration as a training-time phenomenon on small vision tasks, and ask whether calibrated solutions can be obtained reliably by intervening on the training procedure. We identify a tight coupling between calibration, curvature, and margins during training of deep networks under multiple gradient-based methods. Empirically, Expected Calibration Error (ECE) closely tracks curvature-based sharpness throughout optimization. Mathematically, we show that both ECE and Gauss--Newton curvature are controlled, up to problem-specific constants, by the same margin-dependent exponential tail functional along the trajectory. Guided by this mechanism, we introduce a margin-aware training objective that explicitly targets robust-margin tails and local smoothness, yielding improved out-of-sample calibration across optimizers without sacrificing accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper studies calibration as a training-time phenomenon, finding tight coupling between calibration, curvature, and margins during neural network training, and proposes a margin-aware training objective for better calibration.
AI.motivation: Modern neural networks achieve high accuracy but remain poorly calibrated, producing confidence estimates that don't match empirical correctness. The authors want to study calibration as a training-time phenomenon rather than treating it as a post-hoc attribute, and investigate whether calibrated solutions can be obtained reliably through training interventions.
AI.method: The authors study calibration on small vision tasks and identify coupling between calibration, curvature, and margins during training. They show mathematically that Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Gauss-Newton curvature are controlled by the same margin-dependent exponential tail functional. Based on this mechanism, they introduce a margin-aware training objective that explicitly targets robust-margin tails and local smoothness.
AI.result: Empirically, Expected Calibration Error (ECE) closely tracks curvature-based sharpness throughout optimization. The margin-aware training objective yields improved out-of-sample calibration across optimizers without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Calibration is fundamentally linked to training dynamics through curvature and margins. By explicitly targeting margin-dependent tails and local smoothness during training, it's possible to achieve better calibrated models without compromising accuracy.

title: Random Walk on Point Clouds for Feature Detection
comment: 20 pages, 11 figures. Published in Information Sciences
summary: The points on the point clouds that can entirely outline the shape of the model are of critical importance, as they serve as the foundation for numerous point cloud processing tasks and are widely utilized in computer graphics and computer-aided design. This study introduces a novel method, RWoDSN, for extracting such feature points, incorporating considerations of sharp-to-smooth transitions, large-to-small scales, and textural-to-detailed features. We approach feature extraction as a two-stage context-dependent analysis problem. In the first stage, we propose a novel neighborhood descriptor, termed the Disk Sampling Neighborhood (DSN), which, unlike traditional spatially and geometrically invariant approaches, preserves a matrix structure while maintaining normal neighborhood relationships. In the second stage, a random walk is performed on the DSN (RWoDSN), yielding a graph-based DSN that simultaneously accounts for the spatial distribution, topological properties, and geometric characteristics of the local surface surrounding each point. This enables the effective extraction of feature points. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed RWoDSN method achieves a recall of 0.769-22% higher than the current state-of-the-art-alongside a precision of 0.784. Furthermore, it significantly outperforms several traditional and deep-learning techniques across eight evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: RWoDSN is a novel two-stage method for extracting critical feature points from point clouds that outline model shapes, achieving state-of-the-art performance with 0.769 recall and 0.784 precision.
AI.motivation: Feature points that outline model shapes are crucial for point cloud processing tasks in computer graphics and CAD, but existing methods need improvement in handling sharp-to-smooth transitions, scale variations, and texture-to-detail features.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Disk Sampling Neighborhood (DSN) descriptor that preserves matrix structure and normal neighborhood relationships, 2) Random Walk on DSN (RWoDSN) that creates graph-based DSN considering spatial distribution, topology, and geometric characteristics.
AI.result: Achieves recall of 0.769 (22% higher than state-of-the-art) and precision of 0.784, significantly outperforming both traditional and deep-learning methods across eight evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: RWoDSN effectively extracts feature points by combining novel neighborhood descriptors with graph-based analysis, demonstrating superior performance for point cloud feature extraction tasks.

title: ProMMSearchAgent: A Generalizable Multimodal Search Agent Trained with Process-Oriented Rewards
summary:
Training multimodal agents via reinforcement learning for knowledge-intensive visual reasoning is fundamentally hindered by the extreme sparsity of outcome-based supervision and the unpredictability of live web environments. To resolve these algorithmic and environmental bottlenecks, we introduce ProMMSearchAgent, establishing a novel Sim-to-Real training paradigm for multimodal search.
  We decouple policy learning into a deterministic, local static sandbox. Crucially, to learn effectively within this constrained environment, we propose an introspective process-oriented reward. By probing the agent's own parametric knowledge boundaries, we generate dense behavioral metadata that explicitly rewards the correct cognitive decision, initiating a multimodal or text search only when visually or factually uncertain. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our locally-trained policy transfers zero-shot to the live Google Search API. ProMMSearchAgent achieves new SOTA performance, outperforming MMSearch-R1 by +5.1% on FVQA-test, +6.3% on InfoSeek, and +11.3% on MMSearch.

AI.tldr: ProMMSearchAgent introduces a Sim-to-Real training paradigm for multimodal search agents using introspective process-oriented rewards to overcome sparse supervision in live web environments.
AI.motivation: Training multimodal agents via reinforcement learning for knowledge-intensive visual reasoning faces two major challenges: extreme sparsity of outcome-based supervision and unpredictability of live web environments, which fundamentally hinder effective learning.
AI.method: Decouples policy learning into a deterministic local static sandbox and introduces an introspective process-oriented reward mechanism. The approach probes the agent's own parametric knowledge boundaries to generate dense behavioral metadata, rewarding correct cognitive decisions about when to initiate multimodal or text searches based on visual or factual uncertainty.
AI.result: The locally-trained policy transfers zero-shot to live Google Search API and achieves new state-of-the-art performance: +5.1% on FVQA-test, +6.3% on InfoSeek, and +11.3% on MMSearch compared to previous best method MMSearch-R1.
AI.conclusion: ProMMSearchAgent successfully establishes a novel Sim-to-Real training paradigm that overcomes algorithmic and environmental bottlenecks in multimodal search agent training, demonstrating effective zero-shot transfer to real-world search environments with significant performance improvements.

title: GRPO-VPS: Enhancing Group Relative Policy Optimization with Verifiable Process Supervision for Effective Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) by leveraging direct outcome verification instead of learned reward models. Building on this paradigm, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) eliminates the need for critic models but suffers from indiscriminate credit assignment for intermediate steps, which limits its ability to identify effective reasoning strategies and incurs overthinking. In this work, we introduce a model-free and verifiable process supervision via probing the model's belief in the correct answer throughout its reasoning trajectory. By segmenting the generation into discrete steps and tracking the conditional probability of the correct answer appended at each segment boundary, we efficiently compute interpretable segment-wise progress measurements to refine GRPO's trajectory-level feedback. This approach enables more targeted and sample-efficient policy updates, while avoiding the need for intermediate supervision derived from costly Monte Carlo rollouts or auxiliary models. Experiments on mathematical and general-domain benchmarks show consistent gains over GRPO across diverse models: up to 2.6-point accuracy improvements and 13.7% reasoning-length reductions on math tasks, and up to 2.4 points and 4% on general-domain tasks, demonstrating strong generalization.

AI.tldr: RLVR advances LLM reasoning using verifiable rewards, but GRPO has credit assignment issues. This work introduces model-free verifiable process supervision via belief tracking to refine GRPO's feedback, improving accuracy and reducing reasoning length.
AI.motivation: GRPO eliminates critic models in RLVR but suffers from indiscriminate credit assignment for intermediate reasoning steps, limiting identification of effective strategies and causing overthinking. Need for more targeted policy updates without costly intermediate supervision.
AI.method: Introduces model-free verifiable process supervision by probing LLM's belief in correct answer throughout reasoning trajectory. Segments generation into discrete steps, tracks conditional probability of correct answer at each boundary to compute interpretable segment-wise progress measurements, refining GRPO's trajectory-level feedback.
AI.result: Consistent gains over GRPO across diverse models: up to 2.6-point accuracy improvements and 13.7% reasoning-length reductions on math tasks, and up to 2.4 points and 4% on general-domain tasks, demonstrating strong generalization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enables more targeted and sample-efficient policy updates without needing intermediate supervision from costly Monte Carlo rollouts or auxiliary models, effectively addressing GRPO's credit assignment limitations.

title: RefAerial: A Benchmark and Approach for Referring Detection in Aerial Images
summary: Referring detection refers to locate the target referred by natural languages, which has recently attracted growing research interests. However, existing datasets are limited to ground images with large object centered in relative small scenes. This paper introduces a large-scale challenging dataset for referring detection in aerial images, termed as RefAerial. It distinguishes from conventional ground referring detection datasets by 4 characteristics: (1) low but diverse object-to-scene ratios, (2) numerous targets and distractors, (3)complex and fine-grained referring descriptions, (4) diverse and broad scenes in the aerial view. We also develop a human-in-the-loop referring expansion and annotation engine (REA-Engine) for efficient semi-automated referring pair annotation. Besides, we observe that existing ground referring detection approaches exhibiting serious performance degradation on our aerial dataset since the intrinsic scale variety issue within or across aerial images. Therefore, we further propose a novel scale-comprehensive and sensitive (SCS) framework for referring detection in aerial images. It consists of a mixture-of-granularity (MoG) attention and a two-stage comprehensive-to-sensitive (CtS) decoding strategy. Specifically, the mixture-of-granularity attention is developed for scale-comprehensive target understanding. In addition, the two-stage comprehensive-to-sensitive decoding strategy is designed for coarse-to-fine referring target decoding. Eventually, the proposed SCS framework achieves remarkable performance on our aerial referring detection dataset and even promising performance boost on conventional ground referring detection datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RefAerial, a large-scale challenging dataset for referring detection in aerial images, and proposes a novel scale-comprehensive and sensitive (SCS) framework to address the unique challenges of aerial referring detection.
AI.motivation: Existing referring detection datasets are limited to ground images with large objects in small scenes, lacking the challenges of aerial imagery. Aerial images present unique difficulties including low object-to-scene ratios, numerous targets/distractors, complex descriptions, and diverse scenes.
AI.method: 1) Developed RefAerial dataset with human-in-the-loop REA-Engine for semi-automated annotation. 2) Proposed SCS framework with mixture-of-granularity (MoG) attention for scale-comprehensive understanding and two-stage comprehensive-to-sensitive (CtS) decoding for coarse-to-fine target localization.
AI.result: The SCS framework achieves remarkable performance on the RefAerial aerial referring detection dataset and shows promising performance improvements even on conventional ground referring detection datasets.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the challenges of referring detection in aerial images through a novel dataset and framework, demonstrating that existing ground-based approaches degrade on aerial data due to scale variety issues, while the proposed SCS framework effectively handles these challenges.

title: Improving clinical interpretability of linear neuroimaging models through feature whitening
summary: Linear models are widely used in computational neuroimaging to identify biomarkers associated with brain pathologies. However, interpreting the learned weights remains challenging, as they do not always yield clinically meaningful insights. This difficulty arises in part from the inherent correlation between brain regions, which causes linear weights to reflect shared rather than region-specific contributions. In particular, some groups of regions, including homologous structures in the left and right hemispheres, are known to exhibit strong anatomical correlations. In this work, we leverage this prior neuroanatomical knowledge to introduce a whitening approach applied to groups of regions with known shared variance, designed to disentangle overlapping information across correlated brain measures. We additionally propose a regularized variant that allows controlled tuning of the degree of decorrelation. We evaluate this method using region-of-interest features in two psychiatric classification tasks, distinguishing individuals with bipolar disorder or schizophrenia from healthy controls. Importantly, unlike PCA or ICA which use whitening as a dimensionality reduction step, our approach decorrelates anatomically informed pairs of neuroanatomical regions while retaining the full input signal, making it specifically suited for feature interpretation rather than feature selection. Our findings demonstrate that whitening improves the interpretability of model weights while preserving predictive performance, providing a robust framework for linking linear model outputs to neurobiological mechanisms.

AI.tldr: A whitening approach is proposed to decorrelate anatomically informed groups of brain regions (like homologous hemispheres) to improve interpretability of linear model weights in neuroimaging, while maintaining predictive performance.
AI.motivation: Linear models in neuroimaging often produce weights that are hard to interpret clinically due to correlations between brain regions. Strong anatomical correlations (e.g., between left-right homologous structures) cause weights to reflect shared rather than region-specific contributions, limiting meaningful biomarker identification.
AI.method: A whitening approach applied to groups of regions with known shared variance (like homologous hemispheres) to disentangle overlapping information across correlated brain measures. Also includes a regularized variant for controlled tuning of decorrelation degree. Unlike PCA/ICA, it retains full input signal and focuses on anatomically informed region pairs.
AI.result: The method was evaluated on psychiatric classification tasks (bipolar disorder and schizophrenia vs healthy controls) using region-of-interest features. Whitening improved interpretability of model weights while preserving predictive performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed whitening approach provides a robust framework for linking linear model outputs to neurobiological mechanisms by improving weight interpretability without sacrificing predictive power, specifically designed for feature interpretation rather than feature selection.

title: Evian: Towards Explainable Visual Instruction-tuning Data Auditing
summary: The efficacy of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) is critically dependent on the quality of their training data, requiring a precise balance between visual fidelity and instruction-following capability. Existing datasets, however, are plagued by inconsistent quality, and current data filtering methods rely on coarse-grained scores that lack the granularity to identify nuanced semantic flaws like logical fallacies or factual errors. This creates a fundamental bottleneck in developing more reliable models. To address this, we make three core contributions. First, we construct a large-scale, 300K-sample benchmark by systematically injecting diverse, subtle defects to provide a challenging testbed for data auditing. Second, we introduce a novel "Decomposition-then-Evaluation" paradigm that breaks model responses into constituent cognitive components: visual description, subjective inference, and factual claim, enabling targeted analysis. Third, we instantiate this paradigm via EVIAN (Explainable Visual Instruction-tuning Data AuditiNg), an automated framework that evaluates these components along the orthogonal axes of Image-Text Consistency, Logical Coherence, and Factual Accuracy. Our empirical findings challenge the prevailing scale-centric paradigm: a model fine-tuned on a compact, high-quality subset curated by EVIAN consistently surpassed models trained on orders-of-magnitude larger datasets. We also reveal that dividing complex auditing into verifiable subtasks enables robust curation, and that Logical Coherence is the most critical factor in data quality evaluation.

AI.tldr: EVIAN framework improves LVLM training data quality through decomposition-based auditing of visual-text consistency, logical coherence, and factual accuracy, showing that small high-quality datasets outperform massive low-quality ones.
AI.motivation: Current LVLM training datasets suffer from inconsistent quality, and existing filtering methods use coarse-grained scores that can't detect nuanced semantic flaws like logical fallacies or factual errors, creating a bottleneck for developing reliable models.
